topics.title: Introduction to Semantic Web - Limitations of Current Web, Development of Semantic Web, Emergence of the Social Web
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PECMT528 - CONCEPTS IN SOCIAL NETWORK ANALYSIS > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Introduction to Semantic Web - Limitations of Current Web, Development of Semantic Web, Emergence of the Social Web # Module 1 — Introduction to the Semantic Web & The Social Web ## 1.1 Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1.1 What is the "Current Web"? > [!IMPORTANT] > **Formal Definition (KTU Syllabus Standard):** > The **Current Web** (also called the *Syntactic Web* or *Web 1.0*) is a globally distributed hypertext system built primarily on **HTML (HyperText Markup Language)**, **URLs (Uniform Resource Locators)**, and the **HTTP (HyperText Transfer Protocol)**. It was designed by **Tim Berners-Lee** at **CERN** in **1989** to allow interlinked digital documents to be retrieved and displayed by humans through a web browser. In simpler words, the current web is built for **human eyes, not machine understanding**. A webpage looks beautiful to you, but to a computer, it is just a string of tags and text — it has *no idea* what the content *means*. ### 1.1.2 The Three Generations of the Web — A Quick Analogy Imagine a **library**: - **Web 1.0 (Current/Syntactic Web)** = A library full of *books*, but with **no catalog, no index cards, and no librarian**. You can read a book, but the library itself cannot tell you that "Book A on shelf 3 and Book B on shelf 7 are both about Quantum Physics." - **Web 2.0 (Social Web)** = The same library, but now *readers can write their own book reviews, tag books, and form book clubs*. Content becomes interactive, social, and user-generated. - **Web 3.0 (Semantic Web)** = A library where *every book has a smart catalog card* that not only tells you the title and author, but also understands the meaning, relationships, and concepts inside the book. A computer can then answer: *"Find me all books published after 2010 that criticize Einstein's theory and were written by Indian authors."* > [!NOTE] > **Syllabus Highlight:** The progression **Web 1.0 → Web 2.0 → Web 3.0** is the foundational narrative for Module 1. The *current web* is Web 1.0 (with hints of Web 2.0), the *Social Web* is Web 2.0, and the *Semantic Web* is Web 3.0. ### 1.1.3 Limitations of the Current Web The current web suffers from **five critical limitations** that KTU examiners repeatedly test: 1. **Lack of Semantic Meaning (The Meaning Problem):** HTML describes *how content should look* (font, color, layout), but never *what content means*. The tag `

Apple

` could mean the fruit, the company, or a music label — the computer cannot distinguish. 2. **Information Overload (The Search Problem):** Search engines return millions of links ranked by keyword popularity (e.g., Google's **PageRank** algorithm), not by the user's true intent. A search for *"Java"* returns results about the island, the coffee, and the programming language — all jumbled together. 3. **Poor Personalization (The Relevance Problem):** The web treats every user identically. It cannot adapt content based on a user's location, profession, history, or preferences (early Web 1.0 lacked this; Web 2.0 only partially solved it). 4. **Static & Read-Only Nature (Early Web 1.0):** Early websites were digital brochures — users could only *read* content, not *contribute* to it. There was no commenting, no uploading, no interaction. 5. **Machine-Unreadable (The Automation Problem):** Computers can *display* web pages but cannot *understand* them. Tasks like comparing prices of a laptop across 50 e-commerce sites, booking a multi-leg vacation, or aggregating research papers require human effort. ### 1.1.4 The Semantic Web — Formal Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Formal Definition (Tim Berners-Lee, W3C Standard):** > The **Semantic Web** is an extension of the current web in which information is given **well-defined meaning** (semantics), enabling computers and people to work in cooperation. It is built on standards defined by the **W3C (World Wide Web Consortium)** such as **RDF (Resource Description Framework)**, **OWL (Web Ontology Language)**, and **SPARQL** (a query language for RDF data). The goal of the Semantic Web is to convert the web from a *readable-for-humans* medium into a *understandable-for-machines* medium. ### 1.1.5 The Social Web — Formal Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Formal Definition (KTU Standard):** > The **Social Web** (or **Web 2.0**) refers to the second generation of the World Wide Web, characterized by **user-generated content**, **usability**, **interoperability**, and **collaboration** among users. It is built on technologies like **AJAX (Asynchronous JavaScript and XML)**, **REST APIs**, **RSS feeds**, and platforms such as **Facebook, Twitter, YouTube, Wikipedia, and blogs**. In essence, the Social Web transforms *passive readers* into *active participants*. ### 1.1.6 Real-World Analogy Summary | Web Generation | Library Analogy | Human Equivalent | Technology Base | |---|---|---|---| | **Web 1.0** (Current) | Books with no catalog | A printed encyclopedia | HTML, HTTP, URLs | | **Web 2.0** (Social) | Readers writing reviews & forming clubs | Wikipedia, YouTube | AJAX, APIs, social platforms | | **Web 3.0** (Semantic) | A smart, knowledge-aware librarian | A personal AI assistant | RDF, OWL, SPARQL, Ontologies | ## 1.2 Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 1.2.1 The Architecture of the Current Web (Web 1.0) The current web follows a strict **client–server architecture**: - **Client (User's Browser):** Sends an **HTTP GET** request to a server when a user clicks a link or types a URL. - **Server (Web Host):** Receives the request, fetches the requested file (HTML, CSS, images), and returns it with an HTTP status code (e.g., **200 OK**, **404 Not Found**). - **Rendering:** The browser parses the HTML and renders it visually. The server is *unaware* of what the page *means* — it only stores and forwards files. **Key Characteristics of Web 1.0:** - Static HTML pages - One-way communication (server → client) - No user authentication or personalization - Content owned by a small number of publishers - Directory-based navigation (e.g., Yahoo! Directory, 1995–2001) ### 1.2.2 Why the Current Web Fails — A Logical Breakdown | # | Limitation | Root Cause | Engineering Consequence | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | No semantics | HTML lacks a knowledge representation layer | Search engines rely on keyword matching, not meaning | | 2 | Heterogeneity | Data exists in countless incompatible formats (CSV, XML, JSON, PDF) | Integration between systems is costly and error-prone | | 3 | Ambiguity | Natural language is polysemous (e.g., "bank" = river bank or financial bank) | Automated reasoning is impossible without disambiguation | | 4 | Scalability of trust | No built-in mechanism to verify the reliability of online data | Users cannot trust raw web data for critical decisions | | 5 | Lack of context | Pages do not declare their audience, purpose, or domain | Search results are often irrelevant to the user's actual goal | ### 1.2.3 The Development of the Semantic Web Tim Berners-Lee first articulated the Semantic Web vision in his landmark **1998 roadmap** and his **2001 Scientific American article** (co-authored with James Hendler and Ora Lassila, titled *"The Semantic Web"*). The vision was then formalized by the **W3C** through a layered architecture known as the **"Semantic Web Stack"** or **"Layer Cake."** The Semantic Web Stack (from bottom to top): 1. **URI (Uniform Resource Identifier):** The foundational identification layer — every entity (person, place, concept) gets a unique address. 2. **Unicode + XML:** Character encoding and a structured document format that any machine can parse. 3. **RDF (Resource Description Framework):** A standard model for representing data as **Subject → Predicate → Object** triples. This is the *data interchange* layer. 4. **RDF Schema (RDFS):** Adds basic vocabulary to RDF — classes, properties, and inheritance. 5. **Ontology (OWL):** A formal language to define complex relationships, constraints, and rules between concepts. Built on *Description Logic*. 6. **Logic + Proof:** The reasoning layer — machines can infer new facts from existing data using formal logic rules. 7. **Trust:** The topmost layer — cryptographic signatures and digital certificates (e.g., **PKI — Public Key Infrastructure**) ensure the *provenance* and *trustworthiness* of web data. > [!NOTE] > **KTU High-Yield Fact:** The Semantic Web is *not* a separate network. It is an *extension* of the existing web. The current web remains the transport layer; the Semantic Web adds *meaning* on top of it. ### 1.2.4 The Emergence of the Social Web (Web 2.0) The term **"Web 2.0"** was popularized by **Tim O'Reilly** in **2004** (though the underlying technologies emerged around 2002–2005). The Social Web is characterized by **seven core principles** (O'Reilly's framework): 1. **The Web as Platform:** Software runs *in the browser*, not just on the desktop (e.g., Google Docs vs. Microsoft Office). 2. **Harnessing Collective Intelligence:** User contributions make the system smarter (e.g., Wikipedia, Stack Overflow, Reddit up-votes). 3. **Data is the Next Intel Inside:** Applications accumulate and leverage user data (e.g., Facebook's social graph, Netflix's recommendation engine). 4. **End of the Software Release Cycle:** Continuous deployment — software is updated *daily* (e.g., Facebook pushes code multiple times per day). 5. **Lightweight Programming Models:** Use of simple, scalable protocols (e.g., **REST**, **JSON**, **RSS**). 6. **Software Above the Level of a Single Device:** Cross-device experiences (mobile + desktop + tablet). 7. **Rich User Experiences:** AJAX-driven, desktop-like interfaces in the browser (e.g., Gmail, Google Maps). ### 1.2.5 KTU Formula / Concept Cheat Sheet | Concept | Symbolic / Standard Form | Boundary / Domain | Real-World Utility | |---|---|---|---| | **RDF Triple** | $(s, p, o)$ — Subject, Predicate, Object | All three must be URIs or literal values | Foundation of all Semantic Web data exchange | | **RDF Graph** | $G = (V, E)$ where $V$ = nodes, $E$ = edges | Directed, labeled multigraph | Visualizes relationships between web entities | | **Degree Centrality** (in social graph) | $C_D(v) = \deg(v) / (n - 1)$ | $0 \le C_D(v) \le 1$ | Measures a user's popularity in a social network | | **PageRank (Web 1.0 search)** | $PR(p) = (1-d) + d \cdot \sum_{q \to p} \frac{PR(q)}{L(q)}$ | Damping factor $d \approx 0.85$ | Google's original web page ranking algorithm | | **Semantic Web Stack Layers** | 7 layers (URI → Trust) | Each layer depends on the layer below | Engineering blueprint for Web 3.0 applications | | **Web 2.0 Seven Principles** | O'Reilly, 2004 | Applies to all participatory web platforms | Design checklist for social applications | | **HTTP Status Code** | 1xx Informational, 2xx Success, 3xx Redirection, 4xx Client Error, 5xx Server Error | Standardized by IETF RFC 7231 | Diagnostic backbone of the client–server web | > [!NOTE] > **Engineering Insight:** The current web is essentially a **graph of documents** (pages linked by hyperlinks), while the Semantic Web is a **graph of data** (entities linked by typed relationships). The Social Web is a **graph of people** (users linked by friendships, follows, likes). The KTU course **PECMT528** focuses on analyzing this last graph — the *social network graph*. ## 1.3 Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 1.3.1 From a Web Page to an RDF Triple — A Worked Derivation Consider the following plain English sentence: > *"Tim Berners-Lee invented the World Wide Web at CERN in 1989."* **Step 1 — Identify the Subject, Predicate, and Object.** The **Subject** is the entity the statement is *about* → **Tim Berners-Lee**. The **Predicate** is the *relationship* or *property* → **invented**. The **Object** is the *value* of that property → **World Wide Web**. **Step 2 — Express as a triple.** $$ (s, p, o) = (\text{Tim Berners-Lee},\ \text{invented},\ \text{World Wide Web}) $$ **Step 3 — Convert each component to a URI for machine readability.** A URI is a unique string that globally identifies a resource. For example: $$ s = \text{uri} \rightarrow \texttt{http://dbpedia.org/resource/Tim\_Berners-Lee} $$ $$ p = \text{uri} \rightarrow \texttt{http://dbpedia.org/ontology/inventor} $$ $$ o = \text{uri} \rightarrow \texttt{http://dbpedia.org/resource/World\_Wide\_Web} $$ **Step 4 — Represent in RDF/XML or RDF/Turtle syntax (machine format).** In **Turtle** notation (a human-readable RDF format), the triple becomes: ```turtle . ``` **Step 5 — Aggregate multiple triples into an RDF Graph.** A real-world knowledge base contains *billions* of such triples. For example, the same person has many more facts: $$ (\text{Tim\_Berners-Lee},\ \text{bornIn},\ \text{London}) $$ $$ (\text{Tim\_Berners-Lee},\ \text{graduatedFrom},\ \text{Oxford\_University}) $$ $$ (\text{Tim\_Berners-Lee},\ \text{employer},\ \text{W3C}) $$ All of these triples together form a **directed labeled multigraph** $G = (V, E)$ where: - $V$ = the set of all subjects and objects (the *entities*). - $E$ = the set of all predicates (the *labeled edges*). > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Logic Callout:** The Semantic Web essentially converts unstructured English sentences into a *formal, queryable, machine-readable graph*. This is the foundational step that enables all downstream applications — search engines, personal assistants, knowledge panels in Google, and AI reasoning. ### 1.3.2 Derivation of Degree Centrality in a Social Network Graph A **social network** is mathematically a graph $G = (V, E)$ where $V$ is the set of users (nodes) and $E$ is the set of relationships (edges). **Step 1 — Define the degree of a node.** The **degree** of a node $v$, denoted $\deg(v)$, is the number of edges incident to $v$: $$ \deg(v) = \sum_{u \in V} \mathbf{1}_{(u, v) \in E} $$ where $\mathbf{1}_{(\cdot)}$ is the indicator function (equals 1 if the edge exists, 0 otherwise). **Step 2 — Normalize to obtain centrality.** To make the centrality score lie in the interval $[0, 1]$, divide by the maximum possible degree, which is $(n - 1)$ for a graph of $n$ nodes: $$ C_D(v) = \frac{\deg(v)}{n - 1} $$ **Step 3 — Worked numerical example.** Suppose a small network has $n = 6$ users: $V = \{A, B, C, D, E, F\}$. The edge list is: $E = \{(A,B), (A,C), (A,D), (B,C), (C,E), (D,F)\}$. Compute the degree of each node: $$ \deg(A) = 3 \quad (\text{connected to } B, C, D) $$ $$ \deg(B) = 2 \quad (\text{connected to } A, C) $$ $$ \deg(C) = 3 \quad (\text{connected to } A, B, E) $$ $$ \deg(D) = 2 \quad (\text{connected to } A, F) $$ $$ \deg(E) = 1 \quad (\text{connected to } C) $$ $$ \deg(F) = 1 \quad (\text{connected to } D) $$ Now compute the normalized degree centrality: $$ C_D(A) = \frac{3}{6 - 1} = \frac{3}{5} = 0.60 $$ $$ C_D(B) = \frac{2}{5} = 0.40 $$ $$ C_D(C) = \frac{3}{5} = 0.60 $$ $$ C_D(D) = \frac{2}{5} = 0.40 $$ $$ C_D(E) = \frac{1}{5} = 0.20 $$ $$ C_D(F) = \frac{1}{5} = 0.20 $$ > [!NOTE] > **Engineering Interpretation:** Users $A$ and $C$ are the most *central* (most-connected) individuals in this small social network. In a real platform like Twitter, a high $C_D$ corresponds to a *celebrity* or *influencer*. This metric is heavily used in **Social Network Analysis (SNA)**, the focus of the entire PECMT528 course. ### 1.3.3 Analytical Matrix — Comparing the Three Web Generations | Dimension | Web 1.0 (Current/Syntactic) | Web 2.0 (Social) | Web 3.0 (Semantic) | |---|---|---|---| | **Primary Technology** | HTML, HTTP, URLs | AJAX, REST APIs, JavaScript | RDF, OWL, SPARQL, Ontologies | | **Content Source** | Webmaster / Publisher | User-generated (crowdsourced) | Machine-generated from structured data | | **User Role** | Passive reader | Active contributor | Knowledge co-creator + consumer | | **Data Model** | Unstructured HTML pages | Semi-structured (tags, posts, comments) | Fully structured (RDF triples, ontologies) | | **Search Style** | Keyword-based (Google PageRank) | Social signals (likes, shares) | Semantic (intent + meaning) | | **Interoperability** | Low (siloed websites) | Medium (APIs, but still silos) | High (linked open data cloud) | | **Examples (Year)** | Yahoo Directory (1996), Britannica Online (1995) | Wikipedia (2001), Facebook (2004), YouTube (2005) | Google Knowledge Graph (2012), DBpedia, Wikidata | | **Founders / Architects** | Tim Berners-Lee (CERN, 1989) | Tim O'Reilly (term coined 2004) | Tim Berners-Lee + W3C (1998–2001) | | **Trust Mechanism** | None (any site can claim anything) | Reputation systems, peer review | Cryptographic signatures, provenance chains | | **AI-Friendliness** | Very low (requires NLP to parse) | Medium (APIs expose structured data) | Very high (data is born structured) | ## 1.4 Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 1.4.1 The Semantic Web Stack (Layer Cake) — Block Architecture This block-level diagram shows the seven-layer architecture of the Semantic Web. **Each layer builds on the layer immediately below it**, which is the most important architectural fact about the Semantic Web. ```mermaid flowchart BT L7["Layer 7: Trust and Digital Signatures"]:::top L6["Layer 6: Logic and Proof Engine"]:::logic L5["Layer 5: Ontology Vocabulary OWL"]:::ont L4["Layer 4: RDF Schema RDFS"]:::rdfs L3["Layer 3: RDF Data Model Triples"]:::rdf L2["Layer 2: XML and Unicode Syntax"]:::xml L1["Layer 1: URI Identification"]:::uri L1 --> L2 L2 --> L3 L3 --> L4 L4 --> L5 L5 --> L6 L6 --> L7 classDef top fill:#ffd166,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef logic fill:#ef476f,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff classDef ont fill:#06d6a0,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef rdfs fill:#118ab2,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff classDef rdf fill:#073b4c,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff classDef xml fill:#8338ec,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff classDef uri fill:#3a86ff,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff ``` > [!NOTE] > **Reading the diagram:** Start at the bottom (URI is the foundation, every resource must have a unique identifier). Move upward; each layer adds a new capability. Trust sits at the top because *without trust, semantic data is useless* — anyone could publish false triples. ### 1.4.2 Evolution of the Web — From Web 1.0 to Web 3.0 This diagram traces the historical evolution of the web and highlights the paradigm shift at each stage. ```mermaid flowchart LR W1["Web 1.0 1989 to 2003\nSyntactic Web\nRead Only\nHTML and HTTP"]:::w1 W2["Web 2.0 2004 to 2014\nSocial Web\nRead and Write\nAJAX and APIs"]:::w2 W3["Web 3.0 2014 to Present\nSemantic Web\nRead Write Execute\nRDF OWL SPARQL"]:::w3 W1 -- "User generated\ncontent emerges" --> W2 W2 -- "Need for meaning\nand reasoning" --> W3 classDef w1 fill:#ffadad,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef w2 fill:#ffd6a5,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef w3 fill:#caffbf,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 ``` > [!NOTE] > **KTU Insight:** The boundaries between these three generations are not strict. Today's web (mid-2020s) is a *blend* — many sites use semantic markup (Schema.org) while still being primarily Web 2.0 social platforms. The transition is gradual, not abrupt. ### 1.4.3 From Current Web to Social Web — Architectural Shift This block diagram contrasts the request-response model of the current web with the participatory model of the social web. ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph CurrentWeb["Web 1.0 Current Web Model"] direction TB U1["User Browser\nRead Only"]:::user1 S1["Web Server\nStatic HTML Files"]:::server1 U1 -- "HTTP GET Request" --> S1 S1 -- "Static HTML Page" --> U1 end subgraph SocialWeb["Web 2.0 Social Web Model"] direction TB U2a["User A\nReader and Contributor"]:::user2 U2b["User B\nReader and Contributor"]:::user2 P["Central Platform\nFacebook YouTube Wikipedia\nStores User Data"]:::platform DB["Shared Database\nUser Posts Likes Comments"]:::db U2a -- "Read content\nWrite posts\nLike comment" --> P U2b -- "Read content\nWrite posts\nLike comment" --> P P -- "CRUD Operations" --> DB end classDef user1 fill:#a0c4ff,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef server1 fill:#bdb2ff,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef user2 fill:#ffafcc,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef platform fill:#ffc8dd,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef db fill:#ffd6a5,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 ``` > [!NOTE] > **Engineering Takeaway:** In Web 1.0, the *server* is the source of truth. In Web 2.0, the *users collectively* are the source of truth. This is a fundamental architectural inversion, and it is what enables the field of **Social Network Analysis** — the data for our entire PECMT528 course is generated by this Web 2.0 paradigm. ### 1.4.4 RDF Triple Graph — A Visual Schematic This Mermaid graph visualizes how four RDF triples combine into a tiny knowledge graph about a person. ```mermaid graph LR TBL["Tim Berners Lee\nURI Person"]:::person WWW["World Wide Web\nURI Invention"]:::thing CERN["CERN\nURI Organization"]:::org LONDON["London\nURI Place"]:::place W3C["W3C\nURI Organization"]:::org TBL -- "invented" --> WWW TBL -- "bornIn" --> LONDON TBL -- "worksAt" --> CERN TBL -- "founded" --> W3C classDef person fill:#06d6a0,stroke:#000,stroke-width:3px,color:#000 classDef thing fill:#ffd166,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef org fill:#118ab2,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff classDef place fill:#ef476f,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff ``` > [!NOTE] > **Reading the diagram:** Each arrow is a **predicate** (a labeled edge), and each node is a **subject or object** of a triple. This is exactly how a knowledge graph is built — one triple at a time. Google Knowledge Graph, DBpedia, and Wikidata are all massive collections of such triples. ## 1.5 KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 1.5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) > **Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]** > **List any three major limitations of the current web.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** The three major limitations of the current web are: 1. **Lack of Semantic Meaning:** HTML is designed for *presentation*, not *representation of knowledge*. A computer cannot distinguish between the word "Apple" referring to the fruit, the company, or the music label. 2. **Information Overload:** The current web contains billions of pages, but search engines rely on keyword matching. Users often drown in irrelevant results rather than finding precise answers. 3. **Machine Unreadability:** The content is optimized for human eyes via browsers. Automated agents (software bots) cannot reliably *understand* or *reason* over the data, preventing large-scale intelligent automation. > **Q2. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]** > **What is meant by the term "Social Web"? Name two characteristic features of Web 2.0.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** The **Social Web** (Web 2.0) is the second generation of the World Wide Web that emphasizes **user-generated content**, **interoperability**, and **collaboration** among users. It transforms passive readers into active contributors. Two characteristic features of Web 2.0 are: 1. **User-Generated Content:** Platforms such as Wikipedia, YouTube, and Facebook allow users to create, share, and comment on content, making the web a participatory medium. 2. **Rich, Interactive User Interfaces:** Built using technologies like **AJAX (Asynchronous JavaScript and XML)** and **REST APIs**, modern Web 2.0 applications provide desktop-like responsiveness inside the browser (e.g., Gmail, Google Maps). ### 1.5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) — KTU ESE Internal Choice Pattern > **Note on KTU Pattern (2024 Scheme):** Each Part B question carries **14 marks** and offers an **internal choice** (either OR). The standard split is **(a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks**. The model answers below follow the exact KTU valuation key. --- #### **Question A (14 Marks) — Option 1** > **[KTU University Exam — Model Question, 2024 Scheme]** > **(a)** Explain in detail the major limitations of the current (syntactic) web. >     **(b)** Describe the layered architecture of the Semantic Web stack. *(7 + 7 = 14 marks)* **Model Solution:** **(a) Limitations of the Current Web — 7 marks** The current web, designed in 1989 by Tim Berners-Lee, was built for human-to-human information sharing via HTML pages. Over time, several critical limitations have emerged: 1. **Lack of Semantics (1.5 marks):** HTML describes only *how* to display content, never *what* it means. The same tag `

Java

` could refer to an island, a programming language, or coffee. Computers cannot resolve this ambiguity. 2. **Information Overload (1.5 marks):** With billions of pages, finding *relevant* information is extremely hard. Search engines like Google use keyword frequency and link-based ranking (PageRank), but they do not understand user intent. A query like *"best laptop for machine learning under 60,000 rupees"* often returns poorly filtered results. 3. **Poor Personalization and Context (1 mark):** The early web treated all users identically. It could not adapt content based on a user's location (e.g., India vs. USA), profession, or browsing history. 4. **Static Nature (Web 1.0 era) (1 mark):** In the 1990s, websites were digital brochures. Users could only *read*; they could not contribute content, comment, or interact. 5. **Machine Unreadability (2 marks):** Machines can *display* pages but cannot *understand* them. Complex tasks — such as aggregating product prices from 50 e-commerce sites, planning a multi-leg international trip, or extracting medical evidence from research papers — require manual human effort. This is the central motivation for the Semantic Web. **(b) Layered Architecture of the Semantic Web — 7 marks** The Semantic Web architecture, often called the **"Semantic Web Stack"** or **"Layer Cake,"** was proposed by Tim Berners-Lee in his 2001 article. It has **seven layers**, each depending on the one below: | Layer | Name | Function | |---|---|---| | 1 | **URI** | Globally unique identifiers for every resource (entity, concept, document). | | 2 | **Unicode + XML** | Universal character encoding and a machine-parseable structured document syntax. | | 3 | **RDF (Resource Description Framework)** | A standard data model that represents information as **(Subject, Predicate, Object)** triples. | | 4 | **RDF Schema (RDFS)** | Adds basic vocabulary: classes, properties, and subclass relationships. | | 5 | **Ontology (OWL)** | Formal language (based on Description Logic) to define complex constraints, equivalences, and rich relationships between concepts. | | 6 | **Logic + Proof** | Reasoning layer; machines can derive new facts from existing ones using inference rules. | | 7 | **Trust** | Topmost layer; ensures *provenance* and *reliability* of data using digital signatures, PKI, and reputation systems. | The hierarchy ensures that as we move upward, the web becomes *more intelligent* and *more trustworthy*. Without lower layers (e.g., URI or RDF), higher layers (e.g., OWL or Logic) cannot function. > **Diagram Reference:** See the Mermaid block diagram in **Section 1.4.1** for the full visual. --- #### **Question B (14 Marks) — Option 2 (Internal Choice Alternative)** > **[KTU University Exam — Model Question, 2024 Scheme]** > **(a)** Discuss the emergence of the Social Web. Explain the seven principles of Web 2.0 as defined by Tim O'Reilly. >     **(b)** Compare and contrast the Current Web, the Social Web, and the Semantic Web across at least five dimensions. *(7 + 7 = 14 marks)* **Model Solution:** **(a) Emergence of the Social Web & O'Reilly's Seven Principles — 7 marks** The **Social Web (Web 2.0)** emerged around 2003–2005 as a result of three converging forces: - The rise of **broadband internet** access in homes. - The maturation of **browser scripting** (JavaScript, AJAX, CSS). - The willingness of users to *create and share* content online. In **2004**, **Tim O'Reilly** coined the term *Web 2.0* and articulated **seven guiding principles**: 1. **The Web as Platform** (1 mark): Software that previously required installation (e.g., word processors) now runs in the browser as a service (e.g., Google Docs). 2. **Harnessing Collective Intelligence** (1 mark): The more users a system has, the smarter it becomes (e.g., Wikipedia grows more accurate as more people edit; Google's ranking improves as more people link). 3. **Data is the Next "Intel Inside"** (1 mark): Competitive advantage comes from owning and exploiting large, unique datasets (e.g., Facebook's social graph, Amazon's purchase history). 4. **End of the Software Release Cycle** (1 mark): Continuous deployment — features are updated weekly or daily rather than in versioned releases (e.g., Facebook ships code multiple times per day). 5. **Lightweight Programming Models** (1 mark): Use of simple, scalable, open protocols like **REST**, **JSON**, and **RSS** rather than heavyweight, proprietary APIs. 6. **Software Above the Level of a Single Device** (1 mark): Applications work seamlessly across multiple devices (mobile, tablet, desktop) and operating systems. 7. **Rich User Experiences** (1 mark): AJAX and modern JavaScript frameworks deliver responsive, desktop-like interfaces within the browser (e.g., Gmail's instant search, Google Maps' draggable maps). **(b) Comparison of the Three Web Generations — 7 marks** | Dimension | Web 1.0 (Current) | Web 2.0 (Social) | Web 3.0 (Semantic) | |---|---|---|---| | **Era** | 1989 – 2003 | 2004 – 2014 | 2014 – Present | | **Content Source** | Publishers / Webmasters | User-generated (crowdsourced) | Structured data + ontologies | | **User Role** | Passive reader | Active contributor | Knowledge co-creator | | **Data Format** | Unstructured HTML | Semi-structured (tags, posts) | Fully structured (RDF, OWL) | | **Search Style** | Keyword / PageRank | Social signals (likes, shares) | Semantic (intent + meaning) | | **Key Tech** | HTML, HTTP | AJAX, REST, JavaScript | RDF, OWL, SPARQL | | **Examples** | Yahoo Directory, Britannica Online | Facebook, YouTube, Wikipedia | Google Knowledge Graph, DBpedia, Wikidata | Each row in the table is worth approximately **1 mark**; students should pick **five or more** dimensions and elaborate with at least one example per generation to score full marks. --- ### 1.5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU 2024 Exams:** > > 1. **Conflating Web 2.0 and Web 3.0:** Many students mistakenly describe Web 2.0 as the *Semantic Web*. Remember: **Web 2.0 = Social Web (user-generated content, AJAX, social platforms)**. **Web 3.0 = Semantic Web (RDF, OWL, ontologies, machine reasoning)**. Mixing these up is an immediate 2–3 mark deduction. > > 2. **Writing only "HTML cannot represent meaning" without examples:** KTU examiners reward *specific* answers. Always give an example — e.g., *"The word 'Java' can mean a programming language, an island, or coffee; HTML cannot distinguish these."* A bare statement loses half the marks allotted. > > 3. **Listing the Semantic Web layers out of order:** The 7-layer stack has a *strict bottom-up dependency*. If you write "Trust at the bottom and URI at the top," you will lose 2 marks. Memorize the order: **URI → Unicode/XML → RDF → RDFS → OWL → Logic → Trust**. > > 4. **Forgetting the year of Tim Berners-Lee's Semantic Web vision (1998/2001):** Examiners often award 1 mark for a precise historical fact. Know that the vision was first articulated in **1998** and popularized in the **2001 Scientific American article** (Berners-Lee, Hendler, Lassila). > > 5. **For Part B, not dividing the answer into sub-parts (a) and (b):** KTU valuation strictly checks sub-part coverage. A 14-mark question with no sub-parts explicitly addressed may receive a flat 1–2 mark penalty for poor structure. > > 6. **Writing vague answers like "Web 2.0 is better than Web 1.0":** Examiners want *technical contrast*, not value judgments. Always compare on **specific dimensions** — data model, search style, user role, technology base. --- ### 1.5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist) > **Final High-Density Revision Notes — Memorize Before Exam:** - ☐ The **Current Web** is the **Syntactic Web / Web 1.0**, built on **HTML, HTTP, URLs** by **Tim Berners-Lee** in **1989 at CERN**. - ☐ The **Social Web** is **Web 2.0**, coined by **Tim O'Reilly** in **2004**; its hallmark is **user-generated content**. - ☐ The **Semantic Web** is **Web 3.0**, proposed by **Tim Berners-Lee (1998)**, popularized in the **2001 Scientific American article**. - ☐ The Semantic Web is *not* a replacement — it is an **extension** of the current web that adds *meaning* to data. - ☐ The fundamental limitation of the current web is the **lack of machine-understandable semantics**. - ☐ The Semantic Web's data model is **RDF**, which expresses information as **(Subject, Predicate, Object)** triples. - ☐ The Semantic Web Stack has **7 layers**: **URI → Unicode/XML → RDF → RDFS → OWL → Logic → Trust**. - ☐ **OWL (Web Ontology Language)** is used to build **ontologies** — formal models of a domain's concepts and relations. - ☐ **SPARQL** is the query language for RDF data (analogous to SQL for relational databases). - ☐ **Web 2.0's seven principles** (O'Reilly): Web-as-Platform, Collective Intelligence, Data-is-Intel, Continuous Release, Lightweight Models, Cross-Device, Rich UX. - ☐ **AJAX (Asynchronous JavaScript and XML)** is the key enabling technology of Web 2.0. - ☐ **Degree Centrality** in a social network: $C_D(v) = \deg(v) / (n - 1)$, with $0 \le C_D(v) \le 1$. - ☐ **PageRank** is the original Google algorithm for ranking web pages, based on link structure (Web 1.0 era). - ☐ The current web is a **graph of documents**; the Semantic Web is a **graph of data**; the Social Web is a **graph of people**. - ☐ The course **PECMT528** focuses on analyzing the *Social Web's* graph — this is the bridge to all later modules. - ☐ The Semantic Web relies on **W3C** (World Wide Web Consortium) standards — know this for short-answer questions. - ☐ **Linked Open Data (LOD) cloud** is the largest practical deployment of Semantic Web principles (DBpedia, Wikidata, etc.). - ☐ For 14-mark answers, always use **headings, sub-parts (a)/(b), and a comparison table** for full marks.

topics.title: Social Network Analysis - Development of Social Network Analysis, Key Concepts and Measures in Social Network Analysis
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECMT528 - CONCEPTS IN SOCIAL NETWORK ANALYSIS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Social Network Analysis - Development of Social Network Analysis, Key Concepts and Measures in Social Network Analysis

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition of Social Network Analysis (SNA)

**Social Network Analysis (SNA)** is the disciplined, methodical study of social structures through the use of **graph theory**, **mathematical matrices**, and **statistical models**. It treats actors (individuals, organizations, or entities) as **nodes** and their relationships (friendship, communication, collaboration, trade) as **edges**, then quantifies the structural properties of the resulting network to reveal patterns of interaction, influence, centrality, cohesion, and community formation.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** SNA is the mapping and measuring of relationships and flows between people, groups, organizations, computers, URLs, and other connected information/knowledge entities. The strength of SNA lies in its ability to translate a complex social web into a tractable **mathematical object** — a graph $G(V, E)$.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a college classroom. Instead of looking at students as isolated individuals, SNA asks:
- *Who is friends with whom?* (edges)
- *Who is the "connector" linking different friend groups?* (broker / high betweenness)
- *Who is the "star" that everyone knows?* (high degree centrality)
- *Who is the "gossip queen" who hears news fastest?* (high closeness centrality)

**Real-World Analogy — The Airport Map:**
Think of the world airline network. Each airport is a node, each direct flight is an edge. Even if a tiny airport (e.g., Doha) is geographically small, it can be a "super-hub" because *who you are connected to* matters more than *your own size*. SNA quantifies exactly this: **position in the network often outweighs intrinsic attributes.**

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight for KTU Exams:** SNA does **not** focus on the *attributes* of individuals (their age, income, IQ). It focuses on the **structural position** and **relational patterns** that connect them. This is the *relational worldview* — the foundational paradigm shift introduced by SNA.

### 1.3 Standard Metrics in SNA (Highlighted for Quick Recall)

The following bolded metrics form the canonical vocabulary you MUST memorize for the KTU board exam:

- **Nodes / Vertices / Actors** — the entities
- **Edges / Ties / Links** — the relationships
- **Degree** — number of direct connections
- **Centrality** — importance of a node
- **Density** — fraction of all possible ties that exist
- **Diameter** — longest shortest path in the network
- **Clustering Coefficient** — how interconnected a node's neighbors are
- **Community** — a dense subgroup

> [!TIP]
> **Exam Tip:** Examiners love the contrast: *“SNA studies relationships, not attributes.”* Memorize this sentence verbatim for 3-mark questions.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Historical Development of Social Network Analysis (Timeline)

The development of SNA is best understood in **five distinct phases**:

#### Phase 1 — Pre-Formal Era (1930s)
- **Jacob Moreno (1934)** introduced the **sociogram** to map friendships in schoolchildren and workers in a factory. This is widely accepted as the **birth of SNA**.
- He coined the term *"sociometry"* — measurement of social relationships.

#### Phase 2 — Mathematical Foundations (1950s–1960s)
- **MIT's Group Networks Laboratory** (Bavelas, Leavitt) experimentally proved that the *position* of a person in a network (central vs. peripheral) affects their *performance* and *influence* — independent of individual ability.
- **Katz & Powell (1953)**, **Cartwright & Harary (1956)** introduced matrix representations of networks.

#### Phase 3 — Classical Sociological Theory (1970s–1980s)
- **Mark Granovetter (1973)** — *The Strength of Weak Ties*: A landmark paper arguing that **weak ties (acquaintances) are more valuable** for information diffusion and job-finding than strong ties (close friends).
- **Ronald Burt (1973)** introduced **structural holes** — the strategic advantage a node gains by being the only bridge between two otherwise disconnected clusters.
- **Stanley Milgram (1967)** — the famous *Six Degrees of Separation* small-world experiment.

#### Phase 4 — Computational Era (1990s–2000s)
- The rise of the **World Wide Web**, **email networks**, and **online communities** (SixDegrees.com, Friendster) shifted SNA from small hand-drawn sociograms to **massive, computable networks**.
- **Watts & Strogatz (1998)** mathematically modeled the **small-world phenomenon**.
- **Barabási & Albert (1999)** discovered the **scale-free** property of real networks — degree distribution follows a power law.

#### Phase 5 — Modern Big-Data Era (2010s–Present)
- Twitter, Facebook, LinkedIn, and Instagram generate billions of edges daily.
- SNA is now applied in **epidemiology (disease spread), criminology (terrorist cells), marketing (viral influence), bioinformatics (protein interaction), and recommendation systems.**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield:** The 5 names you MUST remember for the board exam are: **Moreno, Granovetter, Milgram, Watts & Strogatz, Barabási & Albert.** Each represents a milestone.

### 2.2 Key Concepts in SNA — The Conceptual Vocabulary

#### A. Graph-Theoretic Foundations
A social network is formally a **graph** $G = (V, E)$ where:
- $V$ = finite set of nodes (actors), with $\vert V \vert = n$
- $E \subseteq V \times V$ = set of edges (ties)

#### B. Types of Networks

| Type | Description | Real Example |
|---|---|---|
| **Undirected** | Ties have no direction (mutual) | Friendship |
| **Directed (Digraph)** | Ties are one-way | Twitter follow, hyperlinks |
| **Weighted** | Edges carry a numerical strength | Frequency of calls |
| **Unweighted** | All edges treated equally | Just "friends" yes/no |
| **Bipartite** | Two disjoint node sets, edges only across | Actors $\leftrightarrow$ Movies |
| **Multigraph** | Multiple edges between same pair | Repeated transactions |
| **Self-loop** | Edge from a node to itself | Self-mention in a post |
| **Simple Graph** | No self-loops, no multiple edges | Most abstract models |

#### C. Connectivity Concepts
- **Walk** — any sequence of nodes and edges
- **Path** — walk with no repeated nodes
- **Geodesic / Shortest Path** — the path of minimum length between two nodes
- **Connected Component** — maximal subgraph where every pair of nodes has a path between them
- **Diameter** $\Delta$ — the maximum geodesic distance between any pair of nodes in the network

#### D. Structural Properties
- **Density** — how "full" the network is
- **Reciprocity** — in directed networks, fraction of mutual ties
- **Transitivity** — if A→B and B→C, does A→C hold?
- **Homophily** — tendency of similar actors to connect ("birds of a feather")
- **Heterophily** — tendency to connect with the *dissimilar*

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **CRITICAL FORMATTING RULE:** No vertical pipe `|` inside table cells. We use `\vert` for absolute value/cardinality to avoid breaking markdown table syntax.

| # | Concept / Measure | Formula | Where Applied | Range / Unit |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Degree (undirected)** | $k_i = \sum_{j} a_{ij}$ | Number of direct ties of node $i$ | $0 \le k_i \le n-1$ |
| 2 | **In-degree** | $k_i^{in} = \sum_{j} a_{ji}$ | Incoming ties (popularity) | $0 \le k_i^{in} \le n-1$ |
| 3 | **Out-degree** | $k_i^{out} = \sum_{j} a_{ij}$ | Outgoing ties (sociability) | $0 \le k_i^{out} \le n-1$ |
| 4 | **Average Degree** | $\langle k \rangle = \dfrac{1}{n}\sum_{i} k_i$ | Network-level connectivity | Real number |
| 5 | **Degree Centrality (normalized)** | $C_D(i) = \dfrac{k_i}{n-1}$ | Relative importance by direct ties | $[0, 1]$ |
| 6 | **Closeness Centrality** | $C_C(i) = \dfrac{n-1}{\sum_{j \ne i} d(i,j)}$ | Speed of information spread | $[0, 1]$ |
| 7 | **Betweenness Centrality** | $C_B(i) = \sum_{s \ne i \ne t} \dfrac{\sigma_{st}(i)}{\sigma_{st}}$ | Brokerage / control | $\ge 0$ |
| 8 | **Eigenvector Centrality** | $A \mathbf{x} = \lambda \mathbf{x}$ | Importance via important neighbors | $\ge 0$ |
| 9 | **PageRank** | $PR(i) = \dfrac{1-d}{n} + d \sum_{j \to i} \dfrac{PR(j)}{k_j^{out}}$ | Web / directed networks | $[0, 1]$ |
| 10 | **Network Density** | $D = \dfrac{2 \vert E \vert}{n(n-1)}$ | Undirected: fraction of possible ties present | $[0, 1]$ |
| 11 | **Network Density (directed)** | $D = \dfrac{\vert E \vert}{n(n-1)}$ | Directed case | $[0, 1]$ |
| 12 | **Clustering Coefficient (local)** | $C_i = \dfrac{2 \cdot (\text{edges among } N_i)}{k_i(k_i-1)}$ | Local cohesion | $[0, 1]$ |
| 13 | **Global Clustering Coefficient** | $C = \dfrac{3 \times \text{triangles}}{\text{connected triples}}$ | Overall transitivity | $[0, 1]$ |
| 14 | **Average Path Length** | $\ell = \dfrac{1}{n(n-1)} \sum_{i \ne j} d(i,j)$ | Small-worldness | Real, in hops |
| 15 | **Diameter** | $\Delta = \max_{i,j} d(i,j)$ | Largest geodesic | Real, in hops |
| 16 | **Degree Distribution** | $P(k) = \dfrac{N_k}{n}$ | Probability a random node has degree $k$ | $[0, 1]$ |
| 17 | **Power Law Degree Dist.** | $P(k) \sim k^{-\gamma}$ | Scale-free signature | $\gamma \in [2, 3]$ typical |
| 18 | **Reciprocity (directed)** | $r = \dfrac{\vert \{(i,j) : (i,j) \in E, (j,i) \in E\} \vert}{\vert E \vert}$ | Mutual ties fraction | $[0, 1]$ |

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility of SNA

- **Epidemiology:** Identifying "super-spreaders" (high betweenness) for targeted vaccination.
- **Cybersecurity:** Detecting bot-net command-and-control nodes (high eigenvector centrality).
- **Recommender Systems:** PageRank-based friend and content suggestions.
- **Organizational Design:** Revealing communication bottlenecks in companies.
- **Criminology:** Mapping covert terror networks to find *bridge nodes* for dismantling.
- **Bioinformatics:** Protein–protein interaction networks to identify essential proteins.

> [!TIP]
> **Board Exam Hack:** Always cite **one engineering application** when defining a measure. Examiners reward applied thinking generously.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Network Density

**Goal:** Derive the formula for density of an undirected simple graph.

**Step 1 — Maximum Possible Edges:**
In an undirected simple graph with $n$ nodes, each node can connect to at most $(n-1)$ other nodes. So the total ordered pairs of distinct nodes is:

$$M = \binom{n}{2} = \dfrac{n(n-1)}{2}$$

This is the number of *combinations* of 2 nodes, which is also the maximum number of undirected edges that can exist without self-loops or multi-edges.

**Step 2 — Definition of Density:**
By definition, density is the *fraction of all possible ties that are actually present*:

$$D = \dfrac{\text{Actual Edges}}{\text{Maximum Possible Edges}}$$

**Step 3 — Substitution:**

$$D = \dfrac{\vert E \vert}{\dfrac{n(n-1)}{2}}$$

**Step 4 — Simplification:**

$$D = \dfrac{2 \vert E \vert}{n(n-1)}$$

**Step 5 — Boundary Conditions:**
- If $\vert E \vert = 0$ (empty network): $D = 0$
- If $\vert E \vert = n(n-1)/2$ (complete graph $K_n$): $D = 1$

> [!NOTE]
> For a **directed** network, the maximum is $n(n-1)$ (since $(i,j)$ and $(j,i)$ are distinct). So $D = \dfrac{\vert E \vert}{n(n-1)}$.

---

### 3.2 Derivation: Average Degree from Density

**Goal:** Show that the average degree $\langle k \rangle$ equals density $\times (n-1)$.

**Step 1 — Handshaking Lemma:**
The sum of degrees over all nodes equals twice the number of edges:

$$\sum_{i=1}^{n} k_i = 2 \vert E \vert$$

*Explanation:* Each edge contributes exactly 1 to the degree of each of its two endpoints, so 2 in total across all nodes.

**Step 2 — Average Degree Definition:**

$$\langle k \rangle = \dfrac{1}{n} \sum_{i=1}^{n} k_i = \dfrac{2 \vert E \vert}{n}$$

**Step 3 — Express in Terms of Density:**
Substituting $\vert E \vert = D \cdot \dfrac{n(n-1)}{2}$:

$$\langle k \rangle = \dfrac{2 \cdot D \cdot \dfrac{n(n-1)}{2}}{n}$$

$$\langle k \rangle = D \cdot \dfrac{n(n-1)}{n}$$

$$\boxed{\langle k \rangle = D \cdot (n-1)}$$

**Step 4 — Interpretation:** A network with density $D=0.5$ has every node connected, on average, to *half* of all other nodes.

---

### 3.3 Derivation: Normalized Degree Centrality

**Goal:** Normalize raw degree into the range $[0, 1]$.

**Step 1 — Raw Degree:** $k_i$ ranges from $0$ (isolated) to $n-1$ (connected to all).

**Step 2 — Division by Maximum:**

$$C_D^{norm}(i) = \dfrac{k_i}{n-1}$$

**Step 3 — Boundary Check:**
- Isolated node: $C_D = 0/(n-1) = 0$ ✓
- Star center: $C_D = (n-1)/(n-1) = 1$ ✓

---

### 3.4 Derivation: Closeness Centrality

**Goal:** Derive the closeness centrality of a node $i$.

**Step 1 — Concept:** A node is "close" if it has short geodesic distances to all others. The total distance to all other nodes is:

$$S_i = \sum_{j \ne i} d(i,j)$$

where $d(i,j)$ is the length of the shortest path from $i$ to $j$.

**Step 2 — Inverse for "Centrality":** The shorter the sum, the *more central* the node. So we take the reciprocal and scale by $(n-1)$ for normalization:

$$C_C(i) = \dfrac{n-1}{\sum_{j \ne i} d(i,j)}$$

**Step 3 — Range Check:** If a node is directly connected to all others (e.g., a star center in an undirected star graph), $d(i,j) = 1$ for all $j$, so $S_i = n-1$, giving $C_C(i) = 1$ — the maximum.

---

### 3.5 Derivation: Betweenness Centrality

**Goal:** Quantify the fraction of shortest paths that pass through node $i$.

**Step 1 — Define:** Let $\sigma_{st}$ = total number of shortest paths from $s$ to $t$. Let $\sigma_{st}(i)$ = number of those paths that *pass through* node $i$.

**Step 2 — Ratio of Paths Through $i$:**

$$g_i(s,t) = \dfrac{\sigma_{st}(i)}{\sigma_{st}}$$

**Step 3 — Sum Over All Node Pairs (excluding $i$):**

$$C_B(i) = \sum_{s \ne i \ne t} g_i(s,t) = \sum_{s \ne i \ne t} \dfrac{\sigma_{st}(i)}{\sigma_{st}}$$

**Step 4 — Normalized Betweenness (for comparison across networks of different size):**

$$C_B^{norm}(i) = \dfrac{C_B(i)}{\dfrac{(n-1)(n-2)}{2}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Normalization Divisor Explanation:** The total number of *ordered* pairs $(s,t)$ excluding $i$ is $(n-1)(n-2)$. Since the sum is symmetric, dividing by 2 gives the unique unordered pairs.

---

### 3.6 Worked Numerical Example — Tiny Network

Consider a directed graph with 4 nodes $A, B, C, D$ and edges:
- $A \to B$, $A \to C$
- $B \to C$
- $C \to D$
- $D \to A$, $D \to C$

**Compute in-degree, out-degree, density, and betweenness.**

**Step 1 — In-degrees and Out-degrees:**

$$k_A^{in} = 1 \; (\text{from } D), \quad k_A^{out} = 2 \; (B,C)$$

$$k_B^{in} = 1 \; (\text{from } A), \quad k_B^{out} = 1 \; (C)$$

$$k_C^{in} = 3 \; (A, B, D), \quad k_C^{out} = 1 \; (D)$$

$$k_D^{in} = 1 \; (\text{from } C), \quad k_D^{out} = 2 \; (A, C)$$

**Step 2 — Total Edges and Density:**

$$\vert E \vert = 6$$

For a directed network:

$$D = \dfrac{\vert E \vert}{n(n-1)} = \dfrac{6}{4 \cdot 3} = \dfrac{6}{12} = 0.5$$

**Step 3 — Identify All Shortest Paths (Geodesics):**
- $A \to C$: direct (length 1) — also indirect $A \to B \to C$ (length 2). Direct is shortest: $\sigma_{AC} = 1$.
- $A \to D$: $A \to C \to D$ (length 2); $A \to B \to C \to D$ (length 3). Shortest: $\sigma_{AD} = 1$, passes through $C$.
- $B \to D$: $B \to C \to D$ (length 2). Passes through $C$.
- $D \to B$: $D \to A \to B$ (length 2). Passes through $A$.

**Step 4 — Betweenness of Each Node:**

For $C$: lies on paths $A \to D$ and $B \to D$. So:

$$C_B(C) = 1 + 1 = 2$$

For $A$: lies on path $D \to B$. So:

$$C_B(A) = 1$$

For $B, D$: do not lie on any shortest path between other nodes.

$$C_B(B) = 0, \quad C_B(D) = 0$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Result:** $C$ is the structural broker — it is the *only conduit* connecting the upstream ($A, B$) to the downstream ($D$).
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Historical Timeline of SNA Development (Mermaid Flow)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    node1[Moreno 1934 - Sociogram Birth]
    node2[Bavelas Leavitt 1950s - Position Theory]
    node3[Milgram 1967 - Six Degrees]
    node4[Granovetter 1973 - Weak Ties]
    node5[Burt 1973 - Structural Holes]
    node6[Watts Strogatz 1998 - Small World]
    node7[Barabasi Albert 1999 - Scale Free]
    node8[Web 2.0 2000s - Online Networks]
    node9[Big Data 2010s Present - Modern SNA]

    node1 --> node2
    node2 --> node3
    node3 --> node4
    node4 --> node5
    node5 --> node6
    node6 --> node7
    node7 --> node8
    node8 --> node9
```

### 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture of SNA Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    rawdata[Raw Social Data]:::raw
    clean[Data Cleaning]:::clean
    build[Graph Construction]:::build
    mat[Adjacency Matrix]:::mat
    measures[Compute Measures]:::meas
    deg[Degree Centrality]:::sub1
    close[Closeness Centrality]:::sub2
    bet[Betweenness Centrality]:::sub3
    eig[Eigenvector Centrality]:::sub4
    pr[PageRank]:::sub5
    cluster[Clustering Coefficient]:::sub6
    density[Network Density]:::sub7
    vis[Visualization]:::vis
    insight[Insights and Decisions]:::ins

    rawdata --> clean
    clean --> build
    build --> mat
    mat --> measures
    measures --> deg
    measures --> close
    measures --> bet
    measures --> eig
    measures --> pr
    measures --> cluster
    measures --> density
    deg --> vis
    close --> vis
    bet --> vis
    eig --> vis
    pr --> vis
    cluster --> vis
    density --> vis
    vis --> insight

    classDef raw fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#FF8C00,color:#000
    classDef clean fill:#E0FFFF,stroke:#008B8B,color:#000
    classDef build fill:#F0FFF0,stroke:#228B22,color:#000
    classDef mat fill:#FFF0F5,stroke:#C71585,color:#000
    classDef meas fill:#FFFAFA,stroke:#000,color:#000
    classDef sub1 fill:#E6E6FA,stroke:#000,color:#000
    classDef sub2 fill:#E6E6FA,stroke:#000,color:#000
    classDef sub3 fill:#E6E6FA,stroke:#000,color:#000
    classDef sub4 fill:#E6E6FA,stroke:#000,color:#000
    classDef sub5 fill:#E6E6FA,stroke:#000,color:#000
    classDef sub6 fill:#E6E6FA,stroke:#000,color:#000
    classDef sub7 fill:#E6E6FA,stroke:#000,color:#000
    classDef vis fill:#FFFACD,stroke:#B8860B,color:#000
    classDef ins fill:#90EE90,stroke:#006400,color:#000
```

### 4.3 Centrality Comparison — Conceptual Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph DEG [Degree Centrality]
        D1[Counts direct ties]
        D2[Local popularity]
        D3[Easy to compute]
    end
    subgraph CLO [Closeness Centrality]
        C1[Sum of shortest paths]
        C2[Speed of information]
        C3[Global reach]
    end
    subgraph BET [Betweenness Centrality]
        B1[Paths through node]
        B2[Brokerage power]
        B3[Gatekeeper role]
    end
    subgraph EIG [Eigenvector Centrality]
        E1[Neighbors importance]
        E2[Quality over quantity]
        E3[Recursive influence]
    end
    subgraph PRK [PageRank]
        P1[Random surfer model]
        P2[Web ranking]
        P3[Damping factor d]
    end
```

### 4.4 Example Network for Visualization (Star + Triangle)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A((HUB)):::hub
    B((X1)):::leaf
    C((X2)):::leaf
    D((X3)):::leaf
    E((Y1)):::tri
    F((Y2)):::tri
    G((Y3)):::tri

    A --- B
    A --- C
    A --- D
    A --- E
    A --- F
    A --- G
    E --- F
    F --- G
    E --- G

    classDef hub fill:#FFD700,stroke:#FF6347,color:#000
    classDef leaf fill:#ADD8E6,stroke:#000,color:#000
    classDef tri fill:#90EE90,stroke:#006400,color:#000
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** Node `A` is a hub with high **degree**, high **closeness** (near to all), and high **betweenness** (path from any leaf to any triangle must pass through it). It illustrates how *a single node* can dominate multiple centrality dimensions.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam - Model Question 1]**
> **Q1. Define Social Network Analysis. Differentiate it from traditional social science research.** *(3 marks)*

**Model Answer:**
Social Network Analysis (SNA) is the systematic study of social structures using graph theory and matrix algebra, where individuals/organizations are represented as **nodes** and their relationships as **edges**.

| Aspect | Traditional Social Science | SNA |
|---|---|---|
| Focus | Attributes of individuals (age, income) | Relationships and structure |
| Unit of analysis | The person | The *tie* between persons |
| Data | Surveys, demographics | Relational data, ties |
| Output | Statistical correlations | Network patterns, centrality |
| View | *Individualist* | *Relational / Structuralist* |

The paradigm shift introduced by SNA is the **relational worldview**: a person's behavior, power, and opportunities are determined more by their **position** in the network than by their intrinsic attributes.

> **[KTU University Exam - Model Question 2]**
> **Q2. With a neat example, explain the concept of "Strength of Weak Ties" proposed by Granovetter.** *(3 marks)*

**Model Answer:**
Mark Granovetter (1973), in his landmark paper *"The Strength of Weak Ties"*, argued that **weak ties (acquaintances)** are often more valuable than **strong ties (close friends)** for:
- **Information diffusion** — new job openings, novel ideas
- **Social mobility** — job referrals from acquaintances
- **Bridge-building** — connecting different social clusters

**Example:** When searching for a job, people often hear about openings through *casual acquaintances* (weak ties), not close friends — because close friends tend to share the same information as you, while acquaintances belong to different social circles and bring *non-redundant* information.

This leads to the principle: **bridging weak ties act as information conduits between otherwise disconnected communities.**

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam - Model Question Pattern, Module 1]**

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**Q(a). Explain in detail the historical development of Social Network Analysis, highlighting the contributions of Jacob Moreno, Mark Granovetter, and Barabási–Albert.** *(7 marks)*

**Model Answer:**

**Jacob Moreno (1934) — The Birth:**
- Moreno introduced the **sociogram** — a visual map of social relationships — to study the interpersonal structure of a classroom and a factory.
- He coined **sociometry**, the quantitative measurement of social relationships.
- His key insight: *social structure exists independent of individual attributes* and can be measured objectively.

**Mark Granovetter (1973) — Strength of Weak Ties:**
- His seminal paper argued that weak ties (acquaintances) are critical channels for novel information flow between communities.
- Strong ties tend to circulate *redundant* information, while weak ties carry *novel* information.
- Implication: people with many weak ties (bridges) have informational and economic advantages.

**Barabási & Albert (1999) — Scale-Free Networks:**
- They discovered that real networks (the web, citation networks, biological networks) are **scale-free**: their degree distribution follows a **power law** $P(k) \sim k^{-\gamma}$, where $\gamma$ typically lies in $[2,3]$.
- Scale-free networks are characterized by the presence of **hubs** — a few nodes with disproportionately many connections.
- The mechanism is **preferential attachment**: new nodes preferentially connect to already well-connected nodes ("the rich get richer").

> [!NOTE]
> **Synthesis:** Moreno founded the *method* (sociograms), Granovetter developed the *theory* (weak ties), and Barabási-Albert provided the *mathematical model* (scale-free). Together, they form three pillars of SNA.

---

**Q(b). Define and explain the following centrality measures with formulas: (i) Degree Centrality (ii) Betweenness Centrality (iii) Eigenvector Centrality.** *(7 marks)*

**Model Answer:**

**(i) Degree Centrality:**
- The simplest and most intuitive measure.
- Counts the **number of direct ties** a node has.
- Formula (raw):

$$C_D(i) = k_i = \sum_{j} a_{ij}$$

where $a_{ij}$ is the $(i,j)$ entry of the adjacency matrix.
- Normalized:

$$C_D^{norm}(i) = \dfrac{k_i}{n-1}$$

- A node with high degree centrality is **popular, active, or socially prominent** (e.g., a celebrity with millions of Twitter followers).

**(ii) Betweenness Centrality:**
- Measures the extent to which a node lies **on the shortest paths** between other nodes.
- Formula:

$$C_B(i) = \sum_{s \ne i \ne t} \dfrac{\sigma_{st}(i)}{\sigma_{st}}$$

- A node with high betweenness acts as a **broker** or **gatekeeper**, controlling information flow between otherwise disconnected groups.
- Example: A manager who is the sole communication link between two departments has very high betweenness.

**(iii) Eigenvector Centrality:**
- Assigns relative scores to nodes based on the principle that **connections to high-scoring nodes contribute more** than equal connections to low-scoring nodes.
- The centrality of a node is proportional to the sum of centralities of its neighbors:

$$x_i = \dfrac{1}{\lambda} \sum_{j} a_{ij} x_j$$

In matrix form:

$$A \mathbf{x} = \lambda \mathbf{x}$$

where $A$ is the adjacency matrix and $\mathbf{x}$ is the eigenvector corresponding to the largest eigenvalue $\lambda_1$.
- Example: Google used a variant (PageRank) — a page is important if it is linked by other important pages.
- Eigenvector centrality captures **prestige, influence, and recursive importance**.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**Q(a). What is meant by a Scale-Free Network? Explain its properties, the Power Law degree distribution, and the Barabási–Albert model of preferential attachment.** *(7 marks)*

**Model Answer:**

**Definition:** A **scale-free network** is a network whose degree distribution follows a **power law** $P(k) \sim k^{-\gamma}$, in contrast to a random (Erdős–Rényi) network whose degree distribution is Poisson.

**Key Properties:**
1. **Hubs exist:** A small number of nodes have a very large number of connections, while most nodes have few.
2. **Power-law tail:** The probability of finding a node with degree $k$ decays polynomially, not exponentially.
3. **Robustness to random failures, fragility to targeted attacks:** Removing random nodes barely affects connectivity, but removing hubs destroys the network.
4. **Self-similarity / scale-invariance:** The structure looks similar at any magnification.

**Power Law Degree Distribution:**

$$P(k) = C \cdot k^{-\gamma}, \quad \gamma \in [2, 3] \text{ (typical)}$$

On a log-log plot, this yields a **straight line** with slope $-\gamma$.

**Barabási–Albert Model (Preferential Attachment):**
- Starts with a small seed network of $m_0$ nodes fully connected.
- At each time step, a new node is added with $m$ edges, where $m \le m_0$.
- The new node connects to an existing node $i$ with probability proportional to its current degree:

$$\Pi(i) = \dfrac{k_i}{\sum_{j} k_j}$$

- This is the *"rich get richer"* or **Yule process**.
- Result: After $t$ steps, the network exhibits a power-law degree distribution with $\gamma = 3$.

**Real-World Examples:** The World Wide Web, citation networks, social networks (Twitter, Facebook), protein interaction networks, and airline networks are all scale-free.

---

**Q(b). For the given network with 5 nodes, calculate the degree centrality, betweenness centrality, and density of the network. The edges are: (1–2), (2–3), (3–4), (4–5), (5–1), (1–3).** *(7 marks)*

**Model Answer:**

This is a 5-node **undirected** cycle with a **chord** (1–3). Total nodes $n = 5$.

**Step 1 — List all edges:**
$\vert E \vert = 6$ (5 cycle edges + 1 chord)

**Step 2 — Degree of Each Node:**

$$k_1 = 3 \; (\text{connects to } 2, 5, 3)$$
$$k_2 = 2 \; (1, 3)$$
$$k_3 = 3 \; (2, 4, 1)$$
$$k_4 = 2 \; (3, 5)$$
$$k_5 = 2 \; (4, 1)$$

**Step 3 — Normalized Degree Centrality:**

$$C_D^{norm}(i) = \dfrac{k_i}{n-1} = \dfrac{k_i}{4}$$

| Node | $k_i$ | $C_D^{norm}$ |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | 3 | 0.75 |
| 2 | 2 | 0.50 |
| 3 | 3 | 0.75 |
| 4 | 2 | 0.50 |
| 5 | 2 | 0.50 |

**Step 4 — Density of the Network:**

$$D = \dfrac{2 \vert E \vert}{n(n-1)} = \dfrac{2 \cdot 6}{5 \cdot 4} = \dfrac{12}{20} = 0.6$$

**Step 5 — Betweenness Centrality:**

We compute $\sigma_{st}$ (total shortest paths) and $\sigma_{st}(i)$ (those passing through $i$).

The shortest-path distances (using chord 1–3):

| Pair | Direct Path Length | Path |
|---|---|---|
| 1–2 | 1 | 1–2 |
| 1–3 | 1 | 1–3 (chord) |
| 1–4 | 2 | 1–3–4 |
| 1–5 | 1 | 1–5 |
| 2–3 | 1 | 2–3 |
| 2–4 | 2 | 2–3–4 |
| 2–5 | 2 | 2–1–5 |
| 3–4 | 1 | 3–4 |
| 3–5 | 2 | 3–1–5 |
| 4–5 | 1 | 4–5 |

For pair 1–4: shortest paths are 1–3–4 ($\sigma = 1$, passes through 3) and 1–5–4 ($\sigma = 1$, passes through 5). So both nodes 3 and 5 are on one shortest path each.

For pair 2–5: shortest paths are 2–1–5 ($\sigma = 1$, passes through 1).

For pair 3–5: shortest path is 3–1–5 ($\sigma = 1$, passes through 1).

| Node | Paths through it | Total Betweenness |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | (2–5), (3–5) | 2 |
| 2 | none | 0 |
| 3 | (1–4) | 1 |
| 4 | none | 0 |
| 5 | (1–4) | 1 |

**Final Results:**

$$C_B(1) = 2, \; C_B(2) = 0, \; C_B(3) = 1, \; C_B(4) = 0, \; C_B(5) = 1$$
$$D = 0.6$$

**Interpretation:** Node 1 is the **primary broker** — it lies on the most shortest paths. Nodes 3 and 5 are *secondary* brokers through the chord.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> - **Common Mistake 1:** Students forget to *normalize* betweenness or degree centrality. For degree, always divide by $(n-1)$. For betweenness, divide by $(n-1)(n-2)/2$ for cross-network comparability.
> - **Common Mistake 2:** Forgetting to use the **correct denominator** for density: $n(n-1)/2$ for *undirected*, $n(n-1)$ for *directed*. A wrong denominator loses 2 marks easily.
> - **Common Mistake 3:** Confusing **geodesic distance** with **Euclidean distance** or any path. Always use the *shortest* path.
> - **Common Mistake 4:** In betweenness, not checking whether there are *multiple* shortest paths between $s$ and $t$. If $\sigma_{st} = 2$ and node $i$ lies on one of them, it gets $1/2$, not 1.
> - **Common Mistake 5:** Treating PageRank and Eigenvector Centrality as identical. PageRank has a **damping factor $d$** (typically 0.85) — Eigenvector Centrality does not.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Block — Read 3 times before exam.**

- ✅ SNA is the study of **structure and relationships**, not individual attributes.
- ✅ The discipline began with **Moreno's sociogram (1934)**.
- ✅ **Granovetter (1973) — Strength of Weak Ties** — bridges bring novel info.
- ✅ **Milgram (1967) — Six Degrees of Separation** — small-world property.
- ✅ **Watts & Strogatz (1998) — Small-World Model** — high clustering + short path.
- ✅ **Barabási–Albert (1999) — Scale-Free / Preferential Attachment** — power law degree distribution.
- ✅ **Degree Centrality:** local popularity, $C_D(i) = k_i / (n-1)$.
- ✅ **Closeness Centrality:** speed of spread, $C_C(i) = (n-1) / \sum d(i,j)$.
- ✅ **Betweenness Centrality:** brokerage, sum of fractions of shortest paths through $i$.
- ✅ **Eigenvector Centrality:** recursive prestige, $A\mathbf{x} = \lambda \mathbf{x}$.
- ✅ **PageRank:** random surfer with damping factor $d \approx 0.85$.
- ✅ **Density (undirected):** $D = 2|E| / [n(n-1)]$; **directed:** $|E| / [n(n-1)]$.
- ✅ **Local Clustering Coefficient:** fraction of pairs of neighbors of $i$ that are connected.
- ✅ **Diameter:** longest geodesic in the network.
- ✅ **Homophily:** similar nodes connect; **Heterophily:** dissimilar connect.
- ✅ **Reciprocity** applies only to directed networks.
- ✅ Always write the **formula first**, then **substitute numbers**, then **comment on result**.
- ✅ Use **examples** (a class, a company, a citation network) to anchor every definition.
- ✅ Cite a **real-world application** in every 7-mark question to score beyond the boundary.
- ✅ Avoid absolute-value pipes `|` inside markdown tables — use `\vert` or `\mid`.
- ✅ Always specify the **type of network** (directed/undirected/weighted) at the start of any calculation.

> [!TIP]
> **Final Exam Mantra:** *“Position over attribute. Structure over content. Bridges over clusters. Weak ties over strong ties. Power-law over Poisson.”* Memorize and you will not lose a single conceptual mark in Module 1.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Electronic Sources for Network Analysis - Electronic Discussion Networks, Blogs and Online Communities, Web-Based Networks, Applications of Social Network Analysis
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECMT528 - CONCEPTS IN SOCIAL NETWORK ANALYSIS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Electronic Sources for Network Analysis - Electronic Discussion Networks, Blogs and Online Communities, Web-Based Networks, Applications of Social Network Analysis

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Electronic Sources for Network Analysis — Formal Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition (KTU Syllabus-Aligned):**
> *Electronic Sources for Network Analysis* refer to the digitally mediated communication infrastructures — including e-mail systems, mailing lists, Usenet groups, discussion forums, blogs, microblogs, online communities, and the World Wide Web — that generate large-scale, traceable, and machine-readable records of human (and machine) interaction. These records form the empirical substrate from which **Social Network Analysis (SNA)** extracts nodes (actors), edges (relations), and higher-order structural patterns.

In the language of the KTU 2024 PECMT528 syllabus, these sources are the **primary data generators** for modern SNA. They have largely replaced the traditional pen-and-paper sociometric surveys used in classical (pre-2000) network research because they offer three decisive advantages:

1. **Scale** — millions of interactions per day (Twitter/X alone generates *billions* of edges daily).
2. **Traceability** — every interaction is timestamped, addressable, and archived.
3. **Permission-free harvesting** — APIs and crawlers enable reproducible data collection.

### 1.2 The Four Pillars of the Topic

| Pillar | Core Question Answered | Typical SNA Output |
|---|---|---|
| **Electronic Discussion Networks** | *Who replies to whom?* | Reply graphs, thread trees |
| **Blogs and Online Communities** | *Who creates, comments, and links?* | Comment cascades, hyperlink webs |
| **Web-Based Networks** | *How are pages connected?* | The Web graph, PageRank-style networks |
| **Applications of SNA** | *Why does structure matter?* | Influence maps, viral-spread models |

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — Plain-English Intuition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The "City Traffic Map" Analogy**
> Imagine a city where every person is a **house** and every interaction (an email reply, a blog comment, a hyperlink) is a **road** connecting two houses. A traffic engineer can study the city purely by looking at the road map — without ever meeting the residents. *Electronic sources* are the equivalent of **automated traffic sensors** that log every road usage 24/7. Social Network Analysis is the engineer who reads those logs to find congestion points (central nodes), isolated villages (peripheral nodes), and busy roundabouts (broker nodes).

### 1.4 Key Terminology Anchors

- **Node (Vertex, Actor):** An individual, account, page, or community participating in the electronic medium.
- **Edge (Link, Tie):** A directed or undirected interaction (reply, mention, hyperlink, friendship).
- **Dyad:** A pair of nodes and the (optional) edge between them.
- **Triad:** A triple of nodes; foundational unit for studying closure, balance, and clustering.
- **Hyperlink Network:** A directed graph in which edges are `<a href="…">` references between web pages.
- **Threaded Discussion:** A tree-structured sequence of posts ordered by reply relation.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why This Matters for KTU Exams:**
> Every definition above has appeared (in paraphrased form) as a 2-mark sub-question in previous KTU university exams. Memorize the *italicized* keywords verbatim.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Electronic Discussion Networks (EDNs)

An **Electronic Discussion Network (EDN)** is a graph constructed from asynchronous, text-based computer-mediated communication. The classical examples are:

- **E-mail networks** — nodes = e-mail addresses, edges = "sent at least one message to."
- **Mailing lists / Listservs** — nodes = subscribers, edges = co-participation in threads.
- **Usenet newsgroups** — nodes = authors, edges = "X replied to Y's post."
- **Bulletin Board Systems (BBS)** — historical predecessor; threads form a tree.

#### 2.1.1 Mathematical Model

Let $\mathcal{D} = (V, E, T)$ be a discussion network where:

$$V = \{v_1, v_2, \dots, v_n\} \quad \text{(set of participants)}$$

$$E \subseteq V \times V \quad \text{(directed reply edges)}$$

$$T : E \to \mathbb{Z}^+ \quad \text{(timestamp function)}$$

A typical SNA question on this data asks: *"Find the most central participant."* Centrality is operationalized in **Section 3** below.

#### 2.1.2 Thread Tree Structure

A single discussion thread is a **rooted tree** in which the root post has no parent and every reply has exactly one parent. The depth of a node (in replies) is a strong predictor of conversational leadership.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Insight:** In e-mail reply networks, the *From* and *To* fields are public metadata. The Reply-To chain therefore gives a **deterministic, ground-truth dyadic dataset** — historically the gold standard for validating SNA algorithms.

### 2.2 Blogs and Online Communities

A **blog (weblog)** is a frequently updated, reverse-chronological web journal authored by an individual or small group. When blogs *link* to each other and to news sources, the resulting structure is the **Blogosphere**.

#### 2.2.1 Three-Layer Network Model

The blog-mediated social network can be decomposed into three coupled layers:

1. **Author layer** — bloggers and their hyperlink citations.
2. **Comment layer** — commenter-to-blogger reply edges.
3. **Community layer** — clusters of blogs sharing tags, trackbacks, or topic keywords.

Online communities (Reddit, Stack Exchange, Discord servers, Facebook Groups) extend this further with **membership edges** and **moderation edges**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Trackback:** A reverse-link protocol (originally a Movable Type feature) by which Blog A automatically informs Blog B that A has cited B — producing a *bi-directional* hyperlink edge.

#### 2.2.2 Structural Properties

Empirical studies (Adamic & Glance 2005 on the political blogosphere; Lerman & Jones 2006) consistently find that blog networks exhibit:

- **Power-law out-degree distribution** — a few "A-list" blogs receive most inbound links.
- **High reciprocity** in the comment layer (≈40–60 % of comment edges are reciprocated).
- **Community structure** aligned with topical affiliation rather than geography.

### 2.3 Web-Based Networks

The **World Wide Web** is the largest human-made network ever measured. As of recent crawls, the publicly indexable web contains on the order of **$10^{10}$ pages** and **$10^{11}$ hyperlinks**.

#### 2.3.1 The Web Graph

Define the web graph as:

$$G_{\text{web}} = (P, H), \quad H \subseteq P \times P$$

where $P$ is the set of pages and each $(p_i, p_j) \in H$ denotes a hyperlink from $p_i$ to $p_j$. The graph is:

- **Directed** — edges have a source page and a target page.
- **Sparse** — the average out-degree is roughly $\langle k_{\text{out}} \rangle \approx 30$–$50$.
- **Scale-free** — the in-degree distribution follows a power law:

$$P(k_{\text{in}}) \propto k_{\text{in}}^{-\gamma}, \quad \gamma \approx 2.1$$

#### 2.3.2 The Bow-Tie Structure of the Web

Broder et al. (2000) showed that the macroscopic structure of the web is a **bow-tie** with four components:

| Component | Description | Typical Fraction |
|---|---|---|
| **SCC (Strongly Connected Core)** | Pages mutually reachable | ≈ 28 % |
| **IN** | Pages that can reach SCC but not be reached back | ≈ 21 % |
| **OUT** | Pages reachable from SCC but cannot reach back | ≈ 21 % |
| **Tendrils / Disconnected** | The rest of the graph | ≈ 30 % |

### 2.4 Applications of Social Network Analysis

SNA drawn from electronic sources is used in:

1. **Marketing & recommendation** — finding influential nodes (influencer marketing).
2. **Epidemiology** — modelling the spread of diseases *and* of memes/viruses.
3. **Political analysis** — mapping polarisation, echo chambers, and information cascades.
4. **Cybersecurity** — bot detection, dark-web community detection, phishing ring analysis.
5. **Organisational science** — measuring intra-firm communication using e-mail logs.
6. **Crisis response** — identifying authoritative voices during disasters (Twitter/X emergency chatter).

### 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table is the **minimum required** for a Module-1 KTU answer. It is built strictly from syllabus-mentioned concepts.

| # | Concept | Formula / Definition | Typical KTU Marks |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Graph order | $\vert V \vert = n$ | 2 |
| 2 | Graph size | $\vert E \vert = m$ | 2 |
| 3 | Degree of node $i$ | $k_i = \sum_{j} a_{ij}$ | 3 |
| 4 | Average degree | $\langle k \rangle = \frac{2m}{n}$ | 3 |
| 5 | Density | $\Delta = \frac{2m}{n(n-1)}$ (undirected) | 5 |
| 6 | Degree centrality (undirected) | $C_D(i) = \frac{k_i}{n-1}$ | 5 |
| 7 | Closeness centrality | $C_C(i) = \frac{n-1}{\sum_{j} d(i,j)}$ | 7 |
| 8 | Betweenness centrality | $C_B(i) = \sum_{s \ne i \ne t} \frac{\sigma_{st}(i)}{\sigma_{st}}$ | 7 |
| 9 | Clustering coefficient (local) | $C_i = \frac{2 e_i}{k_i(k_i-1)}$ | 5 |
| 10 | Reciprocity (directed) | $r = \frac{\vert L \rvert}{\vert L \vert + \vert U \vert}$ | 5 |
| 11 | Power-law degree distribution | $P(k) = C k^{-\gamma}$ | 3 |
| 12 | Bow-tie SCC fraction | $f_{\text{SCC}} = \frac{\vert \text{SCC} \vert}{\vert P \vert}$ | 2 |

> **Notation note:** Per the KTU-PREMIER rules, vertical bars inside math are rendered as $\vert$ rather than `|` to preserve table syntax.

### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Search engines** (Google, Bing) use PageRank — a spectral centrality on the web graph.
- **Spam detection** uses abnormal link patterns detected via SNA metrics.
- **Public-health agencies** use contact networks harvested from electronic check-ins.
- **HR analytics** mine e-mail graphs to detect siloed teams and burnout risk.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 From an Electronic Discussion to a Formal Graph

Consider a small e-mail thread. The raw log is:

> **Thread T1**
> - *A* sends to *B*, *C* at 09:00.
> - *B* replies to *A*, *C* at 09:14.
> - *C* replies to *B* at 09:30.
> - *A* replies to *C* at 09:42.

We model the directed edge set $E$ as the ordered pairs below.

$$\begin{aligned}
E &= \{(A,B), (A,C), \\
&\quad (B,A), (B,C), \\
&\quad (C,B), \\
&\quad (A,C)_{\text{ts}=0942}\}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 1 — Identify vertices.**

$$V = \{A, B, C\} \quad \Rightarrow \quad n = 3$$

**Step 2 — Identify edges.** Note $(A,C)$ appears twice; in the simple-graph model we keep one, so $m = 5$.

**Step 3 — Build adjacency matrix $A$.** Order rows/columns as $A, B, C$.

$$A_{\text{adj}} = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 1 & 1 \\ 1 & 0 & 1 \\ 0 & 1 & 0 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 4 — Compute out-degree $k^{\text{out}}_i$ and in-degree $k^{\text{in}}_i$.**

$$\begin{aligned}
k^{\text{out}}_A &= A_{12} + A_{13} = 1 + 1 = 2 \\
k^{\text{out}}_B &= A_{21} + A_{23} = 1 + 1 = 2 \\
k^{\text{out}}_C &= A_{31} + A_{32} = 0 + 1 = 1
\end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned}
k^{\text{in}}_A &= A_{21} + A_{31} = 1 + 0 = 1 \\
k^{\text{in}}_B &= A_{12} + A_{32} = 1 + 1 = 2 \\
k^{\text{in}}_C &= A_{13} + A_{23} = 1 + 1 = 2
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 5 — Compute degree centrality $C_D$.**

$$\begin{aligned}
C_D(A) &= \frac{2}{3-1} = 1.00 \\
C_D(B) &= \frac{2}{3-1} = 1.00 \\
C_D(C) &= \frac{1}{3-1} = 0.50
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 6 — Compute density $\Delta$.**

$$\Delta = \frac{m}{n(n-1)} = \frac{5}{3 \cdot 2} = \frac{5}{6} \approx 0.833$$
