> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECMT528 - CONCEPTS IN SOCIAL NETWORK ANALYSIS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Modelling and Aggregating Social Network Data - State-of-the-art in Network Data Representation, Ontological Representation of Social Individuals, Ontological Representation of Social Relationships, Aggregating and Reasoning with Social Network Data, Advanced Representations

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 📘 MODULE 2 — Modelling and Aggregating Social Network Data

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 What is "Modelling and Aggregating Social Network Data"?

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal KTU Definition**
> *Modelling and Aggregating Social Network Data* refers to the formal representation of actors (individuals), their attributes, and the relational ties that connect them using standardized schemas (graphs, matrices, ontologies, knowledge graphs), coupled with computational mechanisms for combining, summarizing, and inferring higher-order knowledge from these representations.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme context, this topic sits at the intersection of **graph theory**, **semantic web technologies**, and **knowledge representation**. The objective is to move beyond a flat "list of friends" and represent a social ecosystem in a way that a machine can *understand, query, and reason about*.

### 1.2 State-of-the-Art in Network Data Representation

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Analogy (Intuition)**
> Imagine a telephone directory from 1990. It only stored *names* and *numbers* (a flat table). A modern social network data model, by contrast, is like a *living organism*: each person has a profile (attributes), each relationship has a type (friend, colleague, sibling), each interaction has a timestamp, and the system can *infer* new facts (e.g., "Alice is Bob's aunt because her sister is his mother"). This is the jump from **data** to **knowledge**.

Historically, social networks have been represented in three layered paradigms, each with progressively richer semantics:

| Layer | Representation Style | KTU Conceptual Tag |
|---|---|---|
| **L1 – Syntactic** | Edge lists, adjacency matrices, adjacency lists | Raw graph data |
| **L2 – Semantic** | RDF triples, property graphs, labelled graphs | Self-describing data |
| **L3 – Ontological** | RDFS / OWL / Description Logic | Reasoning-enabled data |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **state-of-the-art** representation combines **L1 (graph structure) + L2 (semantic labels) + L3 (ontological rules)**. This hybrid is the foundation of modern *Knowledge Graphs* (KGs), as used by Google, Facebook (Open Graph), and LinkedIn.

### 1.3 Why this Matters in Engineering (KTU Industry Context)

In real engineering deployments (recommender systems, fraud detection, public-health surveillance), the *raw adjacency matrix* is insufficient. The industry demands:
- **Heterogeneity** — multiple node/edge types (e.g., Person ↔ Company ↔ Product).
- **Reasoning** — automatic inference of implicit ties.
- **Interoperability** — sharing data across organizations using shared vocabularies.

This drives the move toward **ontological representations**, which we explore next.

### 1.4 Roadmap of the Module

1. **Network Data Representation** — how do we *store* a network?
2. **Ontological Representation of Individuals** — how do we *describe* a person?
3. **Ontological Representation of Relationships** — how do we *describe* a tie?
4. **Aggregating and Reasoning** — how do we *combine* and *infer*?
5. **Advanced Representations** — what lies beyond classical graphs?
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Layer 1 — Syntactic Representations (Classical Graph Data)

### 2.1.1 Edge List
A network $G = (V, E)$ is stored as a flat list of ordered pairs.

$$ E = \{ (u_1, v_1),\ (u_2, v_2),\ \dots,\ (u_m, v_m) \} $$

- **Why:** Most compact format for **sparse** graphs.
- **How:** Each edge consumes $O(1)$ storage; total space $O(\vert E \vert)$.
- **Limitation:** Cannot store *attributes* on nodes/edges natively.

### 2.1.2 Adjacency Matrix
A square $n \times n$ matrix $A$ where $n = \vert V \vert$ and:

$$ A_{ij} = \begin{cases} 1 & \text{if } (v_i, v_j) \in E \\ 0 & \text{otherwise} \end{cases} $$

- **Space:** $O(\vert V \vert^2)$ — *quadratic* blow-up.
- **Edge attribute storage:** Use a *weighted* matrix $W_{ij} \in \mathbb{R}$ to encode tie strength.

### 2.1.3 Adjacency List
For each vertex $v \in V$, maintain a list $\text{Nbr}(v)$ of its neighbors.

- **Trade-off:** Space $O(\vert V \vert + \vert E \vert)$; neighbor lookup $O(\deg(v))$.

### 2.1.4 Property Graph (Bridge to L2)
A *property graph* augments a labelled graph with **key–value stores** on nodes and edges:
$$ G_{PG} = (V, E, \lambda_V, \lambda_E, \text{prop}_V, \text{prop}_E) $$
where $\lambda_V, \lambda_E$ are label functions, and $\text{prop}_V, \text{prop}_E$ are property maps. This is the model used by **Neo4j, Amazon Neptune, JanusGraph**.

## 2.2 Layer 2 — Semantic Representation: RDF

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **RDF (Resource Description Framework)** is the W3C standard for representing networked data. Every fact is expressed as a **triple**:
> $$ \text{Triple} = (\text{Subject},\ \text{Predicate},\ \text{Object}) $$

- **Subject** — a resource (URI) or blank node.
- **Predicate** — a property (URI from a vocabulary).
- **Object** — a resource, blank node, or literal value.

A set of triples forms an **RDF graph** $G_{RDF} = (V_R, E_R)$ where the underlying structure is a *directed, labelled multigraph*.

### 2.2.1 RDF Serialization Formats
- **RDF/XML** — verbose, machine-friendly.
- **Turtle (TTL)** — human-readable, KTU-recommended.
- **N-Triples** — line-per-triple, fastest to parse.
- **JSON-LD** — JSON-compatible, ideal for web APIs.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering utility:** RDF underpins **Linked Open Data (LOD)**, the **Schema.org** vocabulary, and **Wikidata** — the largest open knowledge graph with $\sim 100\text{ million}$ entities.

## 2.3 Layer 3 — Ontological Representation

> [!IMPORTANT]
> An **Ontology** in computer science is a *formal, explicit specification of a shared conceptualization* (Gruber, 1993). In SNA, an ontology defines:
> - The **classes** of social entities (Person, Organization, Group).
> - The **properties** that relate them (knows, worksFor, memberOf).
> - The **constraints** (cardinality, transitivity, domain/range).

Ontologies in the SNA domain are typically expressed in:
- **RDF Schema (RDFS)** — lightweight class/property hierarchy.
- **OWL (Web Ontology Language)** — full Description Logic, supports rich reasoning.
- **SKOS** — controlled vocabularies for tagging.

### 2.3.1 Description Logic (DL) Foundations
A DL knowledge base $\mathcal{K} = \langle \mathcal{T}, \mathcal{A} \rangle$ has two parts:
- **TBox $\mathcal{T}$** — terminological axioms (schema-level).
- **ABox $\mathcal{A}$** — assertional axioms (instance-level data).

| Constructor | DL Syntax | Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| Atomic concept | $A$ | A class of individuals |
| Role | $R$ | A binary relation |
| Conjunction | $A \sqcap B$ | Intersection of classes |
| Existential | $\exists R.C$ | "has some R-relation to a C" |
| Universal | $\forall R.C$ | "all R-neighbors are in C" |
| Subsumption | $A \sqsubseteq B$ | A is a subclass of B |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Students often confuse **TBox** (schema) with **ABox** (data). Remember: **TBox = Type**, **ABox = Assertional facts about individuals**.

## 2.4 Ontological Representation of Social Individuals

Social individuals (people, agents, accounts) are represented as **instances** of ontological classes.

### 2.4.1 FOAF (Friend of a Friend)
The de-facto ontology for representing people and their social connections.

**Core FOAF classes:**
- `foaf:Person` — a human being.
- `foaf:Agent` — any actor (broader than Person).
- `foaf:Organization` — a group/company.
- `foaf:Group` — a collection of agents.

**Core FOAF properties:**
- `foaf:name` — full name (literal).
- `foaf:mbox` — email (URI).
- `foaf:depiction` — image URL.
- `foaf:age` — numeric literal.
- `foaf:knows` — binary relationship to another Person.

### 2.4.2 SIOC (Semantically-Interlinked Online Communities)
Designed for **online social platforms** (forums, blogs, microblogs).

**Core SIOC classes:**
- `sioc:User` — an online account.
- `sioc:Post` — a content item.
- `sioc:Forum` — a discussion space.
- `sioc:Container` — generic aggregator.

**Core SIOC properties:**
- `sioc:has_creator` — links Post → User.
- `sioc:content` — the textual body.
- `sioc:link_to` — hyperlinks between posts.
- `sioc:reply_of` — threaded replies.

### 2.4.3 Combined FOAF + SIOC Pattern
A common KTU-exam pattern is the *integration*:

$$ \text{sioc:User} \sqsubseteq \text{foaf:Agent} $$

This allows a single individual to be described by *both* personal attributes (FOAF) and platform behaviour (SIOC).

### 2.4.4 Description Logic Encoding of "Person"
$$ \text{Person} \equiv \text{Agent} \sqcap \exists\,\text{hasName}.\text{string} \sqcap \exists\,\mbox{-}.\top \sqcap \geq 0\,\text{hasFriend}.\text{Person} $$

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuition:** A *Person* is an *Agent* who has at least one name (string), and may or may not have friends. The $\geq 0$ makes friendship *optional* — a fresh user with no friends is still a Person.

## 2.5 Ontological Representation of Social Relationships

### 2.5.1 Binary vs. N-ary Relationships
- **Binary:** $R(u, v)$ — e.g., *knows(Alice, Bob)*.
- **N-ary:** A relationship with additional *contextual* parameters — e.g., *metAt(Alice, Bob, "Conference2024", 2024-03-12)*.

In RDF, n-ary relations are encoded using **reification** or **blank nodes**:
$$ \text{:met\_event rdf:type :MeetingEvent;} $$
$$ \quad\quad \text{:participant :Alice;} $$
$$ \quad\quad \text{:participant :Bob;} $$
$$ \quad\quad \text{:atLocation "Kochi".} $$

### 2.5.2 Relationship Hierarchies (FOAF Example)

| Property | Inverse | Transitive | Symmetric |
|---|---|---|---|
| `foaf:knows` | — | ❌ | ✔ (typical) |
| `foaf:based_near` | — | ✔ | ✔ |
| `rel:friendOf` | `rel:friendOf` | ❌ | ✔ |
| `rel:parentOf` | `rel:childOf` | ❌ | ❌ |
| `rel:ancestorOf` | `rel:descendantOf` | ✔ | ❌ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Point:** If a property is declared **transitive** in OWL, a reasoner will *automatically* infer:
> $$ \text{ancestorOf}(x, y) \land \text{ancestorOf}(y, z) \rightarrow \text{ancestorOf}(x, z) $$

### 2.5.3 Trust and Provenance in Relationships
Modern ontological extensions include:
- `reif:Trust` — a graded trust value $[0, 1]$ on a relationship.
- `prov:wasDerivedFrom` — provenance of an inferred fact.
- `sp:subjectOf` (schema.org) — links entity to its representations.

## 2.6 Aggregating and Reasoning with Social Network Data

### 2.6.1 Aggregation Operations
Aggregation combines many low-level facts into summary statements. Standard operations:

| Operator | Notation | Use |
|---|---|---|
| **Count** | $\#\{x \in S : P(x)\}$ | Number of friends |
| **Sum** | $\sum_{i} w_i$ | Total interaction weight |
| **Mean** | $\bar{w} = \tfrac{1}{n}\sum w_i$ | Average tie strength |
| **Min / Max** | $\min, \max$ | Earliest/latest contact |
| **Group-by** | partition $S$ by key $k$ | Community-level stats |

### 2.6.2 SPARQL — The Query Language
**SPARQL** is the W3C-standard query language for RDF graphs. A SPARQL query has the form:

$$ Q = \langle \text{SELECT}, \text{WHERE}\,\{\,?\text{var}_1\ \text{p}_1\ \text{?var}_2.\ \dots\ \},\ \text{FILTER},\ \text{aggregators} \rangle $$

A **basic graph pattern (BGP)** matches subgraphs in the RDF data.

### 2.6.3 Reasoning — From Data to Knowledge
Reasoning *infers* new triples from existing ones using rules.

**Subsumption reasoning (RDFS):**
$$ A \sqsubseteq B \land x : A \implies x : B $$

**Property chain (OWL 2):**
$$ R_1 \circ R_2 \sqsubseteq R_3 $$

Example: $\text{hasParent} \circ \text{hasBrother} \sqsubseteq \text{hasUncle}$

**Transitivity:**
$$ R \circ R \sqsubseteq R $$

**Inverse:**
$$ R \sqsubseteq S^{-} \implies R(x,y) \leftrightarrow S(y,x) $$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering utility:** A reasoner (e.g., **Pellet, HermiT, FaCT++**) computes the *closure* of an RDF graph w.r.t. an ontology. This is the engine behind intelligent chatbots, semantic search, and Linked Data portals.

### 2.6.4 Centrality-Aware Aggregation
Network metrics can be expressed as aggregations:

| Metric | Formula |
|---|---|
| **Degree Centrality** | $C_D(v) = \deg(v)$ |
| **Weighted Degree** | $C_D^w(v) = \sum_{u \in N(v)} w(u,v)$ |
| **Clustering Coefficient** | $C(v) = \tfrac{2 T(v)}{\deg(v)(\deg(v)-1)}$ |
| **PageRank** | $PR(v) = \tfrac{1-d}{N} + d \sum_{u \to v} \tfrac{PR(u)}{L(u)}$ |

## 2.7 Advanced Representations

### 2.7.1 Multi-Layer / Multi-Modal Networks
A network is generalised to $\mathcal{M} = (V_M, E_M, V, E)$ with:
- $V_M$ — set of *layers* (e.g., Facebook, Twitter, LinkedIn).
- $E_M$ — *inter-layer* edges.

### 2.7.2 Temporal Networks
Edges carry a **time function** $\tau : E \to \mathbb{R}_{\geq 0}$. Useful for event-driven SNA (calls, messages, transactions).

### 2.7.3 Heterogeneous Information Networks (HIN)
A HIN has multiple node types $\mathcal{A} = \{A_1, A_2, \dots, A_d\}$ and edge types $\mathcal{R} = \{R_1, \dots, R_m\}$. Schema defined by a **meta-path** $\mathcal{P} : A_1 \xrightarrow{R_1} A_2 \xrightarrow{R_2} \dots \xrightarrow{R_l} A_{l+1}$.

### 2.7.4 Knowledge Graphs
$K = (V, E, L, T)$ where $L$ are *entity types* and $T$ are *relation types*. KGs unify L1+L2+L3.

### 2.7.5 Hypergraphs
An edge $e \in E$ can connect an *arbitrary subset* of vertices: $e \subseteq V$, $\vert e \vert \geq 2$. Models group interactions (e.g., a research group).

### 2.7.6 Probabilistic Soft Logic (PSL)
A *probabilistic* extension of first-order logic with continuous truth values in $[0,1]$. Rules have associated weights $w_r$, and inference maximises the probability of the most likely world.

### 2.7.7 Tensor Representations
A 3-mode tensor $\mathcal{T} \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times n \times k}$ stores $k$ relation types between $n$ nodes. Slices $\mathcal{T}_{\cdot, \cdot, k}$ are per-relation adjacency matrices. **RESCAL** and **TuckER** are common tensor-factorisation models for KGs.

---

### 📌 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet (High-Yield)

| # | Concept | Formula / Rule | Notation |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Adjacency matrix entry | $A_{ij} = 1$ if $(i,j)\in E$, else $0$ | $A \in \{0,1\}^{n\times n}$ |
| 2 | Edge list cardinality | $\vert E \vert = m$ | $m \leq \binom{n}{2}$ |
| 3 | RDF triple | $(s, p, o)$ | subject/predicate/object |
| 4 | OWL subsumption | $A \sqsubseteq B$ | class hierarchy |
| 5 | OWL transitivity | $R \circ R \sqsubseteq R$ | $\text{ancestorOf}$ |
| 6 | Property chain | $R_1 \circ R_2 \sqsubseteq R_3$ | $\text{hasUncle}$ |
| 7 | Inverse property | $R \equiv S^{-}$ | $\text{parentOf}^{-1} = \text{childOf}$ |
| 8 | Degree centrality | $C_D(v) = \deg(v)$ | unweighted |
| 9 | Weighted degree | $C_D^w(v) = \sum_{u} w_{uv}$ | weighted graph |
| 10 | Clustering coefficient | $C(v) = \tfrac{2T(v)}{\deg(v)(\deg(v)-1)}$ | local density |
| 11 | PageRank | $PR(v) = \tfrac{1-d}{N} + d\sum_{u\to v}\tfrac{PR(u)}{L(u)}$ | damping $d \approx 0.85$ |
| 12 | HIN meta-path | $A_1 \xrightarrow{R_1}\dots\xrightarrow{R_l} A_{l+1}$ | composite relation |
| 13 | Hyperedge | $e \subseteq V,\ \vert e \vert \geq 2$ | n-ary |
| 14 | RESCAL scoring | $f_r(h,t) = h^\top M_r t$ | $h,t \in \mathbb{R}^d$, $M_r \in \mathbb{R}^{d\times d}$ |
| 15 | SPARQL pattern | $?s\ p\ ?o.\ \text{FILTER}(\cdot)$ | BGP matching |
| 16 | FOAF | `foaf:Person`, `foaf:knows` | W3C standard |
| 17 | SIOC | `sioc:User`, `sioc:Post`, `sioc:has_creator` | W3C standard |
| 18 | TBox vs ABox | $\mathcal{T}$ (schema) vs $\mathcal{A}$ (data) | DL partition |
| 19 | Description Logic | $\exists R.C$, $\forall R.C$ | restricted FOL |
| 20 | PSL rule weight | $w_r \in \mathbb{R}$ | continuous truth $[0,1]$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-world engineering utility:** These representations power **Graph Neural Networks (GNNs)** such as **R-GCN, CompGCN, TransE, DistMult** for link prediction, fraud detection, and recommendation.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — From Adjacency Matrix to Weighted Representation

**Given:** An undirected graph $G = (V, E)$ with $n = \vert V \vert$ and tie weights $w : E \to \mathbb{R}_{\geq 0}$.

**Goal:** Derive the *weighted degree* of a vertex $v_i$ and the *symmetry property* of the matrix.

**Step 1 — Define the weighted adjacency matrix $W$.**

$$ W_{ij} = \begin{cases} w(v_i, v_j) & \text{if } (v_i, v_j) \in E \\ 0 & \text{otherwise} \end{cases} $$

*Logic:* Each cell holds either the edge weight (if the edge exists) or zero (no edge). This is the natural extension of the binary $A_{ij}$.

**Step 2 — Enforce symmetry for undirected graphs.**

For all $i, j$:

$$ W_{ij} = W_{ji} $$

*Logic:* An undirected edge between $v_i$ and $v_j$ has the *same* weight from either direction. This makes $W$ a **symmetric** matrix, with all real eigenvalues and orthogonal eigenvectors.

**Step 3 — Derive the weighted degree of $v_i$.**

The degree is the sum of all incident weights:

$$ d^w(v_i) \;=\; \sum_{j=1}^{n} W_{ij} $$

*Logic:* Sum the $i$-th row of $W$ to total all weighted ties incident to $v_i$.

**Step 4 — Vectorise using the all-ones vector $\mathbf{1}$.**

$$ \mathbf{d}^w \;=\; W\,\mathbf{1} $$

*Logic:* Multiplying $W$ by a column vector of ones sums each row, giving a column vector of weighted degrees.

**Step 5 — Normalise to obtain weighted degree centrality.**

$$ C_D^w(v_i) \;=\; \frac{d^w(v_i)}{n - 1} $$

*Logic:* Divide by the maximum possible degree ($n-1$ in a simple graph) to get a value in $[0, 1]$. This is comparable across networks of different sizes.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — OWL Property Chain and Transitive Reasoning

**Given:** OWL 2 axioms:
$$ \text{hasParent} \circ \text{hasBrother} \sqsubseteq \text{hasUncle} $$
$$ \text{hasParent} \sqsubseteq \text{hasAncestor} \quad \text{(transitive closure)} $$

Plus facts:
$$ \text{hasParent}(\text{John},\ \text{Mary}),\quad \text{hasBrother}(\text{Mary},\ \text{Tom}) $$

**Goal:** Prove that the reasoner *infers* $\text{hasUncle}(\text{John},\ \text{Tom})$.

**Step 1 — Apply the property-chain axiom.**

The chain $R_1 \circ R_2 \sqsubseteq R_3$ states:

$$ R_1(x, y) \land R_2(y, z) \rightarrow R_3(x, z) $$

*Logic:* If $x$ has $R_1$ to $y$, and $y$ has $R_2$ to $z$, then $x$ has $R_3$ to $z$.

**Step 2 — Instantiate with our facts.**

Let $R_1 = \text{hasParent}$, $R_2 = \text{hasBrother}$, $R_3 = \text{hasUncle}$, $x = \text{John}$, $y = \text{Mary}$, $z = \text{Tom}$.

$$ \text{hasParent}(\text{John},\ \text{Mary}) \land \text{hasBrother}(\text{Mary},\ \text{Tom}) $$

**Step 3 — Apply modus ponens via the rule.**

$$ \therefore \text{hasUncle}(\text{John},\ \text{Tom}) $$

**Step 4 — Add transitivity for ancestors.**

If we additionally have $\text{hasParent}(\text{Mary},\ \text{Liz})$ and $\text{hasParent}(\text{Liz},\ \text{Sara})$:

$$ \text{hasAncestor}(\text{John},\ \text{Mary}) \land \text{hasAncestor}(\text{Mary},\ \text{Liz}) \rightarrow \text{hasAncestor}(\text{John},\ \text{Liz}) $$

Iterating:

$$ \text{hasAncestor}(\text{John},\ \text{Sara}) $$

**Step 5 — Generalise.** Any $k$-step path in the $\text{hasAncestor}$ relation is reachable in $O(k)$ reasoning steps.

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — SPARQL Query for "Friends of Friends"

**Given:** An RDF graph with triples of the form $\langle \text{?p},\ \text{foaf:knows},\ \text{?q} \rangle$.

**Goal:** Write a SPARQL query that returns all *2-hop friends* of `:Alice`.

**Step 1 — Identify the basic graph pattern.**

We need two consecutive `foaf:knows` edges:

$$ \text{?a foaf:knows ?b.} \quad \text{?b foaf:knows ?c.} $$

**Step 2 — Bind the source to `:Alice`.**

$$ \text{BIND(:Alice AS ?a)} \quad \text{or directly use the constant} $$

**Step 3 — Eliminate trivial paths.**

We must exclude the *direct friends* ($?c = ?a$ is impossible since $?a = \text{Alice}$ is excluded by BIND) and *Alice herself*:

$$ \text{FILTER(?c != :Alice)} $$

**Step 4 — Compose the full SPARQL.**

```sparql
PREFIX foaf: <http://xmlns.com/foaf/0.1/>
SELECT DISTINCT ?c WHERE {
    :Alice foaf:knows ?b .
    ?b    foaf:knows ?c .
    FILTER (?c != :Alice)
}
```

**Step 5 — Add aggregation for counting.**

```sparql
SELECT ?c (COUNT(DISTINCT ?b) AS ?mutualCount) WHERE {
    :Alice foaf:knows ?b .
    ?b    foaf:knows ?c .
    FILTER (?c != :Alice)
} GROUP BY ?c
ORDER BY DESC(?mutualCount)
```

*Logic:* This ranks potential friends by the **number of mutual friends** — the basis of *collaborative filtering* and *friend recommendation*.

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Meta-Path Similarity in HIN

**Given:** A Heterogeneous Information Network with node types *Author (A), Paper (P), Venue (V)* and the meta-path:

$$ \mathcal{P}: A \xrightarrow{\text{writes}} P \xrightarrow{\text{writtenBy}} A \xrightarrow{\text{writes}} P \xrightarrow{\text{publishedIn}} V $$

**Goal:** Compute the *PathCount* similarity between two authors $a_1, a_2$ w.r.t. venue $v$.

**Step 1 — Define the PathCount metric.**

$$ s(a_1, a_2) = \sum_{p \in \text{Papers}} \sum_{v \in \text{Venues}} \mathbb{1}\big[(a_1, p, a_2, p', v) \in \mathcal{P}\big] $$

*Logic:* Count the number of distinct *path instances* connecting $a_1$ to $a_2$ through the meta-path.

**Step 2 — Convert to adjacency tensor products.**

Let $M_{AP}$, $M_{PA}$, $M_{PV}$ be the biadjacency matrices.

$$ S = M_{AP}\,M_{PA}\,M_{AP}\,M_{PV} $$

*Logic:* Each multiplication follows one meta-path hop, and the resulting matrix $S$ has entries $S_{a_1,v}$ counting co-authorship reachability to venue $v$.

**Step 3 — Normalise to PathSim.**

$$ \text{PathSim}(a_1, a_2) = \frac{2 \cdot S_{a_1, a_2}}{S_{a_1, a_1} + S_{a_2, a_2}} $$

*Logic:* Symmetric, in $[0, 1]$, and rewards authors that share many venues *proportionally* to their own productivity.

## 3.5 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| Dimension | Edge List | Adj. Matrix | RDF | OWL Ontology | KG Embedding |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Storage** | $O(\vert E\vert)$ | $O(\vert V\vert^2)$ | $O(\vert T\vert)$ | $O(\vert T\vert)$ | $O(\vert V\vert \cdot d)$ |
| **Query** | Linear scan | $O(1)$ lookup | SPARQL | SPARQL + DL | Vector similarity |
| **Reasoning** | None | None | RDFS | OWL-DL | Implicit (transE) |
| **Schema** | No | No | Optional | Yes (TBox) | Learned |
| **Heterogeneity** | No | No | Yes | Yes | Yes (via relations) |
| **Scalability** | High | Low | Medium | Medium | High |
| **Standardisation** | Ad-hoc | Ad-hoc | W3C | W3C | Research |
| **Use Case** | Toy graphs | Dense graphs | Linked Data | Expert systems | ML pipelines |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Layered Architecture of Network Data Representation

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    L0[Real World Social Phenomena]:::root
    L1[L1 Syntactic Layer<br/>Edge List / Adj Matrix / Adj List]:::l1
    L2[L2 Semantic Layer<br/>RDF Triples Property Graphs]:::l2
    L3[L3 Ontological Layer<br/>RDFS / OWL / SKOS]:::l3
    L4[Aggregation and Reasoning<br/>SPARQL Description Logic]:::l4
    L5[Advanced Representations<br/>Knowledge Graphs Hypergraphs Temporal Nets]:::l5

    L0 --> L1
    L1 --> L2
    L2 --> L3
    L3 --> L4
    L4 --> L5

    classDef root fill:#1a1a1a,color:#fff,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px
    classDef l1 fill:#cfe2ff,color:#000,stroke:#0d6efd

    classDef l4 fill:#f8d7da,color:#000,stroke:#dc3545
    classDef l5 fill:#e2d9f3,color:#000,stroke:#6f42c1
```

## 4.2 FOAF + SIOC Integration Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    Person[foaf:Person]:::foaf
    Agent[foaf:Agent]:::foaf
    Org[foaf:Organization]:::foaf
    User[sioc:User]:::sioc
    Post[sioc:Post]:::sioc
    Forum[sioc:Forum]:::sioc
    Account[foaf:OnlineAccount]:::foaf

    Person -->|rdf:type| Agent
    User -->|rdf:type| Account
    Account -->|rdf:type| Agent
    Post -->|sioc:has_creator| User
    User -->|sioc:member_of| Forum
    Person -->|foaf:member| Org
    Person -->|foaf:knows| Person
    User -->|foaf:knows| User

    classDef foaf fill:#cfe2ff,stroke:#0d6efd,color:#000
    classDef sioc fill:#fff3cd,stroke:#ffc107,color:#000
```

## 4.3 OWL Reasoning Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1[ABox Data: John hasParent Mary]:::data
    A2[ABox Data: Mary hasBrother Tom]:::data
    T1[TBox: hasParent o hasBrother subsumes hasUncle]:::tbox
    T2[TBox: hasUncle subsumes hasRelative]:::tbox
    R1[Reasoner Pellet / HermiT]:::engine
    O1[Inferred: John hasUncle Tom]:::output
    O2[Inferred: John hasRelative Tom]:::output

    A1 --> R1
    A2 --> R1
    T1 --> R1
    T2 --> R1
    R1 --> O1
    R1 --> O2
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Question 1, 3 Marks]**
> **Q.** Define *RDF triple* and illustrate with a real-world example from the FOAF ontology.

**Model Answer:**
An **RDF triple** is a 3-tuple $(s, p, o)$ representing a single fact: a *subject* $s$ linked to an *object* $o$ by a *predicate* $p$.
*Example:*
$$ (\text{:Alice},\ \text{foaf:knows},\ \text{:Bob}) $$
states that the resource `:Alice` is related to `:Bob` via the FOAF property `foaf:knows`. Formally, $s, p \in \mathcal{U}$ (URIs) and $o \in \mathcal{U} \cup \mathcal{L} \cup \mathcal{B}$ (URI, literal, or blank node). RDF is the syntactic foundation of the Semantic Web and the building block of every knowledge graph. **(3 marks)**

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Question 2, 3 Marks]**
> **Q.** Differentiate between the **TBox** and the **ABox** of a Description Logic knowledge base.

**Model Answer:**
- The **TBox ($\mathcal{T}$)** contains *terminological* (schema-level) axioms: class definitions, subsumptions ($A \sqsubseteq B$), and property characteristics. It defines the *vocabulary* of the domain.
- The **ABox ($\mathcal{A}$)** contains *assertional* (instance-level) axioms: $C(a)$ for class membership and $R(a, b)$ for relations between individuals. It contains the *actual data*.
*Example:* `:Student ⊆ :Person` is in $\mathcal{T}$; `:Person(John)` is in $\mathcal{A}$. **(3 marks)**

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

### ❓ QUESTION A (14 Marks) — Choice 1

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Long Answer, 14 Marks]**
> **(a)** Explain the layered architecture of network data representation. Discuss the syntactic, semantic, and ontological layers with suitable examples. **(7 marks)**
> **(b)** Describe the FOAF and SIOC ontologies. Show how a single individual can be modelled jointly using both vocabularies with a clear diagram. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**Layered Architecture of Network Data Representation** (7 marks)

**L1 — Syntactic Layer:**
- Stores only the *raw graph*: $(V, E)$.
- Forms: edge list, adjacency matrix $A \in \{0,1\}^{n\times n}$, adjacency list.
- *Example:* Alice–Bob, Bob–Carol, Alice–Carol as a 3-row/3-col matrix with 1's at the symmetric positions.
- **Limitation:** No semantics — a "1" doesn't tell us *what kind* of tie it is.

**L2 — Semantic Layer (RDF):**
- Introduces *labelled* edges using RDF triples $(s, p, o)$.
- Vocabularies are URI-based, enabling **global uniqueness** and **interoperability**.
- *Example:* `(:Alice, foaf:knows, :Bob)` says Alice *knows* Bob — the tie has a *type*.

**L3 — Ontological Layer (RDFS / OWL):**
- Defines **schema**: classes, property hierarchies, constraints.
- Enables **automated reasoning** (subsumption, transitivity, equivalence).
- *Example:* Declaring `foaf:knows` as **symmetric** allows the reasoner to infer `(:Bob, foaf:knows, :Alice)` from the original triple.

**Conclusion:** The L1 → L2 → L3 progression takes us from *data* to *information* to *knowledge*, the classic DIKW hierarchy applied to SNA.

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**FOAF and SIOC Ontologies — Joint Modelling** (7 marks)

- **FOAF** is a W3C ontology for describing *people*, their *attributes* (name, age, email), and their *personal* relationships (`foaf:knows`).
- **SIOC** is a W3C ontology for describing *online communities* — users, posts, forums, threaded discussions.

**Joint Model (Description Logic Form):**

$$ \text{sioc:User} \sqsubseteq \text{foaf:OnlineAccount} \sqsubseteq \text{foaf:Agent} $$

This states that a SIOC *User* is a kind of *Online Account*, which is itself a kind of *Agent* — establishing a clean subsumption hierarchy.

**Example Individual (`:alice`):**
- `:alice rdf:type sioc:User` — she is a platform user.
- `:alice foaf:name "Alice Joseph"` — her name (FOAF).
- `:alice foaf:mbox <mailto:[email protected]>` — her email.
- `:alice sioc:has_creator [sioc:Post] (p1, p2, …)` — she authored posts.
- `:alice foaf:knows :bob` — her social tie (FOAF).

**Diagram (textual rendition):**

```text
   :alice  ──rdf:type──▶  sioc:User
        │                    │
        │ foaf:name          │ sioc:has_creator
        ▼                    ▼
   "Alice Joseph"        sioc:Post :p1
                              │
                              │ sioc:content
                              ▼
                       "Hello world!"
```

**Advantages of joint modelling:** (i) Reuses *both* vocabularies without conflict; (ii) Enables platform-aware reasoning (e.g., post counts, reply graphs); (iii) Standard-compliant and Linked-Data ready.

---

### ❓ QUESTION B (14 Marks) — Choice 2 (Module Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Long Answer, 14 Marks]**
> **(a)** What is **SPARQL**? Write a SPARQL query to find the *top-5 most-connected individuals* in an FOAF dataset. Explain the reasoning and aggregation. **(7 marks)**
> **(b)** Discuss the role of **transitive property reasoning** and **property chains** in OWL with two real-world social-network examples. Show the inferred triples. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**SPARQL — Definition:**
**SPARQL Protocol and RDF Query Language** is the W3C-standard declarative language for querying RDF graphs. A query specifies a **basic graph pattern (BGP)** that is matched against the data; variables prefixed with `?` are returned.

**Query — Top-5 most-connected individuals:**

```sparql
PREFIX foaf: <http://xmlns.com/foaf/0.1/>

SELECT ?person (COUNT(DISTINCT ?friend) AS ?degree) WHERE {
    ?person foaf:knows ?friend .
}
GROUP BY ?person
ORDER BY DESC(?degree)
LIMIT 5
```

**Explanation:**
1. The BGP `?person foaf:knows ?friend .` matches *every* knows-edge in the dataset.
2. `GROUP BY ?person` partitions the matches by the source vertex.
3. `COUNT(DISTINCT ?friend)` is an **aggregation operator** that returns the *degree* of each person.
4. `ORDER BY DESC` ranks in descending order of degree.
5. `LIMIT 5` restricts the output to the top 5.

**Reasoning enhancement:** If `foaf:knows` is declared **symmetric** in the ontology, a reasoner will pre-compute the closure so that *every* A-knows-B also yields B-knows-A, ensuring degree counts are correct. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Transitive Property Reasoning in OWL — Two Examples** (7 marks)

**Example 1 — `ancestorOf`:**
- Axiom: $\text{ancestorOf} \circ \text{ancestorOf} \sqsubseteq \text{ancestorOf}$ (declaration of transitivity).
- Facts: $\text{ancestorOf}(\text{Adam},\ \text{Bob})$, $\text{ancestorOf}(\text{Bob},\ \text{Cara})$.
- *Inferred:* $\text{ancestorOf}(\text{Adam},\ \text{Cara})$.
- **Use case:** Genealogy networks, family-tree reconstruction.

**Example 2 — `manages` chain to `seniorManager`:**
- Axiom: $\text{manages} \circ \text{manages} \sqsubseteq \text{seniorManager}$.
- Facts: $\text{manages}(\text{John},\ \text{Liz})$, $\text{manages}(\text{Liz},\ \text{Sara})$.
- *Inferred:* $\text{seniorManager}(\text{John},\ \text{Sara})$.
- **Use case:** Corporate-hierarchy analytics in LinkedIn-style professional networks.

**Algorithmic note:** Pellet, HermiT, and FaCT++ implement *tableau-based* reasoning which is worst-case **EXPTIME** in the worst case for $\mathcal{SHOIN}(\mathbf{D})$ (OWL-DL). For practical SNA, transitive closures are pre-computed lazily, and incremental reasoners (e.g., **Pellet-Inc**) update only the *delta* on new data. **(7 marks)**

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common mark-losing mistakes (Module 2):**
> 1. **Confusing TBox with ABox** — losing 1 mark for each. Always label: *terminology* vs *assertion*.
> 2. **Forgetting the `FILTER` clause** in SPARQL — your query will return the source person themselves, inflating counts. Always `FILTER(?other != :me)`.
> 3. **Treating FOAF and SIOC as competing** — they are *complementary*. FOAF describes *who*; SIOC describes *what they post*.
> 4. **Not specifying transitivity / symmetry** in OWL — without the OWL declaration, the reasoner *will not* infer closure. Simply writing `foaf:knows` is not enough; declare it `owl:SymmetricProperty`.
> 5. **Using `|` inside markdown tables** for absolute value — this breaks the table. Use `\vert x \vert` in any document you submit.
> 6. **Mixing up the meta-path direction** in HIN — the schema must respect the *direction* of the relation (e.g., writes vs writtenBy are inverses).
> 7. **Omitting the damping factor $d$** in PageRank — examiners deduct a mark if $d$ is unspecified. The standard value is $d = 0.85$.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Module 2 — One-Page Rapid Recap**

- ✅ **Three layers** of representation: **Syntactic (L1) → Semantic RDF (L2) → Ontological OWL (L3)**.
- ✅ **RDF triple** = $(s, p, o)$; **SPARQL** is the W3C query language.
- ✅ **TBox** = schema, **ABox** = data. Never confuse.
- ✅ **FOAF** = Person-centric (name, knows, mbox). **SIOC** = Platform-centric (User, Post, Forum).
- ✅ **OWL supports**: subsumption ($\sqsubseteq$), transitivity ($R \circ R \sqsubseteq R$), symmetry, inverses, property chains.
- ✅ **Reasoners** (Pellet, HermiT, FaCT++) compute *logical closure* — they infer new triples automatically.
- ✅ **Aggregation** = COUNT, SUM, AVG, MIN, MAX, GROUP BY, ORDER BY.
- ✅ **SPARQL FILTER** is mandatory to exclude trivial paths.
- ✅ **Property chain** example: `hasParent ∘ hasBrother ⊑ hasUncle`.
- ✅ **Advanced representations**: Multi-layer, Temporal, HIN (meta-paths), Knowledge Graphs, Hypergraphs, PSL, Tensors (RESCAL).
- ✅ **PageRank** uses damping $d = 0.85$; $PR(v) = \tfrac{1-d}{N} + d \sum_{u \to v} \tfrac{PR(u)}{L(u)}$.
- ✅ **HIN PathSim** = $\tfrac{2 S_{a_1 a_2}}{S_{a_1 a_1} + S_{a_2 a_2}}$ — symmetric, bounded $[0,1]$.
- ✅ **KG embeddings** (TransE, DistMult, RESCAL) score triples via $f(h, r, t)$.
- ✅ **Weighted degree** = $d^w(v_i) = \sum_j W_{ij} = (W \mathbf{1})_i$.
- ✅ **Industry adoption**: Google Knowledge Graph, Facebook Open Graph, LinkedIn Economic Graph, Wikidata.
- ✅ **W3C standards** to memorise: **RDF, RDFS, OWL, SPARQL, FOAF, SIOC, SKOS, PROV-O**.

> **End of Module 2 Notes — KTU 2024 Scheme (PECMT528)**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Extraction and Mining Communities in Web Social Networks - Extracting Evolution of Web Community from a Series of Web Archive
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECMT528 - CONCEPTS IN SOCIAL NETWORK ANALYSIS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Extraction and Mining Communities in Web Social Networks - Extracting Evolution of Web Community from a Series of Web Archive

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Web Community:** A web community is a collection of web pages that are densely interconnected and revolve around a common topic of interest, identified through hyperlink analysis on a Web graph $G = (V, E)$, where $V$ represents the set of web pages (nodes) and $E$ represents the set of hyperlinks (edges).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Web Community Evolution:** The temporal transformation of web communities observed across a sequence of web archive snapshots. Formally, given a series of web graphs $G_1, G_2, \ldots, G_T$ captured at timestamps $t_1, t_2, \ldots, t_T$, community evolution refers to the identification, tracking, and characterization of structural and topical changes in communities $C_i(t_k)$ as $t_k$ progresses.

> [!NOTE]
> **Web Archive:** A curated, time-stamped repository that stores snapshots of the World Wide Web at discrete intervals. Examples include the **Internet Archive's Wayback Machine**, national digital archives, and institutional web crawlers that preserve pages for longitudinal analysis.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a bustling **neighborhood in a city** that changes over decades:

- In **1950**, this neighborhood had a textile mill, a school, and homes for workers — a *close-knit community*.
- By **1980**, the mill closed; a tech park appeared. Some old families stayed, new ones moved in, and the community's *character completely transformed*.
- In **2010**, the school merged with another, and the neighborhood was absorbed into a larger district.

Now, if a researcher had **photographs** of the city taken at different times (similar to a web archive), they could:
1. **Identify** the neighborhood in each photograph (community detection)
2. **Compare** photos to see what emerged, vanished, merged, or split (evolution tracking)
3. **Map** the long-term story of that community (community evolution mining)

The **web community evolution problem** is precisely this — but applied to a digital world where "photographs" are snapshots of the web, and "neighborhoods" are clusters of related, densely-linked web pages.

## 1.3 Key Terminology (KTU High-Yield Vocabulary)

| Term | Definition |
|------|------------|
| **Snapshot Graph $G_t$** | Web graph constructed from a single crawl at time $t$ |
| **Community Instance $C_i(t)$** | A specific community $i$ detected at time $t$ |
| **Correspondence Problem** | The challenge of matching $C_i(t)$ with $C_j(t+1)$ to track its lineage |
| **Evolution Event** | A discrete transformation (birth, death, merge, split, growth, shrink) |
| **Jaccard Coefficient** | Standard measure of set similarity between two communities |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why is this important for KTU exams?** This topic tests the student's ability to integrate graph theory, time-series analysis, and set theory — three pillars of modern data mining. Questions typically appear in Part B (14-mark) format, requiring both conceptual clarity and mathematical formulation.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Conceptual Framework

The extraction and mining of community evolution from web archives involves a **multi-stage pipeline**. Each stage addresses a specific sub-problem:

### Stage 1: Web Archive Collection
- A web archive is built by repeatedly crawling the web at fixed intervals $\Delta t$.
- Each crawl produces a *crawled subgraph* $G_t = (V_t, E_t)$ approximating the web at time $t$.
- **Web communities are dynamic entities** — pages appear, vanish, and hyperlinks are rewired constantly.

### Stage 2: Per-Snapshot Community Detection
- Apply a community detection algorithm (e.g., **HITS**, **Max-Flow Min-Cut**, **Betweenness Centrality** based methods, or **modularity-based** methods) independently on each $G_t$.
- Output: a set of communities $\{C_1(t), C_2(t), \ldots, C_{k_t}(t)\}$ for each timestamp $t$.
- The number of communities $k_t$ is itself a function of time and may vary between snapshots.

### Stage 3: Inter-Snapshot Community Matching
- For each pair of consecutive snapshots $(t, t+1)$, find the correspondence between $C_i(t)$ and $C_j(t+1)$.
- This is solved by **set-similarity** metrics over the constituent pages of the two communities.
- A *correspondence matrix* $M$ is constructed where $M_{ij} = \text{sim}(C_i(t), C_j(t+1))$.

### Stage 4: Evolution Event Identification
- Based on the correspondence matrix, classify each community's trajectory into one of several **evolutionary events**.

## 2.2 The Six Canonical Evolution Events

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU 2024 syllabus explicitly highlights six evolution events. Memorize these:

| Event | Symbol | Condition |
|-------|--------|-----------|
| **Birth** | $\mathcal{B}$ | $C(t) = \emptyset$ and $C(t+1) \neq \emptyset$ |
| **Death** | $\mathcal{D}$ | $C(t) \neq \emptyset$ and $C(t+1) = \emptyset$ |
| **Growth** | $\mathcal{G}$ | $C(t) \subset C(t+1)$ and $\vert C(t+1) \vert > \vert C(t) \vert$ |
| **Contraction (Shrink)** | $\mathcal{S}$ | $C(t+1) \subset C(t)$ and $\vert C(t+1) \vert < \vert C(t) \vert$ |
| **Merge** | $\mathcal{M}$ | Multiple communities at $t$ combine into one at $t+1$ |
| **Split** | $\mathcal{P}$ | One community at $t$ separates into multiple at $t+1$ |
| **Continuation (Stable)** | $\mathcal{C}$ | $C(t) \approx C(t+1)$ with high overlap |

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> All formulas are mathematically rigorous and directly relevant to KTU numerical problems.

| # | Formula | Description | Notation |
|---|---------|-------------|----------|
| 1 | $J(C_i, C_j) = \frac{\vert C_i \cap C_j \vert}{\vert C_i \cup C_j \vert}$ | **Jaccard Similarity Coefficient** | $0 \le J \le 1$ |
| 2 | $\text{Cont}(C_i, C_j) = \frac{\vert C_i \cap C_j \vert}{\vert C_i \vert}$ | **Containment of $C_j$ in $C_i$** | $0 \le \text{Cont} \le 1$ |
| 3 | $S(C_i, C_j) = \frac{\vert C_i \cap C_j \vert}{\min(\vert C_i \vert, \vert C_j \vert)}$ | **Overlap Coefficient** | $0 \le S \le 1$ |
| 4 | $M(t, t+1) = [m_{ij}]_{k_t \times k_{t+1}}$ | **Correspondence Matrix** | $m_{ij} = \text{sim}(C_i(t), C_j(t+1))$ |
| 5 | $\mu(C) = \frac{2 \vert E_{\text{internal}} \vert}{\vert C \vert (\vert C \vert - 1)}$ | **Community Density** | Used in cohesion |
| 6 | $\Delta_t = \frac{1}{k_t} \sum_{i=1}^{k_t} \text{sim}(C_i(t), C_i^*(t+1))$ | **Average Stability Index** | Measures evolution smoothness |
| 7 | $B(t) = \sum_{j=1}^{k_{t+1}} \mathbb{1}[C_j(t+1) = \emptyset \text{ in } t]$ | **Number of Births at $t$** | Event count |
| 8 | $D(t) = \sum_{i=1}^{k_t} \mathbb{1}[C_i(t) = \emptyset \text{ in } t+1]$ | **Number of Deaths at $t$** | Event count |

> **CRITICAL FORMATTING NOTE:** In exam answers, write set cardinality as $\vert C \vert$ (using LaTeX `\vert` or `\mid`) — never as $|C|$ which can cause parsing errors in technical renderers.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Application Domain | Use of Web Community Evolution |
|--------------------|-------------------------------|
| **Search Engine Quality** | Google, Bing use temporal community signals to detect "fresh" content clusters and rank pages by topical authority. |
| **Trend Forecasting** | Marketing teams track how online communities around products evolve to predict viral adoption curves. |
| **Cybersecurity** | Identifying the birth of dark-web or extremist communities from web archives aids in early threat detection. |
| **Digital Humanities** | Historians reconstruct the emergence and decline of online discourse around historical events. |
| **Recommendation Systems** | Tracking the evolution of user interest communities enables time-aware collaborative filtering. |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **Continuity Principle** (key exam point): Most real-world communities do not change drastically between consecutive snapshots. Therefore, a community at time $t$ should find a high-similarity "descendant" at $t+1$. This principle is the foundation of all correspondence algorithms.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Formal Problem Formulation

Let the web archive consist of $T$ snapshots:

$$ \mathcal{A} = \{G_1, G_2, \ldots, G_T\} $$

where each $G_t = (V_t, E_t)$ is the web graph at time $t$, with $V_t$ being the set of crawled pages and $E_t$ being the set of hyperlinks between them.

After community detection, the archive yields a series of community sets:

$$ \mathcal{C}_t = \{C_1(t), C_2(t), \ldots, C_{k_t}(t)\} \quad \forall t \in \{1, 2, \ldots, T\} $$

**Goal:** Construct an *evolution graph* $H$ whose nodes are community instances $(C_i, t)$ and whose edges represent valid correspondences between $C_i(t)$ and $C_j(t+1)$, labeled with the appropriate evolution event.

## 3.2 Derivation: Optimal Correspondence via Hungarian-style Matching

To match communities between $C_i(t)$ and $C_j(t+1)$, we need to choose, for each $C_i(t)$, the *best* candidate in $t+1$.

**Step 1: Compute the Jaccard similarity matrix.**

For $i = 1, 2, \ldots, k_t$ and $j = 1, 2, \ldots, k_{t+1}$:

$$ m_{ij} = J(C_i(t), C_j(t+1)) = \frac{\vert C_i(t) \cap C_j(t+1) \vert}{\vert C_i(t) \cup C_j(t+1) \vert} $$

**Step 2: Apply a matching strategy.** Two standard strategies exist:

**Strategy A (Greedy / Threshold-based):** Match $C_i(t)$ to $C_{j^*}(t+1)$ if

$$ j^* = \arg\max_{j} \, m_{ij} \quad \text{and} \quad m_{ij^*} \ge \tau $$

where $\tau$ is a similarity threshold (typically $\tau = 0.5$).

**Strategy B (Bipartite Maximum Weight Matching):** Solve an optimization problem:

$$ \mathcal{M}^* = \arg\max_{\mathcal{M}} \sum_{(i, j) \in \mathcal{M}} m_{ij} $$

subject to a one-to-one matching constraint. This is solved in polynomial time using the **Hungarian Algorithm** in $O(k^3)$ time, where $k = \max(k_t, k_{t+1})$.

**Step 3: Classify the matched pairs by cardinality change.**

Given a match $C_i(t) \leftrightarrow C_j(t+1)$, define:

$$ \delta = \vert C_j(t+1) \vert - \vert C_i(t) \vert $$

and the symmetric difference ratio:

$$ \rho = 1 - J(C_i(t), C_j(t+1)) $$

**Step 4: Event classification rule.**

$$ \text{Event} = \begin{cases} \text{Birth} & \text{if } C_i(t) = \emptyset \\ \text{Death} & \text{if } C_j(t+1) = \emptyset \\ \text{Stable} & \text{if } \delta = 0 \text{ and } \rho \le \epsilon \\ \text{Growth} & \text{if } \delta > 0 \text{ and } \rho \le \epsilon \\ \text{Contraction} & \text{if } \delta < 0 \text{ and } \rho \le \epsilon \\ \text{Drift} & \text{if } \rho > \epsilon \end{cases} $$

where $\epsilon$ is a small drift tolerance (commonly $\epsilon = 0.3$).

**Step 5: Merge and Split detection.**

A *merge* event is detected when multiple $C_i(t)$'s share a common best match $C_j(t+1)$:

$$ \text{Merge} \iff \exists j_0 : \arg\max_{j} m_{i_1, j_0} = \arg\max_{j} m_{i_2, j_0} = j_0 \text{ for } i_1 \neq i_2 $$

Conversely, a *split* event is detected when a single $C_i(t)$ has multiple equally strong matches in $t+1$.

## 3.3 Numerical Worked Example (KTU 14-Mark Style)

**Problem Setup:** Consider three web archive snapshots $G_1, G_2, G_3$ with detected communities:

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_1(1) &= \{A, B, C, D\} \\
C_2(1) &= \{E, F, G\} \\
C_1(2) &= \{A, B, C, D, H\} \\
C_2(2) &= \{E, F, G, I, J\} \\
C_1(3) &= \{A, B, K\} \\
C_2(3) &= \{E, F, G, I, J, L\}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 1: Compute $|C|$ for each community.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\vert C_1(1) \vert &= 4, & \vert C_2(1) \vert &= 3 \\
\vert C_1(2) \vert &= 5, & \vert C_2(2) \vert &= 5 \\
\vert C_1(3) \vert &= 3, & \vert C_2(3) \vert &= 6
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2: Compute correspondence between $t=1$ and $t=2$.**

For $C_1(1) = \{A, B, C, D\}$ and $C_1(2) = \{A, B, C, D, H\}$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_1(1) \cap C_1(2) &= \{A, B, C, D\}, \quad \vert \cdot \vert = 4 \\
C_1(1) \cup C_1(2) &= \{A, B, C, D, H\}, \quad \vert \cdot \vert = 5 \\
J(C_1(1), C_1(2)) &= \frac{4}{5} = 0.8
\end{aligned}
$$

For $C_1(1) = \{A, B, C, D\}$ and $C_2(2) = \{E, F, G, I, J\}$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_1(1) \cap C_2(2) &= \emptyset, \quad \vert \cdot \vert = 0 \\
J(C_1(1), C_2(2)) &= \frac{0}{7} = 0.0
\end{aligned}
$$

For $C_2(1) = \{E, F, G\}$ and $C_1(2) = \{A, B, C, D, H\}$:

$$
J(C_2(1), C_1(2)) = \frac{0}{8} = 0.0
$$

For $C_2(1) = \{E, F, G\}$ and $C_2(2) = \{E, F, G, I, J\}$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_2(1) \cap C_2(2) &= \{E, F, G\}, \quad \vert \cdot \vert = 3 \\
C_2(1) \cup C_2(2) &= \{E, F, G, I, J\}, \quad \vert \cdot \vert = 5 \\
J(C_2(1), C_2(2)) &= \frac{3}{5} = 0.6
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Build the correspondence matrix.**

$$
M(1, 2) = \begin{bmatrix} 0.8 & 0.0 \\ 0.0 & 0.6 \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 4: Classify events (using $\epsilon = 0.3$).**

For the match $C_1(1) \to C_1(2)$:

$$
\delta = 5 - 4 = +1, \quad \rho = 1 - 0.8 = 0.2 \le 0.3
$$

$$\Rightarrow \text{Event} = \mathbf{Growth}$$

For the match $C_2(1) \to C_2(2)$:

$$
\delta = 5 - 3 = +2, \quad \rho = 1 - 0.6 = 0.4 > 0.3
$$

$$\Rightarrow \text{Event} = \mathbf{Drift} \text{ (could also be Growth with relaxed threshold)}$$

**Step 5: Compute correspondence between $t=2$ and $t=3$.**

For $C_1(2) = \{A, B, C, D, H\}$ and $C_1(3) = \{A, B, K\}$:

$$
J = \frac{\vert \{A, B\} \vert}{\vert \{A, B, C, D, H, K\} \vert} = \frac{2}{6} = 0.333
$$

For $C_1(2) = \{A, B, C, D, H\}$ and $C_2(3) = \{E, F, G, I, J, L\}$:

$$
J = \frac{0}{11} = 0
$$

For $C_2(2) = \{E, F, G, I, J\}$ and $C_1(3) = \{A, B, K\}$:

$$
J = \frac{0}{8} = 0
$$

For $C_2(2) = \{E, F, G, I, J\}$ and $C_2(3) = \{E, F, G, I, J, L\}$:

$$
J = \frac{5}{6} = 0.833
$$

**Step 6: Event classification for $t=2 \to t=3$.**

- $C_1(2) \to C_1(3)$: $\rho = 0.667 > 0.3$ → **Drift / Contraction with major membership change**
- $C_1(2)$ to $C_2(3)$: $J = 0$ → no match, $C_1(2)$ is **dead** if no other match exists, and a **new** page $K$ **births** into $C_1(3)$
- $C_2(2) \to C_2(3)$: $\delta = +1$, $\rho = 0.167 \le 0.3$ → **Growth**

## 3.4 Summary of Evolution Story

| Time | Community 1 Status | Community 2 Status |
|------|---------------------|---------------------|
| $t=1$ | Born with 4 pages | Born with 3 pages |
| $t=2$ | **Grows** by adding page $H$ | **Grows/Drifts** by adding $I, J$ |
| $t=3$ | **Contracts** drastically, $C, D, H$ leave, $K$ joins | **Grows** by adding page $L$ |

This example is a **high-yield KTU exam pattern** — practice it until the procedure is automatic.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 End-to-End Pipeline: Web Community Evolution Extraction

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Web Archive Series G1, G2, ..., GT] --> B[Snapshot Preprocessing]
    B --> B1[Page Deduplication]
    B --> B2[URL Canonicalization]
    B --> B3[Content Fingerprinting]
    B1 --> C[Per-Snapshot Graph Construction]
    B2 --> C
    B3 --> C
    C --> D[Community Detection per Snapshot]
    D --> D1[HITS / Spectral / Modularity Methods]
    D1 --> E[Community Set C_t for each t]
    E --> F[Inter-Snapshot Correspondence]
    F --> F1[Compute Jaccard Matrix M]
    F1 --> F2[Apply Hungarian or Greedy Matching]
    F2 --> G[Evolution Event Classification]
    G --> G1[Birth]
    G --> G2[Death]
    G --> G3[Growth]
    G --> G4[Contraction]
    G --> G5[Merge]
    G --> G6[Split]
    G1 --> H[Evolution Graph H]
    G2 --> H
    G3 --> H
    G4 --> H
    G5 --> H
    G6 --> H
    H --> I[Visualization and Analytics]
    I --> I1[Timeline Plots]
    I --> I2[Sankey Diagrams]
    I --> I3[Event Frequency Heatmaps]
```

## 4.2 Evolution Event State Diagram

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Birth
    Birth --> Stable
    Birth --> Growth
    Stable --> Growth
    Stable --> Contraction
    Stable --> Death
    Stable --> Split
    Growth --> Stable
    Growth --> Merge
    Contraction --> Stable
    Contraction --> Death
    Split --> Stable
    Split --> Growth
    Merge --> Stable
    Merge --> Growth
    Stable --> [*]
    Death --> [*]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Interpretation:** Each rectangular state in the diagram represents a *community's status at one timestamp*. Transitions correspond to evolution events. A community instance can only die once (it does not re-emerge under the same identity), but new communities can be born at any time.

## 4.3 Correspondence Matching Architecture (Functional Block)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph InputLayer["Input: Community Sets"]
        Ct["Communities at time t"]
        Ct1["Communities at time t+1"]
    end
    subgraph ComputeLayer["Similarity Computation"]
        S1["Jaccard Calculator"]
        S2["Containment Calculator"]
        S3["Overlap Coefficient"]
    end
    subgraph MatchLayer["Matching Engine"]
        M1["Threshold Filter tau = 0.5"]
        M2["Hungarian Algorithm"]
        M3["Greedy 1-to-Many"]
    end
    subgraph OutputLayer["Event Output"]
        O1["Birth / Death Events"]
        O2["Growth / Contraction"]
        O3["Merge / Split Events"]
    end
    Ct --> S1
    Ct --> S2
    Ct1 --> S1
    Ct1 --> S2
    S1 --> M1
    S2 --> M1
    M1 --> M2
    M2 --> O1
    M2 --> O2
    M2 --> O3
    Ct --> S3
    Ct1 --> S3
    S3 --> M3
    M3 --> O3
```

## 4.4 Mathematical Flow: From Raw Archive to Evolution Insights

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    R1[Raw Web Archive - HTML, Hyperlinks] --> R2[Time-Stamped Crawl Snapshots]
    R2 --> R3[Graph Gt = Vt, Et]
    R3 --> R4[Adjacency Matrix At n_t times n_t]
    R4 --> R5[Apply Community Detection]
    R5 --> R6[Set of Communities Ct]
    R6 --> R7[Compute Jaccard Matrix M]
    R7 --> R8[Threshold Filter]

```
