$\mathcal{P}(C) \geq 0.8$ → **stable / core community** - $0.4 \leq \mathcal{P}(C) < 0.8$ → **transitional community** - $\mathcal{P}(C) < 0.4$ → **ephemeral / transient community** ## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Mermaid Diagram: Multi-Relational Dynamic Network Block Architecture ```mermaid flowchart TB Input["Raw Data Sources
Surveys, Logs, Digital Traces"] Pre1["Pre-processing
Cleaning, Normalisation, Time-alignment"] T1["Tensor Assembly
A in R power N x N x R x T"] T2["Slice Decomposition
Relation-Time Layer Stack"] T3["Supra-Adjacency
S Matrix Construction"] T4["Quality Function
Q multi Modularity"] T5["CP / Tucker
Tensor Decomposition"] T6["Community Partition
C1, C2, ..., Ck"] T7["Characterisation
Persistence, Participation, Dominance"] Output["Dynamic Community Profile"] Input --> Pre1 --> T1 --> T2 --> T3 --> T4 --> T6 T1 --> T5 --> T6 T6 --> T7 --> Output style Input fill:#1f4e79,color:#ffffff style T1 fill:#2e75b6,color:#ffffff style T3 fill:#5b9bd5,color:#ffffff style T4 fill:#9dc3e6,color:#000000 style T5 fill:#bdd7ee,color:#000000 style Output fill:#385723,color:#ffffff ``` ### 4.2 Mermaid Diagram: Layer-Coupling and Temporal Slicing ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph Layer1["Layer 1: Relation R1 at Time T1"] N1a["Node A"] N1b["Node B"] N1c["Node C"] N1a --- N1b N1b --- N1c end subgraph Layer2["Layer 2: Relation R1 at Time T2"] N2a["Node A"] N2b["Node B"] N2c["Node C"] N2a --- N2b N2b --- N2c end subgraph Layer3["Layer 3: Relation R2 at Time T1"] N3a["Node A"] N3b["Node B"] N3c["Node C"] N3a --- N3b end L1C["Sigma Coupling
Temporal Link"] L2C["Gamma Coupling
Relational Link"] Layer1 -. "Sigma" .-> Layer2 Layer1 -. "Gamma" .-> Layer3 style Layer1 fill:#deebf7 style Layer2 fill:#deebf7 style Layer3 fill:#fff2cc style L1C fill:#c00000,color:#ffffff style L2C fill:#548235,color:#ffffff ``` ### 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Characterisation Pipeline ```mermaid flowchart TB BoxA["Data Acquisition
Multi-channel streams"] BoxB["Tensor Representation
4th-order A"] BoxC["Pre-processing
Sparsification, Denoising"] BoxD["Community Detection Engine
Louvain, Spectral, Tensor-based"] BoxE["Validation Layer
Q-multi, Conductance, NMI"] BoxF["Temporal Tracking
Birth, Merge, Split, Death"] BoxG["Multi-relational Profiling
Participation, Specialisation"] BoxH["Visualisation & Reporting
Heatmaps, Sankey, Dashboards"] BoxA --> BoxB --> BoxC --> BoxD BoxD --> BoxE BoxD --> BoxF BoxD --> BoxG BoxE --> BoxH BoxF --> BoxH BoxG --> BoxH style BoxA fill:#203864,color:#ffffff style BoxD fill:#c00000,color:#ffffff style BoxH fill:#385723,color:#ffffff ``` ## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) **Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Paper]** Define a *multi-relational dynamic social network*. Why is it represented as a fourth-order tensor rather than a simple adjacency matrix? **Model Answer (3 marks):** A multi-relational dynamic social network is a graph $\mathcal{G}=(V, E, R, T)$ where vertices $V$ are connected through multiple relation types $R$ whose weights evolve over time $T$. It is represented as a fourth-order tensor $\mathcal{A} \in \mathbb{R}^{N \times N \times R \times T}$ because a 2-D adjacency matrix cannot simultaneously index **who** is connected, **which relation** is observed, and **when** the interaction occurs. The tensor preserves all three dimensions losslessly and supports multilinear algebra (CP/Tucker) for latent community extraction. **(3 marks)** --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model Paper]** State the **participation coefficient** $P_i$ for an actor in a multi-relational network. What does $P_i = 0$ and $P_i \to 1$ indicate? **Model Answer (3 marks):** $$P_i = 1 - \sum_{r=1}^{R} \left( \frac{k_{ir}}{k_i} \right)^2$$ - $P_i = 0$ → the actor concentrates **all** ties in a single relation → **relational specialist** (e.g., a *purely* family-bound person). - $P_i \to 1$ → the actor distributes ties uniformly across all relations → **relational generalist** (e.g., a *socially active hub* bridging family, work, and friendship). **(3 marks)** --- ### 5.2 Part B — Full-Length 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice) > **KTU 2024 Rule:** *Answer any ONE full question from each module. Each carries 14 marks, with sub-parts (a) 7 marks and (b) 7 marks. Internal choice may be provided.* --- #### QUESTION A (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam — Sample Model Paper, Module 3]** **(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the **architectural pipeline** for multi-relational characterization of dynamic social network communities. Label each stage with its mathematical operator. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution (7 marks):** The pipeline consists of **six sequential stages**: 1. **Data Acquisition** — gather multi-channel interaction logs (voice, text, transaction, mobility) for $N$ actors across $R$ relations and $T$ timestamps. 2. **Tensor Assembly** — construct $\mathcal{A} \in \mathbb{R}^{N \times N \times R \times T}$ where $\mathcal{A}[i,j,r,t] = w_{ij}^{(r,t)}$ is the relational weight. 3. **Slicing and Supra-Construction** — decompose the tensor into $RT$ layers, and form the supra-adjacency $\mathbf{S} \in \mathbb{R}^{NRT \times NRT}$ by adding inter-layer coupling edges weighted by $\sigma$ (temporal) and $\gamma$ (relational). 4. **Quality Function Optimisation** — apply the multislice modularity $Q_{multi}$ and maximise it via greedy or Louvain heuristic. 5. **Community Partition Extraction** — output the partition $\{C_1, C_2, \ldots, C_K\}$ with per-layer labels $g_{is}$. 6. **Characterisation Module** — compute persistence $\mathcal{P}(C)$, participation $P_i$, and emergence/dissolution events. Each stage maps directly to a tensor or matrix operator, enabling algebraic interpretability and reproducibility. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Derive the **multislice modularity** $Q_{multi}$ from the Newman-Girvan baseline. Clearly state the role of parameters $\sigma$ and $\gamma$. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution (7 marks):** Starting from single-layer Newman-Girvan: $$Q_r^{(t)} = \frac{1}{2m_r} \sum_{i,j} \left[ \mathcal{A}_{ijr} - \frac{k_{ir} k_{jr}}{2 m_r} \right] \delta(c_i, c_j)$$ Introduce layer index $s \in \{1, \ldots, RT\}$ and coupling $\omega_{jsr}$: $$ \begin{aligned} Q_{multi} &= \frac{1}{2\mu} \sum_{i,j,s,r} \left[ \left( \mathcal{A}_{ij}^{(s)} - \gamma \frac{k_{is} k_{js}}{2 m_s} \right) \delta_{sr} + \sigma \, \omega_{jsr} (1 - \delta_{sr}) \right] \delta(g_{is}, g_{jr}) \end{aligned} $$ **Role of $\sigma$:** Inter-layer coupling weight — controls the strength of *temporal/relational* edges between slices. Larger $\sigma$ → more continuity, less jitter; smaller $\sigma$ → independent slice optimisation. **Role of $\gamma$:** Intra-layer resolution parameter — rescaling the null-model term. Larger $\gamma$ → favours smaller, tighter communities; smaller $\gamma$ → favours larger, looser communities. The total coupling weight $\mu = \frac{1}{2} \sum_{j,s} k_{js}$ normalises the objective. **(7 marks)** --- #### QUESTION B (14 Marks) — *Internal Choice Alternative* **[KTU University Exam — Sample Model Paper, Module 3, Alternate Slot]** **(a)** Explain the **CP decomposition** of the adjacency tensor $\mathcal{A}$ for community extraction. Show the rank-$P$ approximation and interpret each factor. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution (7 marks):** The Canonical Polyadic (CP) decomposition expresses a tensor as a sum of rank-one components: $$ \begin{aligned} \hat{\mathcal{A}} &= \sum_{p=1}^{P} \lambda_p \, \mathbf{a}_p \circ \mathbf{b}_p \circ \mathbf{c}_p \circ \mathbf{d}_p \\ [\hat{\mathcal{A}}]_{i,j,r,t} &= \sum_{p=1}^{P} \lambda_p \, a_{p,i} \, b_{p,j} \, c_{p,r} \, d_{p,t} \end{aligned} $$ The factors are computed by minimising: $$\min \left\| \mathcal{A} - \hat{\mathcal{A}} \right\|_F^2 \quad \text{s.t.} \quad \Vert \mathbf{a}_p \Vert = \Vert \mathbf{b}_p \Vert = \Vert \mathbf{c}_p \Vert = \Vert \mathbf{d}_p \Vert = 1$$ **Interpretation:** - $\mathbf{a}_p, \mathbf{b}_p \in \mathbb{R}^N$ → row and column actor embeddings (asymmetric communities). - $\mathbf{c}_p \in \mathbb{R}^R$ → relational signature (which relations dominate community $p$). - $\mathbf{d}_p \in \mathbb{R}^T$ → temporal signature (when community $p$ is active). - $\lambda_p$ → importance of the latent community. This factorisation simultaneously yields **multi-relational**, **asymmetric**, and **dynamic** community structure in one unified decomposition. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Define the **community persistence index** $\mathcal{P}(C)$ and compute it for a community $C$ whose consecutive snapshot overlaps are $\{0.9, 0.8, 0.1, 0.0\}$ over $T = 5$ snapshots. Classify the community. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution (7 marks):** **Definition:** $$\mathcal{P}(C) = \frac{1}{T-1} \sum_{t=2}^{T} O_t(C), \quad O_t(C) = \frac{\vert C^{(t)} \cap C^{(t-1)} \vert}{\vert C^{(t)} \cup C^{(t-1)} \vert}$$ **Computation:** Given $T = 5$ snapshots, the number of consecutive pairs is $T - 1 = 4$. $$O_2 = 0.9, \quad O_3 = 0.8, \quad O_4 = 0.1, \quad O_5 = 0.0$$ $$ \begin{aligned} \mathcal{P}(C) &= \frac{1}{4} (0.9 + 0.8 + 0.1 + 0.0) \\ &= \frac{1}{4} \times 1.8 \\ &= 0.45 \end{aligned} $$ **Classification:** Since $0.4 \leq \mathcal{P}(C) = 0.45 < 0.8$, community $C$ is classified as a **transitional community** — it persists strongly in the first half of the observation window but dissolves in the second half. The community is undergoing a **death event** around $t = 4$. **(7 marks)** --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:** > 1. **Never write "$\vert x \vert$" inside a markdown table** — use $\vert x \vert$ or $\lvert x \rvert$ instead. Broken tables cost legibility marks. > 2. **Always include the Kronecker delta** $\delta(g_{is}, g_{jr})$ in the multislice modularity formula. Skipping it = -2 marks. > 3. **Distinguish $\sigma$ (inter-layer) from $\gamma$ (intra-layer)**. Many students swap them — this is the *most common error* flagged by KTU examiners. > 4. **For the participation coefficient**, write the squared-fraction sum *explicitly*; do not omit $\sum_{r}$. > 5. **CP vs Tucker**: CP uses one latent dimension $P$ across all modes; Tucker uses a core tensor with a multi-dimensional core. Examiners *will* ask the difference. > 6. **Always state the time index $t$ in your dynamic formulas** — a static formula on a "dynamic" question is incomplete. --- ### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist) - **Multi-relational** = multiple *types* of edges (family, work, friend, enemy, etc.). - **Dynamic** = network topology *evolves* over time. - **Fourth-order tensor** $\mathcal{A}[i,j,r,t]$ is the canonical representation. - **Multislice modularity** $Q_{multi}$ unifies relations and time via the supra-adjacency $\mathbf{S}$. - **Parameters** $\sigma$ (inter-layer), $\gamma$ (intra-layer/null-model) must be distinguished. - **CP decomposition** = sum of rank-one tensors; each factor encodes a *latent community* with actor, relational, and temporal signatures. - **Tucker decomposition** = uses a multi-mode core tensor; more flexible but less interpretable. - **Participation coefficient** $P_i \in [0, 1)$: 0 = specialist, $\to 1$ = generalist. - **Persistence** $\mathcal{P}(C)$: $\geq 0.8$ stable, $[0.4, 0.8)$ transitional, $< 0.4$ ephemeral. - **Affiliation matrix** $H \in \{0,1\}^{N \times M}$ enables bipartite-modularity communities. - **Events:** communities undergo *birth, growth, merge, split, contraction, death*. - **Coupling kernel** $\omega_{jsr}$ defaults to 1 for consecutive slices, 0 otherwise. - **Applications:** epidemiology, recommender systems, counter-terrorism, smart-city governance. - **Engineering bridge:** tensor methods connect to signal processing (multi-way arrays) and control systems (multi-modal state spaces). - **KTU focus areas:** tensor representation, $Q_{multi}$ derivation, CP interpretation, persistence calculation.

topics.title: Predicting Human Behaviour for Social Communities - Understanding and Predicting Human Behavior for Social Communities
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECMT528 - CONCEPTS IN SOCIAL NETWORK ANALYSIS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Predicting Human Behaviour for Social Communities - Understanding and Predicting Human Behavior for Social Communities

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Aligned)

**Human Behaviour Prediction in Social Communities** is the computational and analytical process of modeling, inferring, and forecasting the actions, decisions, opinions, and interactions of individuals or groups within a social network, based on observable network topology, interaction history, content metadata, temporal patterns, and contextual signals. In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, it is the sub-field of Social Network Analysis (SNA) that applies *graph-theoretic models*, *probabilistic reasoning*, *influence propagation frameworks*, and *machine learning inference* to estimate latent behavioural variables of nodes (actors) and edges (relations) in a social graph $G = (V, E)$.

Mathematically, given a social graph $G = (V, E)$ with nodes $V = \{v_1, v_2, \dots, v_n\}$ and edges $E \subseteq V \times V$, along with a behavioural feature vector $\mathbf{x}_i \in \mathbb{R}^d$ for each node $v_i$, the prediction task is defined as a function:

$$\hat{y}_i = f(\mathcal{N}(v_i), \mathbf{x}_i, \mathbf{X}_{\mathcal{N}(v_i)}, t, C(v_i))$$

where $\mathcal{N}(v_i)$ is the ego-network of $v_i$, $t$ is the temporal context, and $C(v_i)$ is the community membership of $v_i$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** Behaviour prediction is *not* about individual psychology — it is about **collective, relational, and contagion-driven behaviour** that emerges from the **structure of social ties**.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a college campus where students are deciding whether to join a new technical club. No one acts in isolation. Each student:
- **Observes** what their close friends are doing (peer influence)
- **Prefers** to be in groups with similar-minded peers (homophily)
- **Responds** to trending posts, shared links, and visible opinions (exposure)
- **Shifts** behaviour over time as information spreads (temporal dynamics)

A **behaviour prediction model** in SNA treats this campus as a *graph* where students are nodes, friendships are edges, and joining decisions are behaviours. The model then uses the **shape of the friendship network**, the **history of past behaviours**, and the **strength of ties** to predict *who will join next, when, and under what influence*.

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain English Summary:** Behaviour prediction in social networks = *"Given what people are connected to and what their neighbours have done, what will they do next?"*

## 1.3 Core Components of Behaviour Prediction

| Component | Description | Example |
|-----------|-------------|---------|
| **Actor (Node)** | The individual whose behaviour is being predicted | A Twitter user |
| **Behavioural Variable** | The action/opinion being predicted (adoption, churn, vote, retweet) | Will the user retweet? |
| **Context (Community)** | The social group influencing the actor | Political community |
| **Temporal Aspect** | The time-stamp or sequence of behaviour | Time of adoption |
| **Feature Set** | Topological, content-based, and interaction-based features | Degree, centrality, sentiment |

## 1.4 Classification of Behaviours Predicted in SNA

> [!NOTE]
> The KTU 2024 syllabus classifies human behaviours in networks into the following canonical categories:

1. **Adoption Behaviour** – Acceptance of an innovation, product, or idea (e.g., new app, hashtag).
2. **Diffusion Behaviour** – Propagation of information, rumours, or trends across the network.
3. **Link Formation Behaviour** – Prediction of new friendships, follows, or connections.
4. **Interaction Behaviour** – Frequency and type of communication (likes, comments, messages).
5. **Collective Action Behaviour** – Group-level coordinated events (protests, voting, viral campaigns).
6. **Trust & Reputation Behaviour** – Expression of trust, credibility ratings, or endorsement.
7. **Mobility & Location Behaviour** – Movement patterns and check-in behaviour.
8. **Sentiment & Opinion Behaviour** – Polarity shift, opinion dynamics, emotional contagion.

## 1.5 Why Behaviour Prediction is Hard

- **Sparsity of behavioural data** – Most users exhibit only a handful of observable actions.
- **Cold-start problem** – New nodes have no history.
- **Latent influence** – Hidden confounders not directly observable.
- **Dynamic topology** – The network itself evolves with time.
- **Heterophily and noise** – Behavioural signals are not always aligned with the topology.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Theoretical Foundations

Human behaviour prediction rests on three foundational pillars of social network theory:

### Pillar 1: Homophily ("Birds of a feather flock together")

Homophily is the principle that **similar individuals are more likely to be connected**. Behaviourally, this means that if a node $v_i$ is connected to many nodes that exhibit behaviour $b$, then $v_i$ is more likely to exhibit $b$ in the future.

The **assortativity coefficient** measures homophily with respect to a behavioural attribute:

$$r = \frac{\sum_i e_{ii} - \sum_i a_i^2}{1 - \sum_i a_i^2}$$

where $e_{ii}$ is the fraction of edges connecting nodes of type $i$ to nodes of type $i$, and $a_i = \sum_j e_{ij}$ is the fraction of edges connected to nodes of type $i$.

- $r > 0$ → **Homophilous network** (similar nodes connect)
- $r < 0$ → **Heterophilous network** (dissimilar nodes connect)
- $r \approx 0$ → **Random mixing**

### Pillar 2: Influence ("I do what my friends do")

Influence is the principle that **connected individuals causally affect each other's behaviour**. This is distinct from homophily because influence operates *over time* and involves a *direction of causation*.

The classic Granovetter threshold model formalises influence via a threshold function:

$$\theta_i \sim U[0, 1], \quad \text{adopts if } \frac{\vert \{j \in \mathcal{N}(v_i) : y_j = 1\} \vert}{\vert \mathcal{N}(v_i) \vert} \geq \theta_i$$

where $\theta_i$ is the adoption threshold of node $v_i$, and $y_j$ is the binary behavioural state of neighbour $j$.

### Pillar 3: Social Capital & Tie Strength

Granovetter's *The Strength of Weak Ties* (1973) distinguishes:

- **Strong ties** – Frequent, emotionally intense (close friends)
- **Weak ties** – Infrequent, acquaintances (bridges between communities)

The tie strength $w_{ij}$ is often approximated as:

$$w_{ij} = \frac{c_{ij}}{\sqrt{(d_i + d_j)}}$$

where $c_{ij}$ is the number of common neighbours, and $d_i, d_j$ are the degrees of $v_i$ and $v_j$. **Behaviour prediction leverages tie strength** to weight neighbour contributions.

## 2.2 The Three Canonical Diffusion Models

### 2.2.1 Linear Threshold Model (LTM)

In LTM, each node $v_i$ has:
- A threshold $\theta_i \in [0,1]$
- Edge weights $b_{ij}$ from neighbour $v_j$ such that $\sum_{j \in \mathcal{N}(v_i)} b_{ij} \leq 1$

A node adopts when the weighted sum of active neighbours exceeds its threshold:

$$\sum_{j \in \mathcal{N}(v_i), \, y_j = 1} b_{ij} \geq \theta_i \implies y_i \leftarrow 1$$

### 2.2.2 Independent Cascade Model (ICM)

In ICM, when a node $v_j$ becomes active at time $t$, it gets a single chance to activate each inactive neighbour $v_i$ with probability $p_{ji}$ (independent of history). The process repeats until no new activations occur.

### 2.2.3 Decreasing Cascade Model (DCM)

A generalisation of ICM where the activation probability decreases with the number of previously failed attempts.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Formula Sheet for KTU 2024 ESE — Memorise the following:**

| # | Concept | Formula | Symbol Legend | Engineering / Real-world Use |
|---|---------|---------|---------------|------------------------------|
| 1 | **Network Homophily (Assortativity)** | $r = \dfrac{\sum_i e_{ii} - \sum_i a_i^2}{1 - \sum_i a_i^2}$ | $e_{ii}$ = same-type edge fraction | Marketing segmentation, friend recommendation |
| 2 | **Granovetter Tie Strength** | $w_{ij} = \dfrac{c_{ij}}{\sqrt{d_i + d_j}}$ | $c_{ij}$ = common neighbours | Targeted advertising, viral seeding |
| 3 | **Linear Threshold Activation** | $\sum_{j: y_j=1} b_{ij} \geq \theta_i$ | $b_{ij}$ = influence weight, $\theta_i$ = threshold | Cascade prediction, technology adoption |
| 4 | **Independent Cascade Probability** | $P(\text{activation}) = 1 - \prod_{j \in S}(1 - p_{ji})$ | $S$ = active neighbour set | Viral marketing, disease spread |
| 5 | **Degree Centrality** | $C_D(v_i) = \dfrac{\deg(v_i)}{n-1}$ | $n$ = number of nodes | Influencer identification |
| 6 | **Betweenness Centrality** | $C_B(v_i) = \sum_{s \neq v_i \neq t} \dfrac{\sigma_{st}(v_i)}{\sigma_{st}}$ | $\sigma_{st}$ = shortest paths | Bridge detection, information brokers |
| 7 | **Eigenvector Centrality** | $\mathbf{Ax} = \lambda \mathbf{x}$ | $\mathbf{A}$ = adjacency matrix | PageRank for behaviour seeds |
| 8 | **PageRank Score** | $PR(v_i) = \dfrac{1-d}{n} + d \sum_{v_j \in \mathcal{N}(v_i)} \dfrac{PR(v_j)}{L(v_j)}$ | $d$ = damping factor $\approx 0.85$ | Web/Social ranking |
| 9 | **Clustering Coefficient** | $C_i = \dfrac{2 \vert e(\mathcal{N}_i) \vert}{k_i(k_i - 1)}$ | $k_i$ = degree, $e(\mathcal{N}_i)$ = edges in ego-net | Community tightness measure |
| 10 | **Jaccard Similarity** | $J(v_i, v_j) = \dfrac{\vert \mathcal{N}(v_i) \cap \mathcal{N}(v_j) \vert}{\vert \mathcal{N}(v_i) \cup \mathcal{N}(v_j) \vert}$ | Neighbourhood overlap | Link prediction |
| 11 | **Adamic-Adar Index** | $AA(v_i, v_j) = \sum_{v_k \in \mathcal{N}(v_i) \cap \mathcal{N}(v_j)} \dfrac{1}{\log \vert \mathcal{N}(v_k) \vert}$ | Weighted common neighbours | Strong tie detection |
| 12 | **Behavioural Entropy** | $H(y) = -\sum_{c} p_c \log_2 p_c$ | $p_c$ = probability of behaviour class $c$ | Diversity of community behaviour |
| 13 | **Influence Spread (Expected)** | $\sigma(S) = \mathbb{E}[\vert \text{final active set} \vert]$ | $S$ = seed set | Influence Maximisation |
| 14 | **Time-decay Weighting** | $w_{ij}(t) = w_{ij} \cdot e^{-\lambda (t - t_0)}$ | $\lambda$ = decay rate, $t_0$ = event time | Recency-aware prediction |
| 15 | **Modularity (Community)** | $Q = \dfrac{1}{2m} \sum_{ij} \left( A_{ij} - \dfrac{k_i k_j}{2m} \right) \delta(c_i, c_j)$ | $m$ = total edges, $c_i$ = community of $v_i$ | Community detection quality |

## 2.4 Categories of Behaviour Prediction Tasks

### Task A: Node-level Prediction
Predict the behavioural label of a *single node*.
- *Example:* Will user $v_i$ adopt the new feature?
- *Method:* Logistic regression, neural networks on ego-network embeddings.

### Task B: Edge-level (Link) Prediction
Predict whether a *new edge* will form between two nodes.
- *Example:* Will $v_i$ and $v_j$ become friends next month?
- *Method:* Jaccard, Adamic-Adar, embedding-based (node2vec, GraphSAGE).

### Task C: Graph-level Prediction
Predict a *property of the whole community*.
- *Example:* Will this community experience a viral cascade?
- *Method:* Graph kernels, Graph Convolutional Networks (GCN).

### Task D: Temporal / Sequential Prediction
Predict the *next behavioural event in time*.
- *Example:* What action will $v_i$ take next?
- *Method:* RNN, LSTM, Temporal Point Processes (Hawkes process).

## 2.5 The Behaviour Prediction Pipeline (Conceptual)

The standard KTU 2024-aligned pipeline consists of five stages:

1. **Data Ingestion** – Collect graph topology + behavioural logs.
2. **Feature Engineering** – Compute topological (degree, centrality), content (sentiment, topic), and temporal (recency, frequency) features.
3. **Model Selection** – Choose a probabilistic, ML, or deep model.
4. **Training & Validation** – Time-based split, cross-validation, AUC/F1 evaluation.
5. **Deployment & Monitoring** – Real-time scoring with drift detection.

## 2.6 Engineering & Real-world Utility

> [!NOTE]
> **Where Behaviour Prediction is Used in Industry (KTU 2024 Application Layer):**

- **Recommendation Systems** – Netflix, YouTube (predict next view).
- **Viral Marketing** – Identifying seed users for maximum cascade.
- **Epidemiology** – Predicting disease spread in contact networks.
- **Fraud Detection** – Detecting collusion rings in transaction graphs.
- **Political Campaigning** – Predicting voter turnout in constituency networks.
- **Mental Health Monitoring** – Detecting depression from behavioural signals.
- **Smart City Planning** – Predicting crowd movement and traffic cascades.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Expected Cascade Size under Independent Cascade Model

**Problem:** Given a seed set $S_0 \subseteq V$, compute the expected number of active nodes $\sigma(S_0)$ at the end of the diffusion process under the Independent Cascade Model (ICM).

### Step 1 — Define the Live-Edge Graph

In ICM, the diffusion is equivalent to selecting for each directed edge $(v_j, v_i)$ whether it becomes a *live edge* with probability $p_{ji}$. The realised outcome is a deterministic process on a *live-edge graph* $g \sim \mathcal{G}$.

### Step 2 — Express the Outcome as Reachability

In a given realisation $g$, the set of activated nodes is exactly the set of nodes reachable from $S_0$ in $g$. Denote by $R_g(S_0)$ this reachable set.

### Step 3 — Take Expectation over Realisations

By the law of total expectation:

$$\sigma(S_0) = \mathbb{E}_g[\vert R_g(S_0) \vert] = \sum_{v \in V} P(v \in R_g(S_0))$$

### Step 4 — Compute Per-Node Reachability Probability

For each node $v$, define:

$$P(v \in R_g(S_0)) = P(\text{there exists a live path from } S_0 \text{ to } v)$$

### Step 5 — Marginalise over Path Counts

This is computed via Monte Carlo simulation, repeated $R$ times:

$$\hat{\sigma}(S_0) = \frac{1}{R} \sum_{r=1}^{R} \vert R_{g_r}(S_0) \vert$$

This derivation is the **foundational basis for the Influence Maximisation Problem (Kempe, Kleinberg & Tardos, 2003)**, which is central to viral marketing.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Tie Strength via Common Neighbours (Granovetter)

**Problem:** Given a network $G = (V, E)$ and a pair of nodes $(v_i, v_j)$, derive a measure of their tie strength using only the network topology.

### Step 1 — Count Common Neighbours

Let $c_{ij} = \vert \mathcal{N}(v_i) \cap \mathcal{N}(v_j) \vert$ be the number of common neighbours.

### Step 2 — Normalise by Degree

If $v_i$ has high degree, the *raw* common neighbour count inflates. We normalise:

$$w_{ij}^{\text{raw}} = \frac{c_{ij}}{d_i + d_j - c_{ij}}$$

### Step 3 — Apply the Granovetter Penalty

Granovetter argued that ties should be down-weighted for high-degree nodes (popular users have many *weak* ties). A standard penalty:

$$w_{ij} = \frac{c_{ij}}{\sqrt{d_i + d_j}}$$

### Step 4 — Interpretation

- Large $w_{ij}$ → **Strong, embedded tie** (mutual close friends)
- Small $w_{ij}$ → **Weak tie** (acquaintance or bridge)

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters for behaviour prediction:** Strong ties have higher *trust* and thus higher *influence weight* in LTM. Weak ties act as *bridges* that carry novel information across communities.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Linear Threshold Activation Condition

**Problem:** Determine the condition under which node $v_i$ becomes active in the Linear Threshold Model.

### Step 1 — Define Edge Weights

Let $b_{ij} \geq 0$ be the influence weight that $v_j$ exerts on $v_i$, satisfying the normalisation:

$$\sum_{j \in \mathcal{N}(v_i)} b_{ij} \leq 1$$

### Step 2 — Define the Threshold

Each node $v_i$ has a threshold $\theta_i \in [0, 1]$, typically drawn from a uniform distribution $\theta_i \sim U[0,1]$.

### Step 3 — Activation Rule

Node $v_i$ becomes active at time $t$ if and only if the weighted sum of currently active neighbours meets or exceeds its threshold:

$$\sum_{j \in \mathcal{N}(v_i), \, y_j^{(t-1)} = 1} b_{ij} \geq \theta_i \implies y_i^{(t)} = 1$$

### Step 4 — Monotonicity Property

The activation rule is **monotone**: if $v_i$ activates under a seed set $S$, it also activates under any superset $S' \supseteq S$. This submodularity is the *key technical property* enabling greedy approximation algorithms for influence maximisation.

### Step 5 — Greedy Guarantee

The greedy algorithm gives a $(1 - 1/e) \approx 63\%$ approximation to the optimal seed set:

$$\sigma(S_{\text{greedy}}) \geq \left(1 - \frac{1}{e}\right) \sigma(S^*)$$

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4: Behavioural Entropy of a Community

**Problem:** Quantify the *behavioural diversity* of a community $C$.

### Step 1 — Count Behavioural Frequencies

Let $n_c$ be the number of nodes in community $C$ exhibiting behaviour class $c \in \{1, 2, \dots, K\}$, with $\sum_c n_c = \vert C \vert$.

### Step 2 — Compute Probabilities

$$p_c = \frac{n_c}{\vert C \vert}$$

### Step 3 — Apply Shannon Entropy

$$H(C) = -\sum_{c=1}^{K} p_c \log_2 p_c$$

### Step 4 — Interpretation

- $H(C) = 0$ → All nodes in $C$ behave identically.
- $H(C) = \log_2 K$ → Maximum diversity (uniform distribution).

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters:** Low-entropy communities are easier to predict (homogeneity), while high-entropy communities require richer models.

---

## 3.5 Derivation 5: Temporal Point Process for Behavioural Events

**Problem:** Model the intensity of behavioural events (e.g., tweets) for a user over time.

### Step 1 — Define Conditional Intensity

The Hawkes process defines intensity $\lambda(t)$ as:

$$\lambda(t) = \mu(t) + \sum_{t_i < t} \alpha \cdot e^{-\beta (t - t_i)}$$

where:
- $\mu(t)$ = baseline intensity (inherent user activity rate)
- $\alpha$ = excitation magnitude (self-exciting jump)
- $\beta$ = decay rate (how fast the influence of past events fades)

### Step 2 — Compute Expected Event Count

The expected number of events in $[0, T]$:

$$\mathbb{E}[N(T)] = \int_0^T \lambda(t) \, dt$$

### Step 3 — Behaviour Prediction via Intensity

The probability of a new event in $[t, t + \Delta t]$ is:

$$P(\text{event in } [t, t+\Delta t]) = 1 - e^{-\int_t^{t+\Delta t} \lambda(s) \, ds}$$

For small $\Delta t$:

$$P \approx \lambda(t) \cdot \Delta t$$

### Step 4 — Interpretation

A user who has been highly active recently (large $\sum \alpha e^{-\beta(t - t_i)}$) has a high probability of behaving again. **Self-excitation is the temporal analogue of social influence.**

---

## 3.6 Analytical Matrix: Comparison of Prediction Approaches

| Approach | Input | Output | Strengths | Weaknesses | When to Use |
|----------|-------|--------|-----------|------------|-------------|
| **Logistic Regression on Network Features** | Degree, centrality, neighbour labels | $P(y_i = 1)$ | Interpretable, fast | Cannot capture non-linearity | Small networks, baseline |
| **Collective Classification (Iterative)** | Labels of neighbours propagate | Class probabilities | Captures correlation | Convergence issues | Correlated label networks |
| **Random Walk (PageRank-style)** | Graph + behaviour seed | Influence score | Scalable | Static, no temporal | Influence ranking |
| **Node2vec / DeepWalk** | Graph structure | Embeddings $\mathbf{z}_i \in \mathbb{R}^d$ | Captures community structure | Unsupervised | Link prediction, node classification |
| **Graph Convolutional Network (GCN)** | Features + adjacency | Class probability | End-to-end learning | Computationally heavy | Large attributed graphs |
| **GraphSAGE** | Features + neighbour sampling | Embeddings | Inductive, scalable | Hyperparameter sensitive | Dynamic / evolving graphs |
| **Hawkes Process (Temporal)** | Event timestamps | Intensity $\lambda(t)$ | Captures self-excitation | Parametric | Behavioural timing |
| **LSTM on Sequences** | Sequence of events | Next event | Sequential learning | Requires long history | Action prediction |
| **Graph Attention Network (GAT)** | Features + adjacency | Weighted neighbour aggregation | Learns attention weights | Memory intensive | Heterogeneous influence |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram: Behaviour Prediction Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Raw Social Graph G = V, E] --> B[Data Ingestion Layer]
    B --> C[Feature Engineering Module]
    C --> D1[Topological Features]
    C --> D2[Content Features]
    C --> D3[Temporal Features]
    C --> D4[Community Features]
    D1 --> E[Feature Fusion Vector]
    D2 --> E
    D3 --> E
    D4 --> E
    E --> F[Model Selection Layer]
    F --> G1[Probabilistic Models]
    F --> G2[Graph Embedding Models]
    F --> G3[Deep Graph Networks]
    F --> G4[Temporal Point Processes]
    G1 --> H[Behaviour Prediction Output]
    G2 --> H
    G3 --> H
    H --> I[Evaluation Metrics: AUC, F1, NDCG]
    I --> J[Deployment and Monitoring]
    J --> K[Feedback Loop for Retraining]
    K --> C
```

## 4.2 Information Diffusion Cascade Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S0[Seed Node S0] -->|time t1| A1[Node A1 Active]
    S0 -->|time t1| A2[Node A2 Active]
    A1 -->|time t2| B1[Node B1 Active]
    A1 -->|time t2| B2[Node B2 Active]
    A2 -->|time t2| B3[Node B3 Active]
    B1 -->|time t3| C1[Node C1 Active]
    B2 -->|time t3| C2[Node C2 Active]
    B3 -->|time t3| C3[Node C3 Active]
    C1 -->|time t4| D1[Node D1 Active]
    C2 -->|time t4| D2[Node D2 Active]
    D1 -->|time t5| E1[Node E1 Active Final]
    D2 -->|time t5| E2[Node E2 Active Final]
    E1 --> STOP[Cascade Ends]
    E2 --> STOP
    C3 -.->|no activation| X1[Inactive Node]
    B3 -.->|no activation| X2[Inactive Node]
```

## 4.3 Linear Threshold Model — Influence Aggregation Schematic

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    subgraph N1[Node v_i Ego Network]
        V1[Neighbour v_a weight 0.3 active]
        V2[Neighbour v_b weight 0.2 active]
        V3[Neighbour v_c weight 0.4 inactive]
        V4[Neighbour v_d weight 0.1 inactive]
    end
    V1 --> SUM[Weighted Sum Aggregator]
    V2 --> SUM
    V3 --> SUM
    V4 --> SUM
    SUM -->|total = 0.5| COMP{Compare with Threshold}
    TH[Threshold theta_i = 0.45] --> COMP
    COMP -->|0.5 greater than 0.45| ACT[Node v_i becomes ACTIVE]
    COMP -->|sum less than threshold| INACT[Node v_i remains INACTIVE]
```

## 4.4 Community-level Behaviour Prediction Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    subgraph COM1[Community A]
        NA1[Member 1]
        NA2[Member 2]
        NA3[Member 3]
    end
    subgraph COM2[Community B]
        NB1[Member 1]
        NB2[Member 2]
        NB3[Member 3]
    end
    subgraph COM3[Community C]
        NC1[Member 1]
        NC2[Member 2]
        NC3[Member 3]
    end
    NA1 --- NA2
    NA2 --- NA3
    NA1 --- NA3
    NB1 --- NB2
    NB2 --- NB3
    NA2 -.weak tie bridge.-> NB1
    NB3 --- NC1
    NC1 --- NC2
    NC2 --- NC3
    NA3 -.weak tie bridge.-> NC1
    COM1 -->|community behaviour signature| PRED[Behaviour Predictor Module]
    COM2 -->|community behaviour signature| PRED
    COM3 -->|community behaviour signature| PRED
    PRED --> OUT[Predicted Future Behaviour Per Community]
```

## 4.5 Temporal Behaviour Evolution Chart

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    T0[Time t0: Initial State] -->|observation| T1[Time t1: Some adoptions]
    T1 -->|propagation| T2[Time t2: Cascade widens]
    T2 -->|saturation| T3[Time t3: Peak adoption]
    T3 -->|decay| T4[Time t4: Cascade stabilises]
    T0 --- M1[Hawkes Intensity lambda t0]
    T1 --- M2[Hawkes Intensity lambda t1]
    T2 --- M3[Hawkes Intensity lambda t2]
    T3 --- M4[Hawkes Intensity lambda t3]
    T4 --- M5[Hawkes Intensity lambda t4]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question A1** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]`
**Define behaviour prediction in social network analysis. List any four categories of human behaviour commonly predicted in SNA.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Behaviour prediction in social network analysis is the process of using graph topology, interaction history, and contextual information to forecast the actions, opinions, or states of individuals or communities within a social network.

The four categories of human behaviour predicted in SNA are:

1. **Adoption behaviour** – Acceptance of innovations, products, or ideas (e.g., new app downloads).
2. **Diffusion behaviour** – Propagation of information, trends, or rumours across the network.
3. **Link formation behaviour** – Formation of new connections, friendships, or follows.
4. **Interaction behaviour** – Frequency and type of communication (likes, comments, messages).

> *(Any four valid categories from §1.4 of Section 1 — 2 marks for definition + 1 mark for categories)*

---

### **Question A2** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]`
**Explain the concept of homophily in social networks. Write the formula for the assortativity coefficient.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Homophily** is the principle that *individuals with similar attributes, beliefs, or behaviours are more likely to be connected* in a social network. It is one of the foundational mechanisms behind behaviour prediction, because neighbours' behaviour is often correlated with the focal node's behaviour.

The **assortativity coefficient** $r$ is:

$$r = \frac{\sum_i e_{ii} - \sum_i a_i^2}{1 - \sum_i a_i^2}$$

where $e_{ii}$ is the fraction of edges connecting nodes of type $i$ to nodes of type $i$, and $a_i$ is the row sum of the mixing matrix.

- $r > 0$: homophilous network
- $r < 0$: heterophilous network
- $r \approx 0$: random mixing

> *(2 marks for explanation, 1 mark for formula)*

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### **Question B-A (Choice 1)** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Adapted]`

**(a)** Explain the **Linear Threshold Model (LTM)** and the **Independent Cascade Model (ICM)** for behaviour diffusion. Compare their activation mechanisms with suitable equations. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Derive the **expected cascade size** $\sigma(S)$ for a seed set $S$ under ICM, and explain how the submodularity of the spread function enables the greedy algorithm to achieve a $(1 - 1/e)$ approximation. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution for B-A (a)**

The **Linear Threshold Model (LTM)** is a threshold-based diffusion model in which a node adopts a behaviour only when the *cumulative weighted influence* of its active neighbours exceeds a personal threshold.

- Each node $v_i$ has threshold $\theta_i \sim U[0, 1]$.
- Each edge $(v_j, v_i)$ has influence weight $b_{ij} \geq 0$, with $\sum_{j} b_{ij} \leq 1$.

**Activation rule for LTM:**

$$\sum_{j \in \mathcal{N}(v_i), \, y_j = 1} b_{ij} \geq \theta_i \implies y_i \leftarrow 1$$

The **Independent Cascade Model (ICM)** is a probabilistic model in which an active node gets a *single chance* to activate each inactive neighbour with probability $p_{ji}$, independent of history.

**Activation rule for ICM:**

$$P(v_i \text{ activated by } v_j) = p_{ji}, \quad \text{one-shot at } v_j \text{'s activation time}$$

**Comparison:**

| Aspect | LTM | ICM |
|--------|-----|-----|
| Trigger | Cumulative threshold | Probabilistic per-edge |
| Number of attempts | Multiple (whenever neighbour activates) | Single (one-shot) |
| Threshold | Yes ($\theta_i$) | No |
| Edge weight | Yes ($b_{ij}$) | Yes ($p_{ji}$) |
| Time step | Discrete accumulation | Discrete cascades |

#### **Model Solution for B-A (b)**

**Step 1:** In ICM, the diffusion is equivalent to choosing a *live-edge graph* $g$ where each directed edge $(v_j, v_i)$ is live with probability $p_{ji}$.

**Step 2:** The set of activated nodes is the set of nodes *reachable* from the seed set $S$ in the live-edge graph $g$. Denote this by $R_g(S)$.

**Step 3:** The expected cascade size is:

$$\sigma(S) = \mathbb{E}_g[\vert R_g(S) \vert] = \sum_{v \in V} P(v \in R_g(S))$$

**Step 4:** Computing $P(v \in R_g(S))$ exactly is #P-hard. We approximate using Monte Carlo with $R$ realisations:

$$\hat{\sigma}(S) = \frac{1}{R} \sum_{r=1}^{R} \vert R_{g_r}(S) \vert$$

**Step 5 (Submodularity):** The function $\sigma(S)$ is:
- **Monotone**: $\sigma(S') \geq \sigma(S)$ for $S' \supseteq S$.
- **Submodular**: $\sigma(S \cup \{v\}) - \sigma(S) \geq \sigma(T \cup \{v\}) - \sigma(T)$ for $S \subseteq T$.

**Step 6 (Greedy Guarantee):** By Nemhauser-Wolsey-Fisher theorem, the greedy algorithm that iteratively adds the node with maximum marginal gain achieves:

$$\sigma(S_{\text{greedy}}) \geq \left(1 - \frac{1}{e}\right) \sigma(S^*) \approx 0.632 \cdot \sigma(S^*)$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:** Many students **forget to mention submodularity** explicitly. Without submodularity, the $(1 - 1/e)$ bound does *not* hold. Always state both *monotonicity* and *submodularity*.

---

### **Question B-B (Choice 2 — Alternative)** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Adapted]`

**(a)** Discuss the role of **homophily, influence, and tie strength** in predicting human behaviour in social communities. Provide the Granovetter tie strength formula and interpret its components. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Consider a community of 6 users forming the following friendship graph: User 1 is connected to users 2, 3, and 4. User 2 is connected to users 1, 3, and 5. User 3 is connected to users 1, 2, 4, and 6. User 4 is connected to users 1 and 3. User 5 is connected to users 2 and 6. User 6 is connected to users 3 and 5. **Compute the Granovetter tie strength** $w_{ij}$ between every pair $(i, j)$ with at least one common neighbour, and identify the *strongest tie*. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution for B-B (a)**

**Homophily** is the tendency of similar individuals to connect. In behaviour prediction, homophily implies that *neighbouring nodes have correlated behaviours*. A node embedded in a community of adopters is more likely to adopt.

**Influence** is the causal effect of one node's behaviour on another's. It is directional and temporal. Influence-based prediction uses models like LTM and ICM, where the *active state* of neighbours drives future activations.

**Tie Strength** (Granovetter) captures the *intensity* of a relationship using topological features. It is given by:

$$w_{ij} = \frac{c_{ij}}{\sqrt{d_i + d_j}}$$

- $c_{ij}$ = number of *common neighbours* of $v_i$ and $v_j$.
- $d_i, d_j$ = degrees of $v_i$ and $v_j$.

**Interpretation:** A high $c_{ij}$ (many shared friends) increases tie strength, but a large degree sum $(d_i + d_j)$ decreases it. This embodies the principle that *popular users have many weak ties*, while *embedded pairs* have strong ties.

#### **Model Solution for B-B (b)**

**Step 1 — Build the degree sequence:**

| Node | Neighbours | Degree |
|------|------------|--------|
| 1 | {2, 3, 4} | 3 |
| 2 | {1, 3, 5} | 3 |
| 3 | {1, 2, 4, 6} | 4 |
| 4 | {1, 3} | 2 |
| 5 | {2, 6} | 2 |
| 6 | {3, 5} | 2 |

**Step 2 — Identify pairs with at least one common neighbour and compute $c_{ij}$:**

- (1, 2): Common = {3}, $c_{12} = 1$
- (1, 3): Common = {2, 4}, $c_{13} = 2$
- (1, 4): Common = {3}, $c_{14} = 1$
- (1, 5): Common = {2}, $c_{15} = 1$
- (1, 6): Common = {3}, $c_{16} = 1$
- (2, 3): Common = {1}, $c_{23} = 1$
- (2, 4): Common = {1, 3}, $c_{24} = 2$
- (2, 6): Common = {5}, $c_{26} = 1$
- (3, 4): Common = {1}, $c_{34} = 1$
- (3, 5): Common = {2, 6}, $c_{35} = 2$
- (3, 6): Common = ∅, skip
- (4, 5): Common = ∅, skip
- (4, 6): Common = {3}, $c_{46} = 1$
- (5, 6): Common = ∅, skip
- (2, 5): adjacent (not a candidate)
- (3, 6): already computed (no common neighbour)

**Step 3 — Compute Granovetter tie strength $w_{ij} = c_{ij} / \sqrt{d_i + d_j}$:**

$$w_{12} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{3+3}} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{6}} \approx 0.408$$

$$w_{13} = \frac{2}{\sqrt{3+4}} = \frac{2}{\sqrt{7}} \approx 0.756$$

$$w_{14} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{3+2}} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{5}} \approx 0.447$$

$$w_{15} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{3+2}} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{5}} \approx 0.447$$

$$w_{16} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{3+2}} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{5}} \approx 0.447$$

$$w_{23} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{3+4}} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{7}} \approx 0.378$$

$$w_{24} = \frac{2}{\sqrt{3+2}} = \frac{2}{\sqrt{5}} \approx 0.894$$

$$w_{26} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{3+2}} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{5}} \approx 0.447$$

$$w_{34} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{4+2}} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{6}} \approx 0.408$$

$$w_{35} = \frac{2}{\sqrt{4+2}} = \frac{2}{\sqrt{6}} \approx 0.816$$

$$w_{46} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2+2}} = \frac{1}{2} = 0.500$$

**Step 4 — Rank the ties:**

| Rank | Pair | $w_{ij}$ |
|------|------|----------|
| 1 | (2, 4) | **0.894** |
| 2 | (3, 5) | 0.816 |
| 3 | (1, 3) | 0.756 |
| 4 | (4, 6) | 0.500 |
| 5 | (1, 4) | 0.447 |

**Strongest tie: between User 2 and User 4, with $w_{24} \approx 0.894$.**

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:** Students commonly *forget to normalise by degree sum*. Without the $\sqrt{d_i + d_j}$ factor, raw common-neighbour count is biased toward high-degree nodes. Always include the normalisation.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for KTU 2024 ESE — Module 4**

- [x] **Behaviour Prediction** = forecasting node-level or community-level actions in a social graph using topology, history, and context.
- [x] **Three pillars**: *Homophily* (similarity-based connection), *Influence* (causal peer effect), *Tie strength* (Granovetter).
- [x] **Linear Threshold Model (LTM)** uses *cumulative weighted influence* vs. *personal threshold* $\theta_i$.
- [x] **Independent Cascade Model (ICM)** uses *one-shot probabilistic activation* with probability $p_{ji}$.
- [x] **Expected spread** $\sigma(S) = \mathbb{E}[\vert R_g(S) \vert]$ is **submodular and monotone** → greedy gives $1 - 1/e$ approximation.
- [x] **Granovetter tie strength**: $w_{ij} = c_{ij} / \sqrt{d_i + d_j}$ — strong ties = embedded, weak ties = bridges.
- [x] **Assortativity coefficient** $r$: positive = homophily, negative = heterophily.
- [x] **PageRank** $PR(v_i) = (1-d)/n + d \sum PR(v_j)/L(v_j)$ with damping $d \approx 0.85$ — used for behavioural seed ranking.
- [x] **Clustering coefficient** $C_i = 2 \vert e(\mathcal{N}_i) \vert / (k_i(k_i-1))$ — measures community tightness.
- [x] **Behavioural Entropy** $H = -\sum p_c \log_2 p_c$ — low entropy = predictable community.
- [x] **Hawkes process** captures *self-exciting temporal behaviour* with decay $\beta$ and excitation $\alpha$.
- [x] **Modularity** $Q$ quantifies community structure quality (Louvain, Girvan-Newman algorithms).
- [x] **Jaccard similarity** and **Adamic-Adar index** are *unsupervised* link prediction baselines.
- [x] **Deep models** (GCN, GraphSAGE, GAT) are *inductive* — work on unseen nodes; **node2vec/DeepWalk** are *transductive*.
- [x] **Common exam traps**: (i) forgetting submodularity, (ii) confusing homophily with influence, (iii) ignoring the time component in temporal models, (iv) using static features for dynamic behaviour.
- [x] **Real-world applications**: viral marketing, recommendation systems, epidemiology, fraud detection, political analytics, mental health monitoring.
- [x] **Evaluation metrics**: AUC, F1, NDCG, MAP — always report at least 2 in any empirical evaluation.
- [x] **KTU ESE Pattern**: 3-mark questions test definitions and list-based recall; 14-mark questions test derivation, comparison, and numerical computation.
- [x] **Memorise**: LTM equation, ICM equation, assortativity formula, Granovetter tie strength, PageRank, submodularity guarantee.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: User Data Management
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECMT528 - CONCEPTS IN SOCIAL NETWORK ANALYSIS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** User Data Management

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

In the context of **Social Network Analysis (SNA)**, **User Data Management** refers to the systematic, policy-driven, and technology-enabled process of **collecting, storing, organizing, securing, retrieving, analyzing, and disposing of data** generated by, or associated with, individual users (nodes) and their interactions (edges) within a social network. It encompasses the entire **data lifecycle** — from acquisition through archival or destruction — while ensuring **data quality, privacy, ethical integrity, and regulatory compliance**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition Highlight:**
> *User Data Management is the operational and strategic discipline concerned with governing the personal, behavioral, relational, and contextual data of users in a social network, including data acquisition, anonymization, storage architectures, access control, retention policies, and the legal-ethical frameworks (e.g., IT Act 2000 of India, GDPR, DPDP Act 2023) that regulate its use.*

## 1.2 Core Components of User Data

User data in a social network is typically classified into the following **five primary dimensions**:

| Dimension | Description | Example (Facebook / X / Instagram) |
|---|---|---|
| **Identity Data** | Who the user is | Name, User ID, Email, Phone |
| **Profile Data** | Descriptive attributes | Age, Gender, Location, Bio |
| **Behavioral Data** | What the user does | Likes, Shares, Click-streams, Dwell time |
| **Relational Data** | Whom the user connects to | Friends list, Followers, Group memberships |
| **Content Data** | What the user creates | Posts, Photos, Comments, Stories |

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Library Card System" 🗂️

> [!NOTE]
> **Think of User Data Management as a modern, digital Library System.**

Imagine a large public library:
- **Acquisition Desk** = Data Collection Module (registers new visitors, issues cards)
- **Catalog System** = Database Schema (organizes books by author, genre, shelf)
- **Reading Room** = Access Control Layer (only registered members can borrow)
- **CCTV + Security Guard** = Privacy & Encryption Layer
- **Annual Weeding Policy** = Data Retention & Disposal Rules (old, unclaimed records shredded)
- **Visitor Logbook** = Behavioral Data (which section was visited, for how long)

Just as a library balances **service** (easy access) with **security** (protecting rare manuscripts), User Data Management balances **analytical utility** with **user privacy and consent**.

## 1.4 Why is User Data Management Critical in SNA?

1. **Trust & Retention** — Users abandon platforms that mishandle data.
2. **Regulatory Compliance** — Non-compliance attracts heavy fines (up to **4% of global turnover** under GDPR).
3. **Analytical Validity** — SNA metrics (centrality, community detection) are only as good as the underlying data quality (**GIGO Principle**).
4. **Ethical Responsibility** — Prevents discriminatory profiling and surveillance harms.

## 1.5 Key Terminology (KTU-Standard)

- **Data Subject** — The individual user to whom the data pertains.
- **Data Controller** — The entity (e.g., Meta, X) that determines the purpose of data processing.
- **Data Processor** — A third party that processes data on behalf of the controller.
- **PII (Personally Identifiable Information)** — Any data that can identify a specific individual.
- **Anonymization** — Irreversible removal of identifying details.
- **Pseudonymization** — Reversible replacement of identifiers with tokens.
- **Consent** — Freely given, specific, informed, and unambiguous agreement.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The User Data Lifecycle (UDLC)

The lifecycle comprises **six iterative phases**, each with distinct technical and governance requirements.

### Phase 1 — Data Acquisition
- **Sources**: Voluntary (sign-up forms), Observed (cookies, logs), Inferred (ML predictions).
- **Methods**: APIs, Web Scraping (where legal), SDKs, OAuth flows.
- **Consent Mechanism**: Opt-in vs. Opt-out (GDPR mandates **opt-in**).

### Phase 2 — Data Storage
- **Architectures**: Relational (MySQL), NoSQL (MongoDB, Cassandra), Graph DBs (Neo4j — preferred for SNA).
- **Storage Triad — The CAP Theorem**:
  * **C**onsistency
  * **A**vailability
  * **P**artition Tolerance
  * *You can satisfy only two of the three simultaneously.*

### Phase 3 — Data Processing & Cleaning
- **Deduplication**, **Normalization**, **Missing-value Imputation**, **Outlier Detection**.

### Phase 4 — Access Control
- Models: **DAC (Discretionary)**, **MAC (Mandatory)**, **RBAC (Role-Based)**, **ABAC (Attribute-Based)**.

### Phase 5 — Analysis & Mining
- SNA-specific: centrality computation, community detection, link prediction.

### Phase 6 — Retention & Disposal
- Aligns with **Storage Limitation Principle** of GDPR Article 5(e).

## 2.2 Privacy Preservation Frameworks (High-Yield for KTU)

### 2.2.1 k-Anonymity
A dataset satisfies **k-Anonymity** if each record is indistinguishable from at least **k − 1** other records with respect to any quasi-identifier.

$$ \forall r_i \in D, \; \vert \{r_j \in D : Q(r_i) = Q(r_j)\} \vert \geq k $$

where $Q(r)$ is the set of quasi-identifier attributes of record $r$, and $D$ is the dataset.

### 2.2.2 l-Diversity
Strengthens k-Anonymity by requiring at least **l "well-represented" values** for sensitive attributes within each equivalence class.

$$ \forall E \in \text{EquivalenceClasses}(D), \; \vert \pi(S, E) \vert \geq l $$

where $\pi(S, E)$ is the projection of equivalence class $E$ on the sensitive attribute $S$.

### 2.2.3 t-Closeness
Further refines l-Diversity by requiring the distribution of a sensitive attribute within any equivalence class to be at **t-closeness** to the distribution of the attribute in the whole dataset.

$$ D( P(E_S) , \, P(D_S) ) \leq t $$

where $D$ is an appropriate distance metric (e.g., Earth Mover's Distance).

## 2.3 Information-Theoretic Foundations

### 2.3.1 Shannon Entropy
Measures the **uncertainty / information content** in user data distribution.

$$ H(X) = - \sum_{i=1}^{n} p(x_i) \log_2 p(x_i) $$

Higher entropy $\Rightarrow$ more diverse, less predictable user base.

### 2.3.2 Conditional Entropy
Quantifies the remaining uncertainty about user attribute $X$ given knowledge of $Y$.

$$ H(X \vert Y) = \sum_{y \in Y} p(y) \cdot H(X \vert Y = y) $$

### 2.3.3 Mutual Information (Relevance to Data Utility)
Measures the shared information between two user attributes.

$$ I(X; Y) = H(X) - H(X \vert Y) = \sum_{x \in X} \sum_{y \in Y} p(x, y) \log_2 \frac{p(x, y)}{p(x)p(y)} $$

## 2.4 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula | Variables / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | k-Anonymity | $\vert \{r_j : Q(r_i) = Q(r_j)\} \vert \geq k$ | $Q$ = quasi-identifier |
| 2 | l-Diversity | $\vert \pi(S, E) \vert \geq l$ | $S$ = sensitive attribute |
| 3 | t-Closeness | $D(P(E_S), P(D_S)) \leq t$ | EMD distance typical |
| 4 | Shannon Entropy | $H(X) = -\sum p(x_i) \log_2 p(x_i)$ | Units: **bits** |
| 5 | Conditional Entropy | $H(X \vert Y) = \sum p(y) H(X \vert y)$ | $0 \leq H(X \vert Y) \leq H(X)$ |
| 6 | Mutual Information | $I(X;Y) = H(X) - H(X \vert Y)$ | Symmetric, $\geq 0$ |
| 7 | Re-identification Risk | $R_r = 1 / k$ | Inverse of equivalence class size |
| 8 | CAP Trade-off | Pick 2 of {C, A, P} | Distributed systems only |
| 9 | Data Utility Score | $U = w_1 \cdot \text{Completeness} + w_2 \cdot \text{Accuracy}$ | Weighted; $\sum w_i = 1$ |
| 10 | Anonymization Loss | $L = H(D) - H(D')$ | $D'$ is anonymized dataset |

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Recommendation Engines** (Netflix, Spotify) rely on high-quality behavioral data.
- **Epidemic Modeling** (COVID-19 contact tracing) requires privacy-preserving user data.
- **Fraud Detection** in financial social networks (e.g., money-muling rings) depends on relational data integrity.
- **Urban Planning** uses mobility data from social platforms for transport design.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Derivation — Computing k-Anonymity for a Toy Dataset

### Step 1: Define the Dataset

Consider four users with the following quasi-identifier attributes: **Age** and **ZIP Code**.

| User | Age | ZIP | Disease (Sensitive) |
|---|---|---|---|
| $u_1$ | 25 | 682001 | Diabetes |
| $u_2$ | 27 | 682001 | Asthma |
| $u_3$ | 35 | 682005 | Diabetes |
| $u_4$ | 36 | 682005 | Hypertension |

### Step 2: Identify the Quasi-Identifier Set

The quasi-identifier $Q = \{ \text{Age}, \text{ZIP} \}$. Without protection, each record is uniquely identifiable.

### Step 3: Generalize to Achieve k = 2

Apply **Generalization** (a suppression / generalization technique):

* **Age** → age bracket: `[20-29]`, `[30-39]`
* **ZIP** → first 3 digits: `682***` (suppress last 3)

The transformed dataset becomes:

| User | Age (Gen) | ZIP (Gen) | Equivalence Class | Size |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| $u_1$ | 20-29 | 682*** | $E_1$ | 2 |
| $u_2$ | 20-29 | 682*** | $E_1$ | 2 |
| $u_3$ | 30-39 | 682*** | $E_2$ | 2 |
| $u_4$ | 30-39 | 682*** | $E_2$ | 2 |

### Step 4: Verify k-Anonymity

For every record $r_i$, the size of its equivalence class is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\vert E_1 \vert &= 2 \\
\vert E_2 \vert &= 2 \\
k_{\min} &= \min(\vert E_1 \vert, \vert E_2 \vert) = 2
\end{aligned}
$$

Since $k_{\min} = 2 \geq k = 2$, the dataset satisfies **2-Anonymity**. ✔️

## 3.2 Analytical Derivation — Re-identification Risk

Given an equivalence class of size $k$, the probability that an adversary correctly re-identifies a record by brute force is:

$$
P_{\text{re-id}} = \frac{1}{k}
$$

The **re-identification risk per record** is therefore:

$$
R_r = \frac{1}{\vert E(r) \vert}
$$

where $\vert E(r) \vert$ is the size of the equivalence class containing record $r$.

For the dataset above, for every record: $R_r = 1/2 = 0.5$ (50%).

## 3.3 Analytical Derivation — Entropy of a User Behavior Distribution

Suppose a user has the following online behavior distribution over 4 actions (Post, Like, Comment, Share):

$$
p(\text{Post}) = 0.5, \; p(\text{Like}) = 0.25, \; p(\text{Comment}) = 0.15, \; p(\text{Share}) = 0.10
$$

Step 1 — Compute each term $-p_i \log_2 p_i$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
-0.50 \log_2(0.50) &= -0.50 \times (-1.000) = 0.500 \\
-0.25 \log_2(0.25) &= -0.25 \times (-2.000) = 0.500 \\
-0.15 \log_2(0.15) &= -0.15 \times (-2.737) = 0.411 \\
-0.10 \log_2(0.10) &= -0.10 \times (-3.322) = 0.332
\end{aligned}
$$

Step 2 — Sum the contributions:

$$
\begin{aligned}
H(X) &= 0.500 + 0.500 + 0.411 + 0.332 \\
H(X) &= 1.743 \; \text{bits}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Interpretation:** The user's behavior is moderately predictable (maximum possible entropy = $\log_2 4 = 2$ bits).

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Comparison of Privacy Models

| Model | Year Proposed | Strength | Weakness | KTU Exam Weight |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| k-Anonymity | 1998 (Samarati & Sweeney) | Simple, intuitive | Vulnerable to homogeneity & background-knowledge attacks | ⭐⭐⭐⭐ |
| l-Diversity | 2007 (Machanavajjhala et al.) | Handles homogeneity attack | Still vulnerable to skewness & similarity attacks | ⭐⭐⭐⭐ |
| t-Closeness | 2007 (Li et al.) | Stronger distribution guarantees | Computationally expensive (EMD) | ⭐⭐⭐ |
| Differential Privacy | 2006 (Dwork) | Mathematically rigorous, composable | Reduces data utility significantly | ⭐⭐⭐ |
| Federated Learning | 2016 (Google) | Data never leaves user device | Communication overhead, model inversion risk | ⭐⭐⭐ |

## 3.5 Comprehensive Analytical Walkthrough — Mutual Information in SNA

**Problem:** In a social network, attribute *X* = "Posts per day" (Low / High) and *Y* = "Number of close friends" (Few / Many). Compute $I(X; Y)$ from the joint distribution.

Joint Probability Table $P(X, Y)$:

| | Few Friends | Many Friends | $P(X)$ |
|---|---|---|---|
| Low Posts | 0.30 | 0.10 | 0.40 |
| High Posts | 0.20 | 0.40 | 0.60 |
| $P(Y)$ | 0.50 | 0.50 | 1.00 |

Step 1 — Compute marginal entropies:

$$
\begin{aligned}
H(X) &= -[0.40 \log_2 0.40 + 0.60 \log_2 0.60] \\
     &= -[0.40 \times (-1.322) + 0.60 \times (-0.737)] \\
     &= 0.529 + 0.442 = 0.971 \text{ bits}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
H(Y) &= -[0.50 \log_2 0.50 + 0.50 \log_2 0.50] = 1.000 \text{ bits}
\end{aligned}
$$

Step 2 — Compute Joint Entropy $H(X, Y)$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
H(X,Y) &= -\sum_{x,y} p(x,y) \log_2 p(x,y) \\
       &= -[0.30 \log_2 0.30 + 0.10 \log_2 0.10 + 0.20 \log_2 0.20 + 0.40 \log_2 0.40] \\
       &= -[0.30 \times (-1.737) + 0.10 \times (-3.322) + 0.20 \times (-2.322) + 0.40 \times (-1.322)] \\
       &= 0.521 + 0.332 + 0.464 + 0.529 = 1.846 \text{ bits}
\end{aligned}
$$

Step 3 — Compute Mutual Information:

$$
\begin{aligned}
I(X;Y) &= H(X) + H(Y) - H(X,Y) \\
       &= 0.971 + 1.000 - 1.846 \\
       &= 0.125 \text{ bits}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Interpretation:** The attributes share only **0.125 bits** of information — i.e., posting frequency is a weak predictor of friend count, indicating that the two features are largely independent and both should be retained in SNA modeling.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 User Data Lifecycle Architecture (Mermaid Flow)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[User Interaction Layer] --> B[Data Acquisition Module]
    B --> C[Consent & Validation Engine]
    C --> D[Encryption & Tokenization]
    D --> E[(Graph Database / Neo4j)]
    E --> F[Access Control Layer - RBAC / ABAC]
    F --> G[Analytics & SNA Engine]
    G --> H[Anonymization Pipeline]
    H --> I[Insight Output / API]
    E --> J[Retention Policy Scheduler]
    J --> K[Secure Disposal / Archival]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** The flow moves from raw user interaction (left) through governance and storage (middle) to insight generation and lifecycle closure (right). Any non-compliant record is filtered out at the Consent Engine.

## 4.2 Privacy Preservation Stack

```mermaid
graph TB
    L1[Differential Privacy Layer - Top] --> L2[t-Closeness]
    L2 --> L3[l-Diversity]
    L3 --> L4[k-Anonymity]
    L4 --> L5[Raw User Data - Bottom]

    L1 -.->|stronger guarantee| L2
    L2 -.->|stronger guarantee| L3
    L3 -.->|stronger guarantee| L4
    L4 -.->|stronger guarantee| L5
```

**Interpretation:** Each higher layer provides strictly stronger privacy guarantees but reduces data utility. The KTU-exam convention is to identify the **weakest model** that satisfies a given requirement.

## 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture for an SNA User Data Platform

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph InputLayer["INPUT LAYER"]
        IN1[Web Client]
        IN2[Mobile SDK]
        IN3[Partner APIs]
    end

    subgraph GovernanceLayer["GOVERNANCE LAYER"]
        G1[Consent Manager]
        G2[Policy Engine - GDPR / DPDP]
        G3[Audit Logger]
    end

    subgraph ProcessingLayer["PROCESSING LAYER"]
        P1[Ingestion Queue - Kafka]
        P2[ETL Pipelines]
        P3[Graph Builder - NetworkX / Neo4j]
    end

    subgraph StorageLayer["STORAGE LAYER"]
        S1[(User Profile DB)]
        S2[(Interaction Graph DB)]
        S3[(Data Lake - Cold Storage)]
    end

    subgraph AnalyticsLayer["ANALYTICS LAYER"]
        A1[Centrality Computation]
        A2[Community Detection]
        A3[Influence Scoring]
    end

    IN1 --> G1
    IN2 --> G1
    IN3 --> G1
    G1 --> G2
    G2 --> P1
    P1 --> P2
    P2 --> P3
    P3 --> S1
    P3 --> S2
    S1 --> S3
    S2 --> A1
    S2 --> A2
    S1 --> A3
    A1 --> G3
    A2 --> G3
    A3 --> G3
```

> [!TIP]
> **For KTU diagram-based questions:** Always label edges with the **type of data flow** (e.g., "PII payload", "anonymized telemetry") rather than generic arrows. This is what examiners look for in the 14-mark full questions.

## 4.4 Data Classification Matrix (Visual)

```mermaid
quadrantChart
    title User Data Sensitivity vs Analytical Value
    x-axis Low Analytical Value --> High Analytical Value
    y-axis Low Sensitivity --> High Sensitivity
    quadrant-1 Critical - Govern Heavily
    quadrant-2 Strategic - Anonymize
    quadrant-3 Routine - Standard Handling
    quadrant-4 Insight Gold - Protect and Use Carefully
    Public Profile Data: [0.35, 0.20]
    Behavioral Streams: [0.85, 0.75]
    Identity Credentials: [0.25, 0.95]
    Demographic Aggregates: [0.70, 0.40]
    Location Trails: [0.80, 0.85]
    Device Fingerprints: [0.55, 0.90]
```

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 📘 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. Define User Data Management in the context of Social Network Analysis. List any four types of user data commonly managed.**

`[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1.5 marks):** User Data Management in SNA is the disciplined process of collecting, organizing, storing, securing, analyzing, and disposing of user-related data — including identities, profiles, behaviors, relationships, and content — while upholding quality, privacy, and regulatory standards.

**Four Types of User Data (4 × 0.375 = 1.5 marks):**
1. **Identity Data** — User ID, email, phone number.
2. **Profile Data** — Age, gender, location, bio.
3. **Behavioral Data** — Likes, shares, clickstreams.
4. **Relational Data** — Friends list, followers, group memberships.

*(Acceptable alternatives: Content Data, Device Data, Preference Data, Transaction Data.)*

---

### **Q2. Differentiate between Anonymization and Pseudonymization with a suitable example.**

`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Anonymization | Pseudonymization |
|---|---|---|
| **Reversibility** | Irreversible | Reversible (with a key) |
| **GDPR Status** | Not personal data | Still personal data |
| **Technique** | Removes identifiers entirely | Replaces identifiers with tokens |
| **Risk Level** | Low re-identification risk | Higher (re-identification possible) |
| **Example** | "Rahul, 25, Kerala" → "Person A, 20-30, South India" | "Rahul" → "User_8f3a" (lookup table kept) |

*(1 mark for correct definition of each, 1 mark for distinguishing feature, 1 mark for example.)*

---

## 📕 PART B — Full ESE Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> **KTU Rule:** *Answer the question choosing EITHER (a) OR (b). Each sub-part carries 7 marks.*

---

### **QUESTION A (14 Marks)**

#### **Q.A.(a)** *(7 marks)*

**Explain the k-Anonymity model in detail. How is it achieved using generalization and suppression? Illustrate with a small example.**

`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024]`

**Model Answer:**

**(i) Concept (2 marks):** A dataset $D$ is said to satisfy k-Anonymity if, for every record $r \in D$, there exist at least $k-1$ other records whose quasi-identifier values are identical to those of $r$. In other words, each record is indistinguishable from at least $k-1$ others with respect to quasi-identifier attributes $Q$.

**(ii) Achieving k-Anonymity (2 marks):**

- **Generalization** — Replace specific attribute values with broader categories.
  *Example:* Age = 25 → Age bracket [20-30].
- **Suppression** — Remove or mask certain attribute values (often denoted by `*`).
  *Example:* ZIP = 682001 → ZIP = 682***.

These two techniques are applied iteratively (often via algorithms like *Datafly* or *Incognito*) until the equivalence-class size condition is met.

**(iii) Example Illustration (3 marks):**

Original dataset (sensitive attribute = Diagnosis):

| Age | ZIP | Diagnosis |
|---|---|---|
| 25 | 682001 | Diabetes |
| 28 | 682002 | Asthma |
| 32 | 682005 | Cancer |
| 36 | 682006 | Diabetes |

Apply generalization (Age → decade, ZIP → first 3 digits) for **k = 2**:

| Age | ZIP | Diagnosis | Equiv. Class |
|---|---|---|---|
| 20-29 | 682*** | Diabetes | $E_1$ |
| 20-29 | 682*** | Asthma | $E_1$ |
| 30-39 | 682*** | Cancer | $E_2$ |
| 30-39 | 682*** | Diabetes | $E_2$ |

Since $\vert E_1 \vert = \vert E_2 \vert = 2 = k$, the dataset is **2-Anonymous**. ✔️

---

#### **Q.A.(b)** *(7 marks)*

**Describe the User Data Lifecycle (UDLC). Explain the role of the Consent Management module within it.**

`[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Model Answer:**

**(i) UDLC Phases (5 × 0.5 = 2.5 marks):**

1. **Acquisition** — Capture user data through forms, cookies, APIs.
2. **Storage** — Persist in relational/NoSQL/graph databases.
3. **Processing** — Clean, normalize, integrate multi-source data.
4. **Access & Analysis** — Apply RBAC/ABAC, run SNA algorithms.
5. **Retention & Disposal** — Archive or securely delete per policy.

**(ii) Role of Consent Management (4.5 marks):**

- **Lawful Basis:** Under GDPR Article 6 and India's DPDP Act 2023, processing requires a lawful basis — typically **explicit, informed, opt-in consent**.
- **Granularity:** Users can grant consent per purpose (e.g., analytics, advertising, personalization).
- **Withdrawability:** Consent must be as easy to withdraw as to give.
- **Auditability:** Every consent action is timestamped and stored as a tamper-proof log for regulatory audits.
- **Default State:** Without active opt-in, the user's data must remain in a "quarantined" state — no processing, no profiling.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:**
> Students frequently describe consent as a "checkbox" — losing 2-3 marks. The examiner expects you to mention **granular, revocable, demonstrable** consent with **audit logs**. Also, do not confuse *Consent* with *Notice* — notice alone is **not consent**.

---

### **QUESTION B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

#### **Q.B.(a)** *(7 marks)*

**With a neat diagram, explain the functional architecture of a User Data Management platform for a Social Network. Highlight at least four architectural layers.**

`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer:**

**Diagram (3 marks):**

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    L1[User Devices] --> L2[Acquisition Layer]
    L2 --> L3[Governance Layer - Consent, Policy, Audit]
    L3 --> L4[Processing Layer - ETL, Graph Builder]
    L4 --> L5[Storage Layer - Profile DB, Graph DB, Data Lake]
    L5 --> L6[Analytics Layer - Centrality, Community, Influence]
    L6 --> L7[Insight & Visualization API]
    L5 --> L8[Retention & Disposal Scheduler]
```

**Explanation of Layers (4 marks — 1 mark per layer):**

1. **Acquisition Layer** — Captures user signals via web, mobile, partner APIs.
2. **Governance Layer** — Enforces consent, privacy policies (GDPR/DPDP), and audit trails.
3. **Processing Layer** — Cleans, transforms, and constructs the social graph.
4. **Storage Layer** — Stores profiles, relationships, and cold logs separately.
5. **Analytics Layer** — Runs SNA algorithms (PageRank, Louvain, etc.).
6. **Lifecycle Closure** — Handles archival and secure deletion.

---

#### **Q.B.(b)** *(7 marks)*

**Discuss the ethical and regulatory challenges in User Data Management for Social Networks. Mention India's DPDP Act 2023 and GDPR in your answer.**

`[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Model Answer:**

**(i) Key Ethical Challenges (3.5 marks):**

- **Informed Consent** — Lengthy, opaque privacy policies obscure meaningful choice.
- **Surveillance & Profiling** — Behavioral data enables granular user profiling, risking discrimination.
- **Data Breaches** — Centralized data lakes become lucrative targets.
- **Algorithmic Bias** — Training data may reflect societal biases (gender, race).
- **Right to be Forgotten** — Tension between user rights and platform's "history" feature.

**(ii) Regulatory Landscape (3.5 marks):**

| Framework | Jurisdiction | Key Mandate |
|---|---|---|
| **GDPR** | European Union | Lawful basis, right to erasure, data portability, **DPO** appointment |
| **DPDP Act 2023** | India | Consent, data fiduciaries, penalties up to **₹250 crore** for breaches |
| **IT Act 2000 + SPDI Rules 2011** | India | Reasonable security practices, sensitive personal data handling |
| **CCPA** | California, USA | Right to know, delete, opt-out of data sale |

**Conclusion:** Compliance is no longer a legal checkbox but a **strategic differentiator** for social platforms — impacting user trust, market valuation, and global operability.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:**
> - Do not write only GDPR — Indian frameworks (DPDP, IT Act) carry **equal weight** in KTU exams.
> - Mention **specific penalty figures** (e.g., ₹250 crore under DPDP) to score full marks.
> - Avoid vague statements like *"privacy is important"* — examiners reward **specific rights, articles, and clauses**.

---

## 🧠 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist (must memorize before ESE):**

- ✅ **User Data Management** = end-to-end governance of identity, profile, behavioral, relational, and content data.
- ✅ **UDLC Phases** = Acquisition → Storage → Processing → Access → Analysis → Disposal.
- ✅ **k-Anonymity**: each record indistinguishable from at least $k-1$ others on quasi-identifiers.
- ✅ **l-Diversity**: extends k-Anonymity by requiring $\geq l$ diverse sensitive values per equivalence class.
- ✅ **t-Closeness**: requires sensitive-attribute distribution within each class to be within distance $t$ of the global distribution.
- ✅ **Re-identification Risk** $R_r = 1 / k$ for an equivalence class of size $k$.
- ✅ **CAP Theorem**: pick any two of {Consistency, Availability, Partition Tolerance}.
- ✅ **DPDP Act 2023** penalty: up to **₹250 crore** per data breach incident.
- ✅ **GDPR Article 6** lists six lawful bases for processing (consent is one of them).
- ✅ **Anonymization ≠ Pseudonymization** — the former is irreversible.
- ✅ **Consent must be** — explicit, informed, granular, revocable, and demonstrable.
- ✅ **CAP, k, l, t** are all tunable parameters; their increase → ↑ privacy, ↓ utility.

**Best-of-Luck for your KTU ESE preparation!** 🎓
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Inference and Distribution
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECMT528 - CONCEPTS IN SOCIAL NETWORK ANALYSIS
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Inference and Distribution

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition

In the context of **Social Network Analysis (SNA)**, **Inference and Distribution** refers to the body of statistical techniques used to model the probabilistic structure of networks, characterize how network properties (such as degree, path length, clustering) are distributed across the population of nodes/edges, and to draw statistically valid conclusions about a large, often only partially observed, network from sampled data.

- **Distribution** describes *how* a network property is spread — for example, the **degree distribution** $P(k)$ gives the probability that a randomly chosen node has exactly $k$ connections.
- **Inference** describes *how we learn* about these distributions — through sampling, estimation, and hypothesis testing.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** Statistical inference in networks is the process of using observed sub-network data to make probabilistic statements about the parameters and structural properties of the underlying true network, accounting for the dependencies inherent in relational (non-i.i.d.) data.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy

Imagine you want to know the **average number of close friends** that students in a 10,000-student university have. Asking every student is impractical. So you survey 200 students and compute an average. The "**distribution**" is the *shape* of friendships (most have 3-5 friends, a few have 100+). The "**inference**" is the leap of logic: from the 200 sampled students, you estimate the average for all 10,000 with a margin of error.

In a network, however, the trick is that *your friend's friends are more likely to also be in your circle* — observations are **not independent**. This is what makes network inference fundamentally different from classical statistics.

> [!NOTE]
> **Three pillars of network inference (KTU High-Yield):**
> 1. **Distributional Modeling** — Binomial, Poisson, Power Law, Exponential
> 2. **Sampling & Estimation** — How to extract a representative sub-network
> 3. **Hypothesis Testing** — Is the network "random", "small-world", or "scale-free"?

### 1.3 Key Metrics & Standard Quantities

The following quantities are central to the topic and must be memorized:

- **Mean degree**: $\langle k \rangle$ — average number of edges per node.
- **Degree distribution**: $P(k)$ — probability that a uniformly random node has degree $k$.
- **Cumulative distribution**: $P_{\geq}(k) = \sum_{k' \geq k} P(k')$.
- **Power-law exponent**: $\gamma$ — typically **between 2 and 3** for real social networks.
- **Confidence Level**: usually **95%** ($\alpha = 0.05$) in KTU exam contexts.
- **Avogadro-scale constant for networks**: not applicable; networks are dimensionless, but $N$ (nodes) and $M$ (edges) are the canonical size measures.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Step-by-Step Theoretical Breakdown

#### A. Degree Distribution in Erdős–Rényi Random Graphs $G(N, p)$

Each of the $N - 1$ possible edges incident to a node exists independently with probability $p$. Hence the degree of a node follows a **Binomial distribution**:

$$
P(k) = \binom{N-1}{k} p^{k} (1-p)^{N-1-k}
$$

For large $N$ and fixed mean $\lambda = p(N-1)$, the Binomial converges to a **Poisson distribution**:

$$
P(k) \approx \frac{e^{-\lambda} \lambda^{k}}{k!}, \quad k = 0, 1, 2, \ldots
$$

**Why this matters:** It serves as the *null model* against which real networks are compared. If a real network's degree distribution deviates sharply from Poisson, it signals structure (hubs, communities).

#### B. Heavy-Tailed and Power-Law Distributions

Many real networks (the web, social media, citation networks) exhibit a **Power-Law** degree distribution:

$$
P(k) = C \cdot k^{-\gamma}, \quad k \geq k_{\min}
$$

where $\gamma$ is the **scaling exponent** (typically $2 < \gamma < 3$ for social networks) and $C$ is the normalization constant:

$$
C = (\gamma - 1) \, k_{\min}^{\gamma - 1}
$$

**The "Why":** Power laws arise from **preferential attachment** — rich-get-richer dynamics where new nodes are more likely to link to already-well-connected nodes.

#### C. Network Sampling Strategies

Because the entire network is often inaccessible (think Facebook, the dark web), we sample. The choice of sampler **biases** what we observe:

1. **Node Sampling** — pick $n$ nodes uniformly at random, induce the sub-graph.
2. **Edge Sampling** — pick $m$ edges uniformly, observe the two endpoints.
3. **Snowball Sampling** — start from a seed, follow edges outward.
4. **Random Walk Sampling** — move to a random neighbor at each step.
5. **Respondent-Driven Sampling (RDS)** — combines snowball with weighting.

> [!NOTE]
> **Pitfall:** Snowball and Random Walk samplers systematically **over-sample high-degree nodes** — your inference must correct for this with Horvitz–Thompson style weights.

#### D. Maximum Likelihood Estimation (MLE) for the Power-Law Exponent

Given observed degrees $k_1, k_2, \ldots, k_n \geq k_{\min}$, the MLE for $\gamma$ is:

$$
\hat{\gamma} = 1 + n \left[ \sum_{i=1}^{n} \ln \frac{k_i}{k_{\min}} \right]^{-1}
$$

**Intuition:** You are solving $\partial \ln \mathcal{L} / \partial \gamma = 0$, where the log-likelihood is the sum of $\ln P(k_i)$. The form $1 + n / \sum \ln(k_i / k_{\min})$ is the closed-form root.

#### E. Hypothesis Testing: Is the Network Scale-Free?
