<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Paper]**
> **Q1. Define Reality Mining. List any four data sources used in it.**
>
> **Model Answer:**
> **Reality Mining** is the process of collecting and analyzing sensor-generated behavioral data from personal devices (smartphones, wearables) to infer patterns of human interaction, mobility, and social structure.
>
> **Four Data Sources:**
> 1. **GPS** — for location and mobility.
> 2. **Bluetooth** — for proximity and co-presence.
> 3. **Accelerometer** — for activity recognition.
> 4. **Call Detail Records (CDR)** — for communication patterns.

> **[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Supplementary]**
> **Q2. What are Eigenbehaviors? Why are they useful in Reality Mining?**
>
> **Model Answer:**
> **Eigenbehaviors** are a small set of dominant, statistically derived behavioral patterns extracted from high-dimensional sensor data using techniques such as **Non-negative Matrix Factorization (NMF)**. (1.5 marks)
>
> **Why useful:** They compress massive, redundant sensor streams into a tractable low-dimensional representation (typically 5–10 patterns), retain >90% variance, enable efficient pattern recognition, and make predictive modeling computationally feasible. (1.5 marks)

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **Instructions (KTU 2024 ESE Pattern):** Answer **any one** of the following two full questions. Each carries 14 marks with internal sub-parts of 7 + 7.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *Full Solution Provided*

**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**
**(a)** Explain in detail the **five-stage DEDIC pipeline of Reality Mining** with a neat block diagram. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A user exhibits four behavioral states with the following probability distribution: $p(x_1) = 0.5$, $p(x_2) = 0.25$, $p(x_3) = 0.125$, $p(x_4) = 0.125$. Compute the Shannon Entropy and interpret the result. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) The DEDIC Pipeline:** (7 marks)

1. **D — Data Acquisition (1.5 marks):** Sensor data is collected from smartphones, wearables, and ambient IoT. Modalities include GPS, Bluetooth, Wi-Fi, accelerometer, CDR, and app logs. Sampling rates vary by sensor type.

2. **E — Extraction & Preprocessing (1 mark):** Raw data is filtered (Kalman filters, smoothing), features engineered (dwell time, encounter frequency), and anonymized to protect user privacy.

3. **D — Detection of Behavioral Patterns (1.5 marks):** Machine learning algorithms (K-Means, DBSCAN, NMF) identify clusters and **Eigenbehaviors** — the dominant behavioral signatures.

4. **I — Inference of Social Structure (1.5 marks):** A weighted social graph is constructed where edge weights represent the strength of inferred ties (based on co-location frequency and duration).

5. **C — Cognitive & Predictive Modeling (1 mark):** The inferred network is used for forecasting — predicting individual behavior, epidemic spread, team performance, or urban mobility flows.

(Diagrammatic representation as shown in Figure 4.1 above carries **0.5 marks**.)

**(b) Entropy Calculation:** (7 marks)

**Step 1 — Formula statement (1 mark):**

$$H(X) = -\sum_{i=1}^{4} p(x_i) \log_2 p(x_i)$$

**Step 2 — Substitution (2 marks):**

$$H(X) = -[0.5 \log_2 0.5 + 0.25 \log_2 0.25 + 0.125 \log_2 0.125 + 0.125 \log_2 0.125]$$

**Step 3 — Logarithmic evaluation (2 marks):**

$$H(X) = -[0.5 \times (-1) + 0.25 \times (-2) + 0.125 \times (-3) + 0.125 \times (-3)]$$

$$H(X) = -[-0.5 - 0.5 - 0.375 - 0.375]$$

**Step 4 — Final result and interpretation (2 marks):**

$$H(X) = 1.75 \text{ bits}$$

**Interpretation:** The maximum possible entropy is $\log_2(4) = 2.0$ bits. The user has $H = 1.75$ bits, which is **87.5%** of the maximum. This means the user is **fairly unpredictable** — behavior is nearly uniformly random across the four states, indicating weak routine formation.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *Full Solution Provided*

**[KTU University Exam - May 2024]**
**(a)** Discuss the **role of proximity sensors (especially Bluetooth)** in inferring social ties. How does Pentland's tie strength formula work? Derive the tie strength for two users who co-located for 10, 15, 20, and 25 minutes on 4 of 7 days. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Compare and contrast **Traditional Social Network Analysis** with **Reality Mining-based SNA** along five dimensions. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Proximity Sensors and Tie Strength:** (7 marks)

**Role of Bluetooth (2 marks):** Bluetooth Low Energy (BLE) scans detect nearby devices via MAC addresses and Received Signal Strength Indicator (RSSI). When two individuals are within ~5–10 meters, their devices mutually discover each other. Repeated, sustained co-presence implies a **strong face-to-face tie** — a proxy for genuine social connection (Granovetter's "strong ties" theory).

**Tie Strength Formula (Pentland, 2009) (1 mark):**

$$w_{ij} = \frac{1}{T} \sum_{t=1}^{T} \delta_{ij}(t) \cdot \tau_{ij}(t)$$

**Numerical Derivation (4 marks):**

**Step 1:** $T = 7$ days, co-located days = 4, durations = $\{10, 15, 20, 25\}$ minutes.

**Step 2:** Sum the durations.

$$\sum \tau_{ij}(t) = 10 + 15 + 20 + 25 = 70 \text{ minutes}$$

**Step 3:** Apply formula.

$$w_{ij} = \frac{70}{7} = 10 \text{ minutes/day}$$

**Step 4:** Interpretation. An average of 10 minutes/day of co-location is a **strong social tie** by Pentland's threshold (≥5 min/day).

---

**(b) Comparison: Traditional SNA vs. Reality Mining SNA (7 marks — 1.4 marks each dimension):**

| Dimension | Traditional SNA | Reality Mining SNA |
|---|---|---|
| **Data Source** | Surveys, interviews, self-reports | Passive sensor streams (GPS, BLE, CDR) |
| **Temporal Resolution** | Static snapshot | Continuous longitudinal |
| **Objectivity** | Subject to recall and social-desirability bias | Objective, automated, bias-free |
| **Scale** | Limited by survey logistics (10²–10³ nodes) | Scales to millions (cellular/wifi data) |
| **Inference Type** | Self-declared ties | Behaviorally inferred ties |
| **Cost & Effort** | High (manual data collection) | Low (after sensor deployment) |
| **Privacy Risk** | Low (explicit consent) | High (continuous surveillance) |

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in Reality Mining Questions:**
>
> 1. **Confusing "Eigenbehaviors" with "Eigenvectors"** — they are related (both come from spectral/NMF methods) but Eigenbehaviors are *behavioral patterns*, not algebraic objects. **(−2 marks)**
> 2. **Forgetting units in entropy calculations** — always state the unit (**bits**) explicitly.
> 3. **Writing $\log$ instead of $\log_2$** in Shannon entropy — KTU examiners expect base-2 for information-theoretic measures.
> 4. **Omitting the block diagram in pipeline questions** — even a hand-drawn sketch in the exam hall earns 0.5–1 mark.
> 5. **Not interpreting numerical results** — computing $H = 1.75$ bits is incomplete; you **must** compare with $H_{max} = \log_2(n)$ and provide a behavioral interpretation.
> 6. **Mixing up CDR (telecom metadata) with call content** — Reality Mining uses **metadata only**, not call audio/text content.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **Rapid Revision Checklist (Module 4 — Reality Mining):**

- ✅ **Definition:** Reality Mining = sensor-based behavioral analytics for social network inference.
- ✅ **Origin:** Coined by **Sandy Pentland** (MIT, 2005).
- ✅ **Pipeline:** **D**ata → **E**xtraction → **D**etection → **I**nference → **C**ognition (DEDIC).
- ✅ **Data Sources:** GPS, Bluetooth, Wi-Fi, Accelerometer, CDR, App logs.
- ✅ **Eigenbehaviors:** Low-dimensional behavioral signatures via NMF (typically $k = 5$ to $10$).
- ✅ **Tie Strength Formula:** $w_{ij} = \frac{1}{T} \sum \delta_{ij}(t) \cdot \tau_{ij}(t)$.
- ✅ **Shannon Entropy:** $H(X) = -\sum p(x_i) \log_2 p(x_i)$, measured in **bits**.
- ✅ **Mutual Information:** $I(X;Y) = H(X) - H(X \vert Y)$.
- ✅ **Jaccard Similarity:** $J(A,B) = \vert A \cap B \vert / \vert A \cup B \vert$.
- ✅ **Network Density:** $D = 2E / [N(N-1)]$.
- ✅ **Privacy vs. Utility Trade-off:** Always mention in any applied question.
- ✅ **Key Application Domains:** Public health, urban planning, organizational science, marketing, epidemiology.
- ✅ **Pentland's Sociometer Insight:** Face-to-face interaction is the strongest predictor of team productivity.
- ✅ **NMF Decomposition:** $V \approx W \cdot H$, where $W$ = user-behavior membership, $H$ = eigenbehavior matrix.
- ✅ **Remember to compare $H$ with $H_{max} = \log_2(n)$** when interpreting entropy.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Context - Awareness
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECMT528 - CONCEPTS IN SOCIAL NETWORK ANALYSIS
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Context - Awareness

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Context** is any information that can be used to characterize the situation of an entity (person, place, or object) that is considered relevant to the interaction between a user and an application, including the user and the application themselves. *(Standardized definition per Dey & Abowd, 2000 — adopted in KTU SNA module framework)*

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Context-Awareness** is the capability of a system to gather information about its environment (the *context*) at any given time and adapt its behavior accordingly, providing highly relevant, situation-specific information and/or services to the user.

In the framework of **Social Network Analysis (SNA)**, context-awareness extends beyond a single user–device dyad. It encompasses the *relational*, *temporal*, *spatial*, and *activity-based* dimensions that govern the formation, evolution, and dissolution of social ties.

### 1.1.1 The Five W's of Context (Schilit's Taxonomy)

| Dimension | Symbol | Meaning in SNA |
|---|---|---|
| **Who** | Identity | User identity, friends, social ties |
| **What** | Activity | Task being performed, content being shared |
| **When** | Time | Temporal patterns, peak hours, session duration |
| **Where** | Location | Physical/virtual geo-location, venue |
| **Why** | Motivation | Underlying goal or intent of interaction |

> [!NOTE]
> KTU 2024 SNA module emphasizes that in a social network, **context** is not merely a passive descriptor but an *active driver* of tie strength, link prediction, and community detection outcomes.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!TIP]
> **Real-World Analogy — "The Smart Café"**
>
> Imagine you walk into your favorite café every morning at **8:00 AM**. The barista doesn't just hand you a menu; they immediately start preparing your *regular order* — a black coffee, no sugar, in a takeaway cup. Why? Because they are **context-aware**:
>
> - **When** → it's 8 AM (your daily routine).
> - **Who** → they recognize *you* (identity-based context).
> - **Where** → you're at the café counter (location context).
> - **What** → you typically order a black coffee (activity context).
> - **Why** → you're rushing to office (intent context).
>
> A **context-aware social network** behaves exactly the same way. Instead of serving generic feeds, it serves **personalized, situation-specific** content, friend suggestions, or recommendations based on the user's present context.

### 1.2.1 Why Context-Awareness Matters in SNA

- **Edge Formation**: Two users at the *same location + same time + same activity* are far more likely to form a social tie.
- **Edge Weighting**: Tie strength is contextually modulated (a "Work" tie behaves very differently from a "Family" tie).
- **Information Diffusion**: The spread of information in a network is heavily context-dependent (rumors spread faster in physical proximity).
- **Recommendation Quality**: Friend, content, and product recommendations improve dramatically with context integration.
- **Privacy & Trust**: Context defines *with whom* information may be safely shared.

---

## 1.3 Standard Metrics & Constants

| Metric | Notation | Description |
|---|---|---|
| Contextual Dimensions | $d$ | Number of independent context types considered |
| Context Vector | $\mathbf{C} = [c_1, c_2, \ldots, c_d]$ | Multi-dimensional representation of a context instance |
| Context Similarity | $S(C_x, C_y)$ | A scalar in $[0, 1]$ measuring contextual closeness |
| Context Entropy | $H(C)$ | Uncertainty of a context variable (Shannon entropy, in **bits**) |
| Context-Adaptive Score | $R_{u,i}^C$ | Recommendation score for user $u$ and item $i$ under context $C$ |

> [!NOTE]
> All context representations in modern SNA systems are **vector-valued, temporal, and probabilistic** in nature. The unit of entropy is the **bit**, and similarity is **dimensionless** in $[0, 1]$.

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Decomposition of Context-Aware Systems

A context-aware social network system performs **four sequential operations**:

### Step 1 — **Sensing / Acquisition**
- The system acquires raw contextual signals from heterogeneous sources.
- **Sensors**: GPS, Wi-Fi, Bluetooth beacons, accelerometers, microphone, calendar, social check-ins, etc.
- **Sources**: User devices, environment, social platform logs.

### Step 2 — **Modeling / Representation**
- The raw signals are structured into a formal context model.
- The model answers: *"How do I represent 'Anu is at a coffee shop with Binu at 8 AM'?"*
- Common models: key-value pairs, markup schemes, object-oriented, logic-based, **ontology-based (OWL/RDF)**.

### Step 3 — **Reasoning / Inference**
- The system *infers* higher-level, abstract context from low-level sensor data.
- Example: Low-level signal (GPS coordinates) $\rightarrow$ High-level context ("At Coffee Shop ☕").
- Methods: rule-based, fuzzy logic, Bayesian networks, **ontological reasoning (Description Logic)**.

### Step 4 — **Adaptation / Actuation**
- The system adapts its behavior — recommendation list, privacy filter, UI layout, notification priority.
- The *closed loop* between sensing and adaptation makes the system **context-aware**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Why-How Matrix**
>
> | | **Why?** | **How?** |
> |---|---|---|
> | **Sensing** | Capture user state | APIs, sensors, OS hooks |
> | **Modeling** | Standardize representation | Schema, ontology, vectors |
> | **Reasoning** | Derive hidden context | Rules, ML, probabilistic inference |
> | **Actuation** | Personalize experience | UI changes, recommendations |

---

## 2.2 Taxonomy of Context

### 2.2.1 By Layer

| Layer | Type | Example in SNA |
|---|---|---|
| **L1** | Physical | Location, noise, light, temperature |
| **L2** | Network | Bandwidth, latency, connectivity |
| **L3** | User | Profile, mood, activity, biometric data |
| **L4** | Social | Group membership, role, tie strength |
| **L5** | Application | Task, content type, service being used |

### 2.2.2 By Temporal Behaviour

- **Static context**: Slowly-changing (e.g., date of birth, hometown).
- **Dynamic context**: Rapidly-changing (e.g., current location, current activity).
- **Semi-dynamic context**: Changes over weeks/months (e.g., social cluster membership).

---

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following equations are exam-grade essentials. **Memorize the form, not just the symbol**.

| # | Concept | Formula | Variables | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Context Vector | $\mathbf{C} = [c_1, c_2, \ldots, c_d]$ | $c_i$ = value of $i$-th context dimension | Ordered tuple |
| 2 | Context Entropy | $H(C) = -\sum_{i=1}^{n} p(c_i)\log_2 p(c_i)$ | $p(c_i)$ = probability of context state | Measured in **bits** |
| 3 | Cosine Similarity of Contexts | $S(C_x, C_y) = \frac{\mathbf{C}_x \cdot \mathbf{C}_y}{\lVert\mathbf{C}_x\rVert \cdot \lVert\mathbf{C}_y\rVert}$ | $C_x, C_y$ = context vectors | $S \in [0, 1]$; use `\Vert` for norm |
| 4 | Joint Context Probability | $P(C_1, C_2) = P(C_1 \mid C_2) \cdot P(C_2)$ | Conditional probability | Bayes' rule foundation |
| 5 | Posterior (Bayesian) | $P(C \mid E) = \frac{P(E \mid C) \cdot P(C)}{P(E)}$ | $E$ = evidence | Context reasoning |
| 6 | Contextual Tie Strength | $w_{ij}^C = w_{ij} \cdot f(C_{ij})$ | $f(C_{ij})$ = context boost function | $w_{ij}$ = base weight |
| 7 | Context-Aware Recommendation | $R_{u,i}^{C} = R_{u,i} \cdot \alpha^{d(C, C_{u})}$ | $\alpha \in (0,1]$ decay, $d$ = distance | Exponential decay with context mismatch |
| 8 | Context Recall | $\text{Recall} = \frac{\text{TP}}{\text{TP} + \text{FN}}$ | Standard ML metric | Evaluates context detection |
| 9 | Context Precision | $\text{Precision} = \frac{\text{TP}}{\text{TP} + \text{FP}}$ | Standard ML metric | Evaluates false positives |
| 10 | Context F1-Score | $F_1 = 2 \cdot \frac{P \cdot R}{P + R}$ | $P$ = Precision, $R$ = Recall | Harmonic mean |

> [!WARNING]
> **Pipe Symbol Rule:** In any table cell, never write `|x|`. Always use `\vert x \vert` or `\mid x \mid` in LaTeX.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!NOTE]
> All derivations below are **exam-grade**, fully explicit, and follow KTU's "show every step" valuation policy. **No skipped algebra.**

---

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Contextual Tie Strength from Base Tie Weight

**Statement.** In a social network $G = (V, E)$ with base tie weights $w_{ij}$, the context-modulated tie strength is defined as:

$$
w_{ij}^{C} \;=\; w_{ij} \cdot f(C_{ij})
$$

**Derivation of the Boost Function $f(C_{ij})$:**

Assume the context vector difference between two users $i$ and $j$ is:

$$
\Delta\mathbf{C}_{ij} \;=\; \mathbf{C}_i - \mathbf{C}_j \;\in\; \mathbb{R}^{d}
$$

The *context mismatch distance* is the L2 norm:

$$
d(C_i, C_j) \;=\; \lVert \Delta\mathbf{C}_{ij} \rVert_2 \;=\; \sqrt{\sum_{k=1}^{d}(c_{i,k} - c_{j,k})^2}
$$

We require $f$ to be a **monotonically decreasing** function of $d$, with $f(0) = 1$ (perfect context match = full boost) and $f(\infty) = 0$ (total mismatch = no boost). The natural choice is the **exponential decay**:

$$
f(C_{ij}) \;=\; e^{-\lambda \cdot d(C_i, C_j)}, \quad \lambda > 0
$$

**Substituting back:**

$$
\boxed{\,w_{ij}^{C} \;=\; w_{ij} \cdot e^{-\lambda \cdot \sqrt{\sum_{k=1}^{d}(c_{i,k} - c_{j,k})^2}}\,}
$$

**Interpretation:**
- If $i$ and $j$ are in identical context ($d=0$), then $w_{ij}^{C} = w_{ij}$ (no penalty).
- If they are in completely different context ($d \to \infty$), then $w_{ij}^{C} \to 0$ (contextual tie vanishes).
- $\lambda$ is the **sensitivity parameter**; large $\lambda$ makes tie strength highly sensitive to context mismatch.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Bayesian Context Reasoning

**Problem Setup.** A user's *activity* context $A$ is not directly observable. We only observe *sensor evidence* $E$ (e.g., accelerometer, microphone, calendar). We wish to compute $P(A \mid E)$.

**Bayes' Theorem:**

$$
P(A \mid E) \;=\; \frac{P(E \mid A) \cdot P(A)}{P(E)}
$$

**Step-by-step Expansion:**

**Step 1.** The denominator (evidence) is computed by marginalizing over all possible activities:

$$
P(E) \;=\; \sum_{a \in \mathcal{A}} P(E \mid A = a) \cdot P(A = a)
$$

**Step 2.** Expand the posterior:

$$
P(A \mid E) \;=\; \frac{P(E \mid A) \cdot P(A)}{\sum_{a \in \mathcal{A}} P(E \mid a) \cdot P(a)}
$$

**Step 3.** Assume two activities: $A = \{\text{Working}, \text{Resting}\}$. Numerical illustration:
- Prior: $P(\text{Working}) = 0.6$, $P(\text{Resting}) = 0.4$.
- Likelihood from sensor: $P(E \mid \text{Working}) = 0.8$, $P(E \mid \text{Resting}) = 0.3$.

**Step 4.** Compute denominator:

$$
P(E) = (0.8)(0.6) + (0.3)(0.4) = 0.48 + 0.12 = 0.60
$$

**Step 5.** Compute posteriors:

$$
P(\text{Working} \mid E) = \frac{(0.8)(0.6)}{0.60} = \frac{0.48}{0.60} = 0.80
$$

$$
P(\text{Resting} \mid E) = \frac{(0.3)(0.4)}{0.60} = \frac{0.12}{0.60} = 0.20
$$

**Conclusion.** The system infers that the user is **Working with 80% confidence**.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Context-Weighted Recommendation Score

**Setup.** A standard recommendation score $R_{u,i}$ (e.g., from collaborative filtering) is to be modulated by context distance $d(C_u, C_{\text{target}})$.

**Modulation formula:**

$$
R_{u,i}^{C} \;=\; R_{u,i} \cdot \alpha^{\,d(C_u, C_{\text{target}})}, \quad 0 < \alpha \le 1
$$

**Derivation of why $\alpha^d$:**

**Step 1.** Require: if $d = 0$ (perfect match), $R_{u,i}^{C} = R_{u,i}$ (no penalty).
$\Rightarrow \alpha^{0} = 1$. ✓

**Step 2.** Require: monotonic decrease in $d$ for fixed $\alpha < 1$.
$\Rightarrow \frac{d}{dd}\alpha^{d} = \alpha^{d}\ln\alpha < 0$ since $\alpha < 1$. ✓

**Step 3.** Require: $R_{u,i}^{C} \to 0$ as $d \to \infty$.
$\Rightarrow \alpha^{d} \to 0$ for $\alpha < 1$. ✓

**Step 4.** Worked example:
- Base score $R_{u,i} = 0.9$, $\alpha = 0.8$.
- $d = 0$: $R^C = 0.9 \cdot (0.8)^{0} = 0.900$.
- $d = 1$: $R^C = 0.9 \cdot (0.8)^{1} = 0.720$.
- $d = 3$: $R^C = 0.9 \cdot (0.8)^{3} = 0.9 \cdot 0.512 = 0.4608$.

$$
\boxed{\,R_{u,i}^{C} \;=\; R_{u,i} \cdot \alpha^{\,d(C_u, C_{\text{target}})}\,}
$$

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Context Entropy and Information Content

**Setup.** A context variable $C$ has $n$ possible states $\{c_1, c_2, \ldots, c_n\}$ with probabilities $p_1, p_2, \ldots, p_n$.

**Shannon Entropy:**

$$
H(C) \;=\; -\sum_{i=1}^{n} p(c_i) \cdot \log_2 p(c_i)
$$

**Proof of properties (selected):**

**Property 1: $H(C) \ge 0$.** Since $0 \le p(c_i) \le 1$, we have $\log_2 p(c_i) \le 0$, so $-p(c_i)\log_2 p(c_i) \ge 0$. Summing non-negative terms gives $H(C) \ge 0$.

**Property 2: Maximum at uniform distribution.** Using Lagrange multipliers with constraint $\sum p_i = 1$:

$$
\mathcal{L} = -\sum p_i \log_2 p_i + \lambda\left(\sum p_i - 1\right)
$$

Setting $\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial p_i} = 0$:

$$
-\log_2 p_i - \frac{1}{\ln 2} + \lambda = 0 \;\Rightarrow\; p_i = 2^{\lambda - 1/\ln 2} = \text{const}
$$

Since $\sum p_i = 1$, we get $p_i = \frac{1}{n}$ for all $i$, giving $H_{\max} = \log_2 n$ **bits**.

**Worked Example:** $C$ has 4 states with probabilities $(0.5, 0.25, 0.125, 0.125)$:

$$
H(C) = -(0.5)\log_2(0.5) - (0.25)\log_2(0.25) - 2(0.125)\log_2(0.125)
$$

$$
H(C) = 0.5 + 0.5 + 0.75 = 1.75 \text{ bits}
$$

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Context-Awareness Across SNA Tasks

| SNA Task | Role of Context | Most Useful Formula | Difficulty |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Friend Recommendation** | Co-location & co-activity boost | $w_{ij}^{C} = w_{ij} \cdot e^{-\lambda d}$ | ★★★ |
| **Link Prediction** | Context as a feature vector | $\mathbf{C} = [c_1, c_2, \ldots, c_d]$ | ★★★★ |
| **Community Detection** | Context as cluster-cohesion metric | $H(C) = -\sum p(c_i)\log_2 p(c_i)$ | ★★★★ |
| **Information Diffusion** | Context as transmission channel | $P(A \mid E) = \frac{P(E \mid A)P(A)}{P(E)}$ | ★★★ |
| **Influence Maximization** | Context-aware seed selection | $R_{u,i}^{C} = R_{u,i}\alpha^{d}$ | ★★★★★ |
| **Sentiment Analysis** | Context as disambiguator | Joint $P(C_1, C_2)$ | ★★ |
