# KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Style]**

> Define **Computational Trust** in the context of an online social network. List any **four** distinct types of trust recognised in the KTU PECMT528 syllabus.

#### Model Answer (3 marks)
Computational trust is the formal, algorithmic representation of a trustor node's belief about a trustee node's future behaviour, expressed as a scalar or vector value computable from interaction history, network structure, and external attestations.

Four distinct types (1 mark each — any 4):
1. **Direct (Experiential) Trust** — from first-hand interactions.
2. **Indirect (Propagated) Trust** — inferred via intermediaries.
3. **Reputation** — aggregated community-wide perception.
4. **System / Institutional Trust** — trust in the platform itself.
5. **Dispositional Trust** — user-level baseline tendency to trust.

---

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam — June 2024 Style]**

> State the **Beta Reputation** formula. Using it, compute the trust value of node B as perceived by node A, given that A has recorded **12 satisfactory** and **3 unsatisfactory** interactions with B.

#### Model Answer
**Formula (1 mark):**

$$T_{A \rightarrow B} = \frac{S_{AB} + 1}{S_{AB} + U_{AB} + 2}$$

**Substitution and calculation (2 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{A \rightarrow B} &= \frac{12 + 1}{12 + 3 + 2} = \frac{13}{17} \approx 0.7647
\end{aligned}
$$

**Interpretation (bonus):** A trust value of $\approx 0.76$ indicates a *favourable but not absolute* trust — A should transact with B but with moderate confidence.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning (Module 4 — Trust)**
> Students most commonly **lose marks** on this topic for the following reasons:
> 1. **Conflating trust with reputation** — they are related but *not* identical. Trust is dyadic ($a \to b$); reputation is global (community view of $b$).
> 2. **Forgetting the "+2 smoothing"** in the Beta formula — writing $\frac{S}{S+U}$ instead of $\frac{S+1}{S+U+2}$ costs a mark.
> 3. **Omitting the pre-trusted vector $\vec{p}$** in EigenTrust derivations — without it, the model is not Sybil-resilient, and the examiner will deduct for "incomplete defence."
> 4. **Using the wrong combining operator** for trust propagation — strict multiplication is for single chains; weighted averaging is for multi-path or reputation aggregation. Mixing them up signals conceptual confusion.
> 5. **Skipping the damping term** in propagation derivations — examiners *expect* the $d^{L}$ factor for any path of length $> 1$.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) — *[Internal Choice Option A]*

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 / Model Paper Style]**

> **(a)** Explain the concept of **Trust Propagation** in an online social network. Derive the **damped multiplicative** trust propagation formula for an $L$-hop path and justify why damping is mathematically necessary. **\[7 Marks\]**
>
> **(b)** Describe the **EigenTrust** algorithm. State its update rule, explain the role of the pre-trusted vector $\vec{p}$, and show one full iteration of the power method on the trust matrix below. **\[7 Marks\]**
>
> $$M = \begin{pmatrix} 0 & 0.5 & 0.5 \\ 0.6 & 0 & 0.4 \\ 0.3 & 0.7 & 0 \end{pmatrix}, \quad \vec{p} = (0, 1, 0)^{T}, \quad \epsilon = 0.2, \quad \vec{t}^{(0)} = (1/3, 1/3, 1/3)^{T}$$

#### Model Answer

### (a) Trust Propagation & Damped Multiplication — 7 marks

**Definition (2 marks):** Trust propagation is the process of inferring a trust value between two nodes $a$ and $b$ that have *not* directly interacted, by aggregating trust values along one or more indirect paths connecting them in the social graph.

**Derivation (3 marks):** For a single path $a = v_0 \to v_1 \to \dots \to v_L = b$, the strict multiplicative rule is:

$$T_{a \rightarrow b}^{\text{strict}} = \prod_{i=0}^{L-1} T_{v_i \to v_{i+1}}$$

This is justified by the **independence assumption** — each link is treated as an independent Bernoulli event, and joint probability is the product. To prevent trust inflation / starvation across long chains, we multiply by a decay factor $d^{\,L}$ with $0 < d < 1$:

$$T_{a \rightarrow b}^{\text{damped}} = \left(\prod_{i=0}^{L-1} T_{v_i \to v_{i+1}}\right) \cdot d^{\,L}$$

**Justification of damping (2 marks):**
1. **Boundedness** — without damping, the product $0.9^{30} \approx 0.042$ is so small that any node more than 10 hops away is effectively "untrusted by default," which is unrealistic.
2. **Semantic length penalty** — a long chain of intermediaries introduces more opportunities for error, dishonesty, or context drift. The decay $d^{\,L}$ mathematically encodes the principle that "trust decays with distance."
3. **Resistance to manipulation** — attackers who create long artificial chains of fake accounts to inflate a target's trust are penalised exponentially in $L$.

---

### (b) EigenTrust Algorithm — 7 marks

**Description (2 marks):** EigenTrust is a global reputation algorithm that computes a single trust score for every node in a network by running a PageRank-like power iteration on the row-normalised local trust matrix. It was proposed by Kamvar, Schlosser, and Garcia-Molina (2003) for P2P networks.

**Update rule (2 marks):**

$$\vec{t}^{(i+1)} = (1 - \epsilon)\, M^{T} \vec{t}^{(i)} + \epsilon\, \vec{p}$$

where $M$ is the row-normalised local trust matrix, $\vec{p}$ is the pre-trusted anchor distribution, and $\epsilon \in (0,1)$ is the damping factor.

**Role of $\vec{p}$ (1 mark):** The pre-trusted vector $\vec{p}$ injects known-good nodes into the iteration, ensuring that a Sybil cluster (a tight group of mutually-trusting malicious nodes) cannot become the dominant eigenvector. It is the *Sybil-resilience* mechanism.

**Numerical iteration (2 marks):** With $\vec{p} = (0, 1, 0)^{T}$ and $\epsilon = 0.2$:

**Step 1:** Compute $M^{T} \vec{t}^{(0)}$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
M^{T} \vec{t}^{(0)} &= \begin{pmatrix} 0 \cdot \frac{1}{3} + 0.6 \cdot \frac{1}{3} + 0.3 \cdot \frac{1}{3} \\ 0.5 \cdot \frac{1}{3} + 0 \cdot \frac{1}{3} + 0.7 \cdot \frac{1}{3} \\ 0.5 \cdot \frac{1}{3} + 0.4 \cdot \frac{1}{3} + 0 \cdot \frac{1}{3} \end{pmatrix} = \begin{pmatrix} 0.300 \\ 0.400 \\ 0.300 \end{pmatrix}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2:** Apply the update rule.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\vec{t}^{(1)} &= 0.8 \cdot \begin{pmatrix} 0.300 \\ 0.400 \\ 0.300 \end{pmatrix} + 0.2 \cdot \begin{pmatrix} 0 \\ 1 \\ 0 \end{pmatrix} = \begin{pmatrix} 0.240 \\ 0.520 \\ 0.240 \end{pmatrix}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Interpretation:** After one iteration, node 2 has emerged as the most globally trusted node (score $0.520$), which is consistent with $\vec{p}$ placing its anchor mass on node 2. Further iterations will continue to redistribute trust, with node 2's dominance persisting due to the pre-trust bias and its incoming links from nodes 1 and 3.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — *[Internal Choice Option B]*

**[KTU University Exam — June 2024 / Model Paper Style]**

> **(a)** Differentiate between **Trust** and **Reputation** in an online social network. Describe the **Beta Reputation System** with its formula, and explain the role of the "+1 smoothing" in the numerator and denominator. **\[7 Marks\]**
>
> **(b)** List and briefly explain **four major attacks** on online trust systems. For each attack, propose a suitable counter-mechanism. **\[7 Marks\]**

#### Model Answer

### (a) Trust vs. Reputation & Beta Reputation System — 7 marks

**Distinction (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Trust | Reputation |
|---|---|---|
| Scope | **Dyadic** (one trustor, one trustee) | **Global** (community view of one node) |
| Source | Direct + indirect experience | Aggregated opinions of many nodes |
| Symmetry | **Asymmetric** ($T_{ab} \neq T_{ba}$ in general) | Generally symmetric across the community |
| Computation | Local, per-edge | Requires aggregation over the network |
| Personal vs. Public | Personal belief | Public perception |

**Beta Reputation formula (2 marks):**

$$T_{A \rightarrow B} = \frac{S_{AB} + 1}{S_{AB} + U_{AB} + 2}$$

This is the posterior mean of a $\text{Beta}(S+1, U+1)$ distribution, derived via Bayesian update with a uniform prior.

**Role of "+1 smoothing" (2 marks):**
- In the **numerator**, "+1" is the prior pseudo-count of successes, preventing $T = 0$ when $S = 0$. Without it, a single negative experience would make $T = 0/2 = 0$ (impossible to recover from).
- In the **denominator**, "+2" is the total prior pseudo-counts ($1 + 1$), reflecting the uniform Beta(1,1) prior. It guarantees $T < 1$ even when $S + U$ is large and $U = 0$, which is correct: no amount of finite evidence justifies *complete* certainty.
- **Joint effect:** The smoothing implements Laplace's rule of succession, making the trust value *principled* even with very sparse data — exactly the regime in which new online interactions begin.

---

### (b) Four Major Attacks on Trust Systems — 7 marks

**1. Self-Promotion Attack (1.5 marks):** A malicious node creates fake identities (or uses sock puppets) to give itself positive ratings, artificially inflating its reputation.

**Counter-mechanism:** Use the **pre-trusted anchor vector** $\vec{p}$ in EigenTrust, and apply **identity persistence cost** (e.g., require minimum transaction history before a node's votes count).

**2. Whitewashing Attack (1.5 marks):** A node whose reputation has been damaged simply discards the old identity and creates a fresh one, effectively resetting its trust to a neutral value.

**Counter-mechanism:** Impose an **identity acquisition cost** (e.g., CAPTCHA, KYC verification, or a monetary deposit) that makes repeated re-registration economically unprofitable.

**3. Bad-Mouthing Attack (1.5 marks):** A malicious node gives **negative** ratings to honest, well-reputed competitors to damage their standing.

**Counter-mechanism:** **Weighted reputation** — discount the votes of any recommender whose own trust score is below a threshold. Sybil-resilient systems like EigenTrust automatically limit the impact of untrusted recommenders through the trust-matrix multiplication.

**4. Ballot-Stuffing Attack (1.5 marks):** A colluding group of malicious nodes all give each other positive ratings, forming a Sybil ring that inflates each member's reputation collectively.

**Counter-mechanism:** **Sparse trust link requirements** — restrict trust propagation to links that have been validated by at least one pre-trusted node. EigenTrust's $\vec{p}$ anchor is the canonical defence: the Sybil ring cannot generate outgoing trust from the pre-trusted subspace, so its members' global trust remains low.

**5. (Bonus, optional) On-Off Attack (0.5 marks):** A node behaves honestly for a while to build trust, then defrauds once. **Counter-mechanism:** Time-decayed trust that weights recent evidence more heavily than old.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Trust in Online Environment**

- [x] **Trust** is *dyadic and personal*; **Reputation** is *global and aggregated*. Never use them interchangeably.
- [x] The **Beta Reputation formula** is $T = \frac{S+1}{S+U+2}$ — the "+2" is from the uniform prior, *not* a typo.
- [x] **Confidence** in a trust value grows with $S + U$; it is computed as $C = \frac{S+U}{S+U+2}$.
- [x] **Trust propagation** along a single path uses **multiplication**; across multiple paths, it uses **weighted averaging** with damping.
- [x] **Damping factor** $d^{L}$ (with $0 < d < 1$) is *mandatory* for paths of length $L > 1$ — it prevents trust starvation and resists chain-based manipulation.
- [x] **EigenTrust** is mathematically **PageRank on the trust graph**; its update rule is $\vec{t}^{(i+1)} = (1-\epsilon) M^{T} \vec{t}^{(i)} + \epsilon \vec{p}$.
- [x] The **pre-trusted vector** $\vec{p}$ is the *Sybil-defence* — it anchors the iteration in a known-good subspace.
- [x] **Four canonical attacks**: Self-Promotion, Whitewashing, Bad-Mouthing, Ballot-Stuffing. Each has a known counter-mechanism (anchors, identity cost, weighted reputation, sparse trust links).
- [x] **Hybrid Trust Fusion**: $T_{\text{final}} = \alpha D + \beta P + \gamma R$ with $\alpha + \beta + \gamma = 1$ — direct, propagated, and reputation components.
- [x] **Risk** is $1 - T \cdot C$; a decision is taken when Risk falls below a threshold.
- [x] **Real-world deployments**: Amazon/eBay ratings, EigenTrust in P2P, Zero-Trust Architecture in cybersecurity, trust-weighted collaborative filtering in recommenders.
- [x] **Common cold-start proxy**: Jaccard / Cosine similarity of neighbour sets, used when behavioural data is absent.
- [x] **Exam pitfalls**: missing the "+2 smoothing", omitting $\vec{p}$ from EigenTrust, confusing multiplication (chains) with averaging (paths).

> **End of Module 4 — Trust in Online Environment Notes**
> *Prepared under the KTU 2024 Scheme (NEP 2020 aligned), aligned to PECMT528 Course Outcomes and Revised Bloom's Taxonomy (L2–L5: Understand, Apply, Analyse, Evaluate).*

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Trust Models Based on Subjective Logic
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PECMT528 - CONCEPTS IN SOCIAL NETWORK ANALYSIS > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Trust Models Based on Subjective Logic # Module 4 — Trust Models Based on Subjective Logic ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Definition **Subjective Logic (SL)** is a type of probabilistic logic that explicitly models *uncertainty* and *subjective beliefs* about propositions. It was formalized by **Audun Jøsang** as a framework for representing and reasoning with trust relationships in networks where evidence is incomplete or conflicting. In SL, a **subjective opinion** about a proposition $x$ is expressed as a quadruple: $$\omega_x = (b_x, d_x, u_x, a_x)$$ where: - $b_x$ → **belief mass** (evidence supporting $x$) - $d_x$ → **disbelief mass** (evidence refuting $x$) - $u_x$ → **uncertainty mass** (lack of evidence) - $a_x$ → **base rate** (a-priori probability in absence of evidence) The components satisfy the additivity constraint: $$b_x + d_x + u_x = 1, \quad b_x, d_x, u_x, u_x \in [0,1], \quad a_x \in [0,1]$$ The **expected probability** (projected probability) of $x$ is computed as: $$E(x) = b_x + a_x \cdot u_x$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Subjective Logic is the *de-facto* mathematical framework for trust modeling in decentralized networks, recommender systems, and reputation systems. It is a high-weight topic for both Part A (3-mark definitions) and Part B (14-mark derivations). ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Overview > [!NOTE] > **Real-world Analogy — "The Weather Forecaster":** > > Imagine two weather forecasters predicting rain: > - **Forecaster A:** "It will rain tomorrow with **90% confidence** because I have analyzed 10 cloud patterns." → High belief, low uncertainty. > - **Forecaster B:** "It might rain, **I am not sure** because I have very little data." → Low belief, high uncertainty. > > Classical probability cannot distinguish these two cases, because both might give $P(\text{rain}) = 0.5$. Subjective Logic introduces **uncertainty as a first-class citizen**, allowing us to express the *quality* of evidence alongside the *quantity* of belief. In **social network trust**, this is critical: if Alice says "I trust Bob", we need to know: 1. **How strongly** she believes Bob is trustworthy ($b$) 2. **How strongly** she disbelieves it ($d$) 3. **How uncertain** she is about her own belief ($u$) 4. **What default assumption** she would make in absence of evidence ($a$) ### 1.3 Why Subjective Logic (and not just Bayesian Probability)? | Aspect | Bayesian Probability | Subjective Logic | |---|---|---| | Models uncertainty | Indirectly (via priors) | Explicitly (via $u$) | | Handles ignorance | Poorly | Naturally | | Computes trust fusion | Complex integrals | Closed-form operators | | Reasoning with conflicting sources | Difficult | Built-in consensus/averaging | | Suitable for trust propagation | Limited | Native (transitivity + discounting) | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Standard metrics in SL:** > - **Atomicity (relative atomicity):** $\alpha = 2$ for binary propositions, $\alpha = N$ for $N$-ary propositions. > - **Vacuously true opinion:** $\omega = (0, 0, 1, a)$ — total uncertainty. > - **Absolutely true opinion:** $\omega = (1, 0, 0, a)$ — absolute belief. --- ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 The Opinion Triangle A subjective opinion $\omega = (b, d, u)$ with $a$ as the base rate can be visualized as a point in the **opinion triangle** (also called the *Jøsang triangle* or *subjective logic simplex*). - The three vertices correspond to: - Top: **Belief = 1** (absolute belief) - Bottom-left: **Disbelief = 1** (absolute disbelief) - Bottom-right: **Uncertainty = 1** (vacuous opinion) - The base rate $a$ defines a point on the bottom edge of the triangle. ### 2.2 Trust Modeling: A Directed Graph Perspective A **trust network** is modeled as a directed graph $G = (V, E)$ where: - Vertices $V$ → agents - Edges $E$ → trust relationships - Each edge $(A, B)$ carries an opinion $\omega_{B}^{A}$ (A's trust in B) Trust propagation uses two key operators: 1. **Discounting (transitivity):** "Alice trusts Bob, Bob trusts Carol → how much does Alice trust Carol?" 2. **Fusion (combination):** "Alice receives recommendations about Carol from multiple sources → how does she combine them?" ### 2.3 Discounting Operator (Transitivity of Trust) If agent $X$ has opinion $\omega_Y^X = (b_Y^X, d_Y^X, u_Y^X, a_Y^X)$ about agent $Y$, and $Y$ has opinion $\omega_Z^Y$ about $Z$, then $X$'s *discounted* opinion of $Z$ via $Y$ is: $$\omega_Z^{X \circ Y} = (b_Z^{X \circ Y}, d_Z^{X \circ Y}, u_Z^{X \circ Y}, a_Z^{X \circ Y})$$ where: $$ \begin{aligned} b_Z^{X \circ Y} &= b_Y^X \cdot b_Z^Y \\ d_Z^{X \circ Y} &= b_Y^X \cdot d_Z^Y \\ u_Z^{X \circ Y} &= d_Y^X + u_Y^X + b_Y^X \cdot u_Z^Y \\ a_Z^{X \circ Y} &= a_Z^Y \end{aligned} $$ > [!NOTE] > **Intuition:** Uncertainty grows as trust propagates through a chain. The further away the recommender, the more uncertain the trust becomes. ### 2.4 Cumulative Fusion Operator (Cumulative Evidence) If two independent sources $Y_1$ and $Y_2$ provide opinions about the same proposition $x$, the **cumulative fusion** combines them as if the evidence were pooled: $$ \begin{aligned} b_x^{Y_1 \diamond Y_2} &= \frac{(b_x^{Y_1} u_x^{Y_2} + b_x^{Y_2} u_x^{Y_1})}{(u_x^{Y_1} + u_x^{Y_2} - u_x^{Y_1} u_x^{Y_2})} \\ d_x^{Y_1 \diamond Y_2} &= \frac{(d_x^{Y_1} u_x^{Y_2} + d_x^{Y_2} u_x^{Y_1})}{(u_x^{Y_1} + u_x^{Y_2} - u_x^{Y_1} u_x^{Y_2})} \\ u_x^{Y_1 \diamond Y_2} &= \frac{u_x^{Y_1} u_x^{Y_2}}{u_x^{Y_1} + u_x^{Y_2} - u_x^{Y_1} u_x^{Y_2}} \end{aligned} $$ > [!IMPORTANT] > This is appropriate when sources observe **the same event** (e.g., two witnesses testifying about the same incident). ### 2.5 Averaging Fusion Operator (Independent Beliefs) If sources provide **independent opinions** (e.g., two different product reviews), the **averaging fusion** is used: $$ \begin{aligned} b_x^{Y_1 \underline{\diamond} Y_2} &= \frac{b_x^{Y_1} u_x^{Y_2} + b_x^{Y_2} u_x^{Y_1} - (b_x^{Y_1} + b_x^{Y_2}) \cdot \gamma}{1 - \gamma} \\ d_x^{Y_1 \underline{\diamond} Y_2} &= \frac{d_x^{Y_1} u_x^{Y_2} + d_x^{Y_2} u_x^{Y_1} - (d_x^{Y_1} + d_x^{Y_2}) \cdot \gamma}{1 - \gamma} \\ u_x^{Y_1 \underline{\diamond} Y_2} &= \frac{u_x^{Y_1} u_x^{Y_2}}{1 - \gamma} \end{aligned} $$ where: $$\gamma = b_x^{Y_1} u_x^{Y_2} + b_x^{Y_2} u_x^{Y_1} + d_x^{Y_1} u_x^{Y_2} + d_x^{Y_2} u_x^{Y_1} - (b_x^{Y_1} + d_x^{Y_1}) u_x^{Y_2} - (b_x^{Y_2} + d_x^{Y_2}) u_x^{Y_1}$$ ### 2.6 Consensus Operator (Combining Equally-Weighted Independent Opinions) For two independent sources giving opinions about $x$ with base rate $a_x$: $$ \begin{aligned} b_x^{Y_1 \widetilde{\diamond} Y_2} &= \frac{b_x^{Y_1} u_x^{Y_2} + b_x^{Y_2} u_x^{Y_1}}{u_x^{Y_1} + u_x^{Y_2} - 2 u_x^{Y_1} u_x^{Y_2}} \quad \text{(when } u_x^{Y_1}, u_x^{Y_2} < 1\text{)} \\ d_x^{Y_1 \widetilde{\diamond} Y_2} &= \frac{d_x^{Y_1} u_x^{Y_2} + d_x^{Y_2} u_x^{Y_1}}{u_x^{Y_1} + u_x^{Y_2} - 2 u_x^{Y_1} u_x^{Y_2}} \\ u_x^{Y_1 \widetilde{\diamond} Y_2} &= \frac{2 u_x^{Y_1} u_x^{Y_2}}{u_x^{Y_1} + u_x^{Y_2} - 2 u_x^{Y_1} u_x^{Y_2}} \end{aligned} $$ ### 2.7 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | Concept | Formula | Domain / Range | When to Use | |---|---|---|---| | Opinion | $\omega_x = (b_x, d_x, u_x, a_x)$ | $b, d, u \in [0,1]; a \in [0,1]$ | Representing subjective belief | | Additivity | $b + d + u = 1$ | Constraint | Always | | Expected Probability | $E(x) = b + a \cdot u$ | $E \in [0,1]$ | When converting to classical probability | | Discounting | $b_Z^{X \circ Y} = b_Y^X \cdot b_Z^Y$ | $b \in [0,1]$ | Trust chain: A→B→C | | Discounting uncertainty | $u_Z^{X \circ Y} = d_Y^X + u_Y^X + b_Y^X \cdot u_Z^Y$ | $u \in [0,1]$ | Trust chain | | Cumulative fusion $b$ | $\dfrac{b_1 u_2 + b_2 u_1}{u_1 + u_2 - u_1 u_2}$ | $b \in [0,1]$ | Same event, multiple sources | | Cumulative fusion $u$ | $\dfrac{u_1 u_2}{u_1 + u_2 - u_1 u_2}$ | $u \in [0,1]$ | Same event, multiple sources | | Path length penalty | $u$ grows with each hop | Always $\geq 0$ | Long trust chains degrade | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Use `\vert` or `\mid` for absolute value in tables (NOT `|`)**: e.g., write $\vert x \vert$ instead of $|x|$ to avoid breaking markdown table syntax. ### 2.8 Real-World Utility in Engineering - **P2P Networks (e.g., blockchain trust):** Peers assess the trustworthiness of unknown nodes using path-based subjective opinions. - **Recommender Systems:** Combine user ratings weighted by trust opinions. - **Cybersecurity:** Model the trust level of a software component or sender. - **IoT & Edge Computing:** Devices with limited evidence use $u$ to express their confidence in a measurement. - **Multi-Agent Systems:** Agents negotiate and form coalitions using SL-derived trust. --- ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation 1: Discounting Operator (Intuitive Walkthrough) > [!NOTE] > **Scenario:** Alice ($X$) has some trust in Bob ($Y$), and Bob has some trust in Carol ($Z$). Find Alice's trust in Carol via Bob. **Step 1 — Define Alice's opinion of Bob:** $$\omega_Y^X = (b_Y^X, d_Y^X, u_Y^X, a_Y^X)$$ **Step 2 — Define Bob's opinion of Carol:** $$\omega_Z^Y = (b_Z^Y, d_Z^Y, u_Z^Y, a_Z^Y)$$ **Step 3 — Discounting of belief (transitive belief propagation):** Alice should believe Carol is trustworthy *only to the extent* that Alice believes Bob is trustworthy, multiplied by how much Bob believes Carol is trustworthy. $$b_Z^{X \circ Y} = b_Y^X \cdot b_Z^Y$$ **Step 4 — Discounting of disbelief:** Similarly, the disbelief is scaled by Alice's belief in Bob: $$d_Z^{X \circ Y} = b_Y^X \cdot d_Z^Y$$ **Step 5 — Discounting of uncertainty:** This is the "leftover" probability mass. It consists of: - $d_Y^X$ (Alice disbelieves Bob → she has no reason to trust Bob's opinion) - $u_Y^X$ (Alice is uncertain about Bob → she cannot fully rely on his opinion) - $b_Y^X \cdot u_Z^Y$ (Alice trusts Bob, but Bob himself is uncertain about Carol) $$u_Z^{X \circ Y} = d_Y^X + u_Y^X + b_Y^X \cdot u_Z^Y$$ **Step 6 — Verify additivity:** $$ \begin{aligned} b_Z^{X \circ Y} + d_Z^{X \circ Y} + u_Z^{X \circ Y} &= b_Y^X \cdot b_Z^Y + b_Y^X \cdot d_Z^Y + d_Y^X + u_Y^X + b_Y^X \cdot u_Z^Y \\ &= b_Y^X (b_Z^Y + d_Z^Y + u_Z^Y) + d_Y^X + u_Y^X \\ &= b_Y^X \cdot 1 + d_Y^X + u_Y^X \\ &= (b_Y^X + d_Y^X + u_Y^X) = 1 \quad \checkmark \end{aligned} $$ This confirms the constraint is satisfied. ### 3.2 Derivation 2: Cumulative Fusion of Evidence > [!NOTE] > **Scenario:** Two sources $Y_1, Y_2$ observe the *same* event. Their observations are independent. Combine the evidence. **Step 1 — Let observation counts be** $r_i$ (positive), $s_i$ (negative), for $i \in \{1, 2\}$. Then belief, disbelief, uncertainty are defined as: $$b_i = \frac{r_i}{r_i + s_i + 2}, \quad d_i = \frac{s_i}{r_i + s_i + 2}, \quad u_i = \frac{2}{r_i + s_i + 2}$$ (with relative atomicity $\alpha = 2$ for binary propositions). **Step 2 — Pool the evidence:** Total evidence after pooling is $r_1 + r_2$ positive and $s_1 + s_2$ negative. $$b_{12} = \frac{r_1 + r_2}{(r_1 + r_2) + (s_1 + s_2) + 2}$$ **Step 3 — Express in terms of $b_i, d_i, u_i$:** From Step 1: $$r_i = \frac{2 b_i}{u_i}, \quad s_i = \frac{2 d_i}{u_i}$$ **Step 4 — Substitute:** $$ \begin{aligned} b_{12} &= \frac{\frac{2b_1}{u_1} + \frac{2b_2}{u_2}}{\frac{2b_1}{u_1} + \frac{2b_2}{u_2} + \frac{2d_1}{u_1} + \frac{2d_2}{u_2} + 2} \\ &= \frac{\frac{b_1}{u_1} + \frac{b_2}{u_2}}{\frac{b_1 + d_1}{u_1} + \frac{b_2 + d_2}{u_2} + 1} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 5 — Use $b + d = 1 - u$:** $$ \begin{aligned} b_{12} &= \frac{\frac{b_1}{u_1} + \frac{b_2}{u_2}}{\frac{1 - u_1}{u_1} + \frac{1 - u_2}{u_2} + 1} \\ &= \frac{\frac{b_1}{u_1} + \frac{b_2}{u_2}}{\frac{1}{u_1} + \frac{1}{u_2} - 1} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 6 — Multiply numerator and denominator by $u_1 u_2$:** $$ \begin{aligned} b_{12} &= \frac{b_1 u_2 + b_2 u_1}{u_2 + u_1 - u_1 u_2} = \frac{b_1 u_2 + b_2 u_1}{u_1 + u_2 - u_1 u_2} \quad \blacksquare \end{aligned} $$ The derivation for $d$ follows symmetrically, and $u_{12} = 1 - b_{12} - d_{12}$ yields the cumulative uncertainty formula. ### 3.3 Numerical Worked Example: Discounting > [!NOTE] > **Problem:** Alice has opinion $\omega_B^A = (0.6, 0.2, 0.2, 0.5)$ about Bob. Bob has opinion $\omega_C^B = (0.8, 0.1, 0.1, 0.5)$ about Carol. Compute Alice's opinion of Carol. **Step 1 — Identify the values:** - $b_B^A = 0.6, \; d_B^A = 0.2, \; u_B^A = 0.2$ - $b_C^B = 0.8, \; d_C^B = 0.1, \; u_C^B = 0.1$ - $a_C^B = 0.5$ **Step 2 — Apply discounting formulas:** $$ \begin{aligned} b_C^{A \circ B} &= b_B^A \cdot b_C^B = 0.6 \times 0.8 = 0.48 \\ d_C^{A \circ B} &= b_B^A \cdot d_C^B = 0.6 \times 0.1 = 0.06 \\ u_C^{A \circ B} &= d_B^A + u_B^A + b_B^A \cdot u_C^B = 0.2 + 0.2 + 0.6 \times 0.1 = 0.46 \\ a_C^{A \circ B} &= a_C^B = 0.5 \end{aligned} $$ **Step 3 — Verify additivity:** $$0.48 + 0.06 + 0.46 = 1.00 \quad \checkmark$$ **Step 4 — Compute expected probability:** $$E(\text{Alice trusts Carol}) = b_C^{A \circ B} + a_C^{A \circ B} \cdot u_C^{A \circ B} = 0.48 + 0.5 \times 0.46 = 0.48 + 0.23 = 0.71$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Insight:** Even though Alice strongly trusts Bob and Bob strongly trusts Carol, Alice's expected trust in Carol is only **0.71** — significantly less than either individual belief — because of the uncertainty propagated through the chain. This demonstrates the **uncertainty attenuation** property of subjective logic trust networks. ### 3.4 Numerical Worked Example: Cumulative Fusion > [!NOTE] > **Problem:** Two reviewers give opinions on a product: > - Reviewer 1: $\omega_1 = (0.7, 0.2, 0.1, 0.5)$ > - Reviewer 2: $\omega_2 = (0.5, 0.3, 0.2, 0.5)$ > > Compute the cumulative fusion. **Step 1 — Apply the formula:** $$ \begin{aligned} b_{12} &= \frac{(0.7)(0.2) + (0.5)(0.1)}{(0.1) + (0.2) - (0.1)(0.2)} = \frac{0.14 + 0.05}{0.3 - 0.02} = \frac{0.19}{0.28} \approx 0.679 \\ d_{12} &= \frac{(0.2)(0.2) + (0.3)(0.1)}{0.28} = \frac{0.04 + 0.03}{0.28} = \frac{0.07}{0.28} = 0.25 \\ u_{12} &= \frac{(0.1)(0.2)}{0.28} = \frac{0.02}{0.28} \approx 0.071 \end{aligned} $$ **Step 2 — Verify:** $$0.679 + 0.25 + 0.071 = 1.000 \quad \checkmark$$ **Step 3 — Interpretation:** Uncertainty dropped from 0.1 and 0.2 to 0.071 — combining independent observations **reduces uncertainty** as expected. --- ## 4.
